[PATCH v6 2/4] mm: huge_memory: refactor anon_enabled_store() with change_anon_orders()
[PATCH v6 3/4] mm: huge_memory: refactor enabled_store() with change_enabled()
[PATCH v6 4/4] mm: ratelimit min_free_kbytes adjustment messages

review.summary: This series reduces unnecessary start_stop_khugepaged() calls by only invoking it when THP settings actually change, and rate-limits the resulting pr_info/pr_warn messages. The approach is sound but Patch 3 introduces a data race by replacing atomic bit operations with non-atomic ones in set_global_enabled_mode() without adding a protecting lock.

series.title: mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()
series.author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()

review.summary: The patch fixes an unsigned integer underflow in filemap_map_pages() where a concurrent truncation between next_uptodate_folio() and the file_end/end_pgoff recalculation can leave xas.xa_index > end_pgoff, causing nr_pages to wrap to a huge value and set_pte_range() to map far beyond the folio boundary. The fix moves the end_pgoff recalculation before next_uptodate_folio() so any returned folio is guaranteed to satisfy xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff.
review.review_inline:
From: <reviewer>
Subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in
         filemap_map_pages()

On [date], Baolin Wang wrote:

> diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
> --- a/mm/filemap.c
> +++ b/mm/filemap.c

[ ... ]

> +	/*
> +	 * Recalculate end_pgoff based on file_end before calling
> +	 * next_uptodate_folio() to avoid races with concurrent
> +	 * truncation.
> +	 */
> +	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
> +	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
> +
>  	rcu_read_lock();
>  	folio = next_uptodate_folio(&xas, mapping, end_pgoff);
>  	if (!folio)
>  		goto out;
>  
> -	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
> -	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);

The underflow is confirmed.  xas_next_entry() inside
next_uptodate_folio() limits its scan to end_pgoff, so a returned
folio's head index satisfies xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff only when
end_pgoff is already clamped to the current i_size.  In the old
ordering, end_pgoff was narrowed after the folio was in hand, leaving
the window where a concurrent truncation could shrink i_size enough
that xas.xa_index > end_pgoff, causing

    nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1

to wrap to a huge unsigned value, passing that into
filemap_map_folio_range() and ultimately triggering the
VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO in __folio_rmap_sanity_checks() at

    VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO(page_folio(page + nr_pages - 1) != folio, folio)

I also checked next_uptodate_folio(): it does re-read i_size_read()
while holding the folio lock and skips folios whose head index is >=
max_idx.  That guard prevents returning a folio whose head is already
past the new EOF, but it does not close the window above: the wrapping
happens in the caller after next_uptodate_folio() returns, using the
stale end_pgoff.  The fix correctly clamps end_pgoff at the call site
before the folio is fetched.

The PMD eligibility check (file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)) and
the file_end argument forwarded to filemap_map_folio_range() both
consume the same file_end variable, which is now computed earlier; the
semantics of both paths are unchanged.

Looks good to me.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>

series.title: liveupdate: Fix module unloading and unregister API
series.author: Pasha Tatashin <pasha.tatashin@soleen.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/6] liveupdate: Fix module unloading and unregister API
[PATCH 1/6] liveupdate: Protect file handler list with rwsem
[PATCH 2/6] liveupdate: Protect FLB lists with rwsem
[PATCH 3/6] liveupdate: Defer file handler module refcounting to active sessions
[PATCH 4/6] liveupdate: Defer FLB module refcounting to active sessions
[PATCH 5/6] liveupdate: Make unregister functions return void
[PATCH 6/6] liveupdate: Remove luo_session_quiesce()

review.summary: This series introduces rwsem-based list protection and defers module reference counting from registration time to active live-update sessions, allowing modules to be gracefully unloaded when not in use. Two bugs are present: a module ref imbalance in the file handler path that defeats the series' stated goal, and a compilation error in the disabled-liveupdate stub.
review.review_inline:
On Sun, Mar 17, 2026 at 12:00:00AM +0000, Pasha Tatashin wrote:
> liveupdate: Fix module unloading and unregister API

Reviewed the rwsem introduction (patches 1-2), the deferred
refcounting logic (patches 3-4), and the void-return / quiesce
removal (patches 5-6).  Two issues found.

--- [PATCH 3/6] liveupdate: Defer file handler module refcounting ---

> diff --git a/kernel/liveupdate/luo_file.c b/kernel/liveupdate/luo_file.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -922,7 +934,6 @@ int liveupdate_unregister_file_handler(...)
>  		list_del(&ACCESS_PRIVATE(fh, list));
>  	}
> -	module_put(fh->ops->owner);
>  	luo_session_resume();

This removes module_put from unregister, but
liveupdate_register_file_handler still calls
try_module_get(fh->ops->owner) inside the rwsem_write scope, and
that call survives unchanged through all subsequent patches,
including patch 6.  There is no corresponding module_put anywhere
for that registration-time reference, so the module remains
permanently pinned after register -- exactly the problem this
series is meant to fix.

For the FLB side, patch 4 correctly removes try_module_get from
liveupdate_register_flb and defers it to luo_flb_file_preserve_one.
The file handler path needs the same treatment: remove
try_module_get from liveupdate_register_file_handler and keep it
only in luo_preserve_file and luo_file_deserialize, where it
already lands after patch 3.

--- [PATCH 5/6] liveupdate: Make unregister functions return void ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/liveupdate.h b/include/linux/liveupdate.h

[ ... ]

> -static inline int liveupdate_unregister_flb(
> -				struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
> -				struct liveupdate_flb *flb)
> +static inline void liveupdate_unregister_flb(
> +				struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
> +				struct liveupdate_flb *flb)
>  {
>  	return -EOPNOTSUPP;
>  }

The return type is changed to void but the body still contains
"return -EOPNOTSUPP;".  This is a compilation error when
CONFIG_LIVEUPDATE is not set.  The liveupdate_unregister_file_handler
stub correctly removes its return statement; the
liveupdate_unregister_flb stub needs the same fix.

series.title: mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()
series.author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH] mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()

review.summary: The patch fixes an unsigned integer underflow in filemap_map_pages() where a concurrent file truncation could shrink end_pgoff below xas.xa_index after a folio was already retrieved, causing nr_pages to wrap to a huge value and filemap_map_folio_range() to map far beyond the folio boundary. The fix moves the i_size read and end_pgoff clamping before next_uptodate_folio(), guaranteeing xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff at the point of the nr_pages calculation.
review.review_inline:
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()

The race and fix are correctly identified. Here is what I verified.

> diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
> --- a/mm/filemap.c
> +++ b/mm/filemap.c
> @@ -3879,14 +3879,14 @@ vm_fault_t filemap_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
>  	unsigned int nr_pages = 0, folio_type;
>  	unsigned short mmap_miss = 0, mmap_miss_saved;
>  
> +	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
> +	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
> +
>  	rcu_read_lock();
>  	folio = next_uptodate_folio(&xas, mapping, end_pgoff);
>  	if (!folio)
>  		goto out;
>  
> -	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
> -	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);

The underflow is here in the loop body:

    nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1;

All three operands are unsigned (pgoff_t / unsigned long), so when
xas.xa_index > end_pgoff the subtraction wraps to a huge value.
filemap_map_folio_range() then iterates that many pages, walking far
beyond the folio and corrupting _mapcount on unrelated pages - matching
the reported "Bad page state" and the VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO in
folio_add_file_rmap_ptes() that fires when page + nr_pages - 1 falls
outside the folio.

The fix establishes the invariant xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff before the
loop. next_uptodate_folio() advances xas via xas_next_entry(xas,
end_pgoff), which caps the returned xa_index at end_pgoff, so a
successful return always satisfies xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff. By
clamping end_pgoff first, that cap is tight enough to prevent the
underflow.

I also read next_uptodate_folio() and confirmed it has its own internal
i_size re-check (max_idx guard at the xas_next_entry loop): it rejects
any folio whose xa_index >= DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(), PAGE_SIZE). This
is an additional safety net but does not close the original window on its
own, since it and the caller's i_size_read() are separate reads that can
straddle the truncation. The patch eliminates the problematic second
i_size_read() in the caller entirely.

The loop's continuation call

    } while ((folio = next_uptodate_folio(&xas, mapping, end_pgoff))
                                                          ^^^^^^^^

also uses the now-fixed end_pgoff, so all subsequent iterations are
equally protected.

Moving i_size_read() outside rcu_read_lock() is correct: the rcu section
protects the xarray walk, not the inode size, and i_size_read() is safe
on its own (seqlock on 32-bit, atomic on 64-bit).

The file_end value is still consumed correctly by the PMD condition and
as an argument to filemap_map_folio_range(). Both uses tolerate the same
degree of staleness they always could, since file_end was never inside
a critical section with respect to i_size.

Verified: unsigned arithmetic underflow path, xas cap semantics,
next_uptodate_folio internal max_idx guard, loop continuation, file_end
secondary uses. No regressions found.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>

series.title: mm/vmalloc: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink
series.author: Shivam Kalra via B4 Relay <devnull+shivamkalra98.zohomail.in@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v5 0/3] mm/vmalloc: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink
[PATCH v5 1/3] mm/vmalloc: extract vm_area_free_pages() helper from vfree()
[PATCH v5 2/3] mm/vmalloc: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink
[PATCH v5 3/3] lib/test_vmalloc: add vrealloc test case

review.summary: This series implements partial page freeing in vrealloc() when shrinking across a page boundary, extracting a vm_area_free_pages() helper, adding the shrink logic with correct exclusions for huge-page and VM_FLUSH_RESET_PERMS allocations, and a lib/test_vmalloc test case.
review.review_inline:
Shivam Kalra <shivamkalra98@zohomail.in>

[PATCH v5 0/3] mm/vmalloc: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink

> diff --git a/mm/vmalloc.c b/mm/vmalloc.c

[Patch 1/3: extract vm_area_free_pages()]

> +static void vm_area_free_pages(struct vm_struct *vm, unsigned int start,
> +			       unsigned int end)
> +{
> +	unsigned int i;
> +
> +	for (i = start; i < end; i++) {
> +		struct page *page = vm->pages[i];
> +
> +		BUG_ON(!page);
> +		if (!(vm->flags & VM_MAP_PUT_PAGES))
> +			mod_lruvec_page_state(page, NR_VMALLOC, -1);
> +		__free_page(page);
> +		vm->pages[i] = NULL;
> +		cond_resched();
> +	}
> +}

Refactoring verified against the original loop in vfree(). The NULLing
of vm->pages[i] is safe: vfree() (after this patch) calls
vm_area_free_pages(vm, 0, vm->nr_pages), and vm->nr_pages is reduced to
new_nr_pages by patch 2 before vfree() sees the allocation. The NULL
slots at [new_nr_pages, original_nr_pages) are never visited.

[ ... ]

[Patch 2/3: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink]

> +	/* Free tail pages when shrink crosses a page boundary. */
> +	if (new_nr_pages < vm->nr_pages && !vm_area_page_order(vm) &&
> +	    !(vm->flags & VM_FLUSH_RESET_PERMS)) {
> +		unsigned long addr = (unsigned long)p;
> +
> +		vunmap_range(addr + (new_nr_pages << PAGE_SHIFT),
> +			     addr + (vm->nr_pages << PAGE_SHIFT));
> +
> +		vm_area_free_pages(vm, new_nr_pages, vm->nr_pages);
> +		vm->nr_pages = new_nr_pages;
> +	}

Operation ordering verified:
  1. memset [size, old_size) runs while pages are still mapped.
  2. vunmap_range() clears PTEs and flushes TLBs before __free_page().
  3. vm->nr_pages is reduced only after freeing, so BUG_ON(!page) in
     vm_area_free_pages() is safe for the range being freed.

The subsequent vfree() path is safe. remove_vm_area() calls
free_unmap_vmap_area(), which invokes vunmap_range_noflush() over the
full original VA range. Since the tail PTEs are already cleared, the
second vunmap is a no-op. vm_reset_perms() is excluded by the
VM_FLUSH_RESET_PERMS guard above, so set_area_direct_map() only sees
the reduced vm->nr_pages. No double-free, no stale page pointers.

KASAN: __kasan_vrealloc() poisons shadow for [size, old_size) after
pages are unmapped. The KASAN shadow region is not backed by the vmalloc
pages themselves, so writes to shadow for the freed VA range are safe.

> -	if (size <= alloced_size) {
> +	if (size <= (size_t)vm->nr_pages << PAGE_SHIFT) {

Correct fix. get_vm_area_size() reads vm->size, which reflects the
virtual reservation and does not change on shrink. After pages are freed,
vm->nr_pages reflects actual physical backing, so a grow-in-place beyond
vm->nr_pages * PAGE_SIZE would access freed pages without this fix.

The page_order > 0 exclusion is correct: compound huge pages cannot be
partially freed at order-0. The VM_FLUSH_RESET_PERMS exclusion is
correct: vm_reset_perms() in vfree() must see all pages with direct-map
permissions intact, requiring them to remain until final vfree().

[ ... ]

[Patch 3/3: lib/test_vmalloc: add vrealloc test case]

The four-step sequence (grow-realloc, shrink-across-boundary,
shrink-within-page, grow-in-place) exercises all code paths introduced
in patch 2. Data integrity is checked at each step. The bit 12 (id
4096) mask assignment is consistent with the existing scheme.

Overall the series looks correct.

series.title: mm/damon: support MADV_COLLAPSE via DAMOS_COLLAPSE scheme action
series.author: <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH v2 1/1] mm/damon: support MADV_COLLAPSE via DAMOS_COLLAPSE scheme action

review.summary: Adds DAMOS_COLLAPSE, a new DAMOS scheme action that calls madvise_collapse() synchronously to collapse a virtual address region into a THP, independent of khugepaged. The implementation routes through the existing damos_madvise() helper with MADV_COLLAPSE.
review.review_inline:
Author: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

mm/damon: support MADV_COLLAPSE via DAMOS_COLLAPSE scheme action

Looks good to me.  Checked the following:

> +	case DAMOS_COLLAPSE:
> +		madv_action = MADV_COLLAPSE;
> +		break;

damos_madvise() calls do_madvise() which, for MADV_COLLAPSE, takes
mmap_read_lock internally (get_lock_mode() in madvise.c returns
MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK for MADV_COLLAPSE).  No lock is held by the
caller before entering damos_madvise(), so there is no deadlock or
lock ordering concern.

When CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is disabled, madvise_behavior_valid()
does not include MADV_COLLAPSE (it lives under #ifdef
CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE alongside MADV_HUGEPAGE/MADV_NOHUGEPAGE),
so do_madvise() returns -EINVAL and damos_madvise() returns 0 bytes
applied.  This is identical to how DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE
behave under the same config; the documentation note about silent
failure is accurate.

The fvaddr operations set shares damon_va_apply_scheme() directly, so
it inherits the new case without any additional change needed.  The
paddr operations set has a default no-op case; DAMOS_COLLAPSE correctly
falls through to it, matching the doc restricting support to
vaddr/fvaddr.

Sysfs action_show/store iterate damos_sysfs_action_names[] by name
string and enum value comparison (not by array index), so inserting
DAMOS_COLLAPSE between DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE and DAMOS_LRU_PRIO, which
shifts subsequent enum values, does not break the sysfs interface.
NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS is defined but not used as an array bound anywhere
in-tree.

series.title: selftests/mm: fix failures and robustness improvements
series.author: Sayali Patil <sayalip@linux.ibm.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 00/13] selftests/mm: fix failures and robustness improvements
[PATCH v2 01/13] selftests/mm: restore default nr_hugepages value during cleanup in charge_reserved_hugetlb.sh
[PATCH v2 02/13] selftests/mm: fix hugetlb pathname construction in charge_reserved_hugetlb.sh
[PATCH v2 03/13] selftests/mm: fix hugetlb pathname construction in hugetlb_reparenting_test.sh
[PATCH v2 04/13] selftest/mm: fix cgroup task placement and tolerance in hugetlb_reparenting_test.sh
[PATCH v2 05/13] selftests/mm: size tmpfs according to PMD page size in split_huge_page_test
[PATCH v2 06/13] selftest/mm: adjust hugepage-mremap test size for large huge pages
[PATCH v2 07/13] selftest/mm: register existing mapping with userfaultfd in hugepage-mremap
[PATCH v2 08/13] selftests/mm: ensure destination is hugetlb-backed in hugepage-mremap
[PATCH v2 09/13] selftests/mm: skip uffd-wp-mremap if UFFD write-protect is unsupported
[PATCH v2 10/13] selftests/mm: skip uffd-stress test when nr_pages_per_cpu is zero
[PATCH v2 11/13] selftests/mm: fix double increment in linked list cleanup in compaction_test
[PATCH v2 12/13] selftests/mm: move hwpoison setup into run_test() and silence modprobe output for memory-failure category
[PATCH v2 13/13] selftests/cgroup: extend test_hugetlb_memcg.c to support all huge page sizes

review.summary: This 13-patch series fixes selftests/mm failures on powerpc with 64K base pages by correcting hugetlb cgroup path construction for 1GB hugepages, fixing cgroup task placement races, sizing tmpfs dynamically for split_huge_page_test, adjusting hugepage-mremap size logic, making the userfaultfd registration apply to the correct hugetlb region, and ensuring the mremap destination is hugetlb-backed to avoid hash MMU slice conflicts.
review.review_inline:
Author: Sayali Patil <sayalip@linux.ibm.com>

selftests/mm: fix failures and robustness improvements (v2)

Overall the series looks correct. I read all ten modified files against
the diffs and traced through the key control paths. Notes below.

> --- [PATCH v2 01/13] selftests/mm: restore default nr_hugepages ---
>
> -  echo 0 >/proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages
> +  echo "$nr_hugepgs" > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages

nr_hugepgs is captured at script start before any test runs, so it
survives even when cleanup() is called from an early-exit path. Correct.

[ ... ]

> --- [PATCH v2 02/13] selftests/mm: fix hugetlb pathname construction ---
>
> +if (( MB >= 1024 )); then
> +        UNIT="GB"
> +        MB_DISPLAY=$((MB / 1024))
> +else
> +        UNIT="MB"
> +        MB_DISPLAY=$MB
> +fi

get_machine_hugepage_size() returns kB/1024, so for a 1GB page MB=1024,
MB_DISPLAY=1, UNIT=GB -> "hugetlb.1GB.current". For 512MB (if it ever
appears) MB=512, MB_DISPLAY=512, UNIT=MB -> "hugetlb.512MB.current",
which also matches the kernel naming. Correct.

One nit: run_test() in the same file still mounts hugetlbfs with
"-o pagesize=${MB}M", which for 1GB hugepages becomes "pagesize=1024M".
The kernel's memparse() accepts 1024M == 1G, so this is harmless, but
it could be made consistent with MB_DISPLAY/UNIT if desired.

[ ... ]

> --- [PATCH v2 04/13] selftest/mm: fix cgroup task placement ---
>
> +  ./write_to_hugetlbfs -p "$path" -s "$size" -m 0 -o -d & pid=$!
> +  echo "$pid" > "$cg_file"
> +  wait "$pid"

The -d flag fires sleep(1) inside the getopt switch, after -p/-s/-m/-o
are already parsed and before execution reaches the mmap call. The
parent's echo to cg_file is effectively instant, so the 1-second window
is more than sufficient in practice.

With set -e active at all call sites, a non-zero exit from
wait "$pid" (e.g. allocation failure) correctly aborts the test.

> +	case 'd':
> +		sleep(1);
> +		break;

A caller passing -d twice would sleep twice. Not a bug for this
controlled usage, but worth documenting if write_to_hugetlbfs is
used more broadly.

[ ... ]

> --- [PATCH v2 05/13] selftests/mm: size tmpfs according to PMD ---
>
> +	unsigned long size = 2 * pmd_pagesize;
> +	char opts[64];
>  ...
> +	snprintf(opts, sizeof(opts), "huge=always,size=%lu", size);

pmd_pagesize is uint64_t; size is unsigned long. On 64-bit hosts these
are the same width. The longest plausible string is
"huge=always,size=2147483648" (2 * 1GB PMD) at 27 bytes, well within
the 64-byte opts buffer. Correct.

[ ... ]

> --- [PATCH v2 06/13] selftest/mm: adjust hugepage-mremap test size ---
>
> +if [ "$hpgsize_KB" -gt 10240 ]; then
> +	len_mb=$(( (2 * hpgsize_KB) / 1024 ))
> +	CATEGORY="hugetlb" run_test ./hugepage-mremap "${len_mb}"
> +else
> +	CATEGORY="hugetlb" run_test ./hugepage-mremap
> +fi

For 1GB pages hpgsize_KB=1048576, len_mb=2048. hugepage-mremap uses
atoi(argv[1]) so this lands as 2048 MB. The comment in hugepage-mremap.c
says >=1GB is needed to trigger PMD sharing; 2GB satisfies that.
Correct.

[ ... ]

> --- [PATCH v2 07/13] selftest/mm: register existing mapping with uffd ---
>
> -	addr = mmap(NULL, len, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,
> -		    MAP_PRIVATE | MAP_ANONYMOUS, -1, 0);
> -	if (addr == MAP_FAILED)
> -		ksft_exit_fail_msg("mmap: %s\n", strerror(errno));
>  	if (uffd_register(uffd, addr, len, true, false, false))
>  		ksft_exit_fail_msg("ioctl-UFFDIO_REGISTER: %s\n", strerror(errno));

The removed mmap was registering an unrelated anonymous mapping instead
of the hugetlb region passed by the caller. Fixing this so uffd tracks
the actual test region is the right thing to do.

The uffd fd opened in this function is never closed; this was also true
before the patch and is a pre-existing leak. O_CLOEXEC limits the damage
to the test's own lifetime, so not a regression.

> --- [PATCH v2 08/13] selftests/mm: ensure destination is hugetlb-backed ---
>
> -#define FLAGS (MAP_SHARED | MAP_ANONYMOUS)
> +#define FLAGS (MAP_HUGETLB | MAP_SHARED | MAP_POPULATE)
>  ...
> -	void *vaddr =
> -		mmap((void *)suggested_addr, length, PROTECTION, FLAGS, -1, 0);
> +	void *vaddr = mmap((void *)suggested_addr, length, PROTECTION, FLAGS, fd, 0);

All three mappings (haddr, daddr, vaddr) now use MAP_SHARED on the same
fd, so they share the same physical hugepages. Total hugepage usage
remains ~length (not 3x), since MAP_SHARED of the same file offset
reuses the same pages. MAP_POPULATE on vaddr pre-faults already-mapped
physical pages, so no additional hugepages are consumed beyond what
haddr and daddr already faulted in.

One subtle point: haddr is registered with uffd using
UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_MISSING, then mremap'd to vaddr. Because
MAP_POPULATE already faulted all pages in before registration, no
MISSING faults are generated during the subsequent write_bytes() call.
The absence of a uffd fault-handler thread therefore does not stall the
test. Verified this path.

[ ... ]

I also verified: the nr_pages_per_cpu == 0 skip path in uffd-stress
returns KSFT_SKIP (4) which run_vmtests.sh counts as [SKIP] correctly;
the uffd_wp_feature_supported() guard in uffd-wp-mremap exits with
ksft_exit_skip when UFFD_FEATURE_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_WP is absent; the
hwpoison setup inside run_test() correctly silences modprobe output and
sets skip=1 if the module is not available; and the MEMCG_CHARGE_BATCH
batching tolerance in test_hugetlb_memcg is well-motivated for 64K
base pages where nr_pages per hugepage can be below the batch threshold.

No regressions found across all ten modified files.

series.title: mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v3] mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

review.summary: The patch fixes DAMON_STAT to monitor all System RAM resources instead of only the largest one, by replacing damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default() with a new walk that captures the bounding box [first_start, last_end] across all System RAM regions.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

Verified the walk logic, address conversion, error paths, and how
non-RAM gaps in the bounding box are handled by paddr ops.

> +static int damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn(struct resource *res, void *arg)
> +{
> +	struct damon_stat_system_ram_range_walk_arg *a = arg;
> +
> +	if (!a->walked) {
> +		a->walked = true;
> +		a->res.start = res->start;
> +	}
> +	a->res.end = res->end;
> +	return 0;
> +}

walk_system_ram_res() iterates in ascending address order (via
walk_res_desc which advances start = res.end + 1 each step).  The
callback correctly records the start of the first region and keeps
overwriting res.end so the last region's end is retained, producing a
correct bounding box.  Returning 0 unconditionally ensures all regions
are visited.

> +	walk_system_ram_res(0, -1, &arg, damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn);
> +	if (!arg.walked)
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +	addr_range.start = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.start, addr_unit);
> +	addr_range.end = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.end + 1, addr_unit);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

damon_stat_res_to_core_addr() is an exact copy of the static
damon_res_to_core_addr() in core.c, including the div_u64() path for
the 64-bit resource_size_t / 32-bit unsigned long combination.  The
+1 on arg.res.end correctly converts the inclusive end to an exclusive
end for DAMON's addr_range convention, matching what
damon_find_biggest_system_ram() did for the single-region case.

ctx->addr_unit is initialised to 1 by damon_new_ctx() and is not
changed by damon_select_ops(), so there is no division-by-zero hazard.

The -EINVAL return propagates to damon_stat_build_ctx()'s goto
free_out, which calls damon_destroy_ctx() -- the same cleanup path
triggered by the old function returning an error.

For non-RAM gaps between System RAM regions that now fall inside the
bounding box: damon_pa_prepare_access_checks() samples a random
address in each region and calls damon_pa_mkold(), which calls
damon_get_folio(); if there is no folio at that physical address the
function returns early without touching anything.  Similarly
damon_pa_young() returns false for addresses with no backing folio.
So gaps are treated as not-accessed, which is harmless.

Looks good.

series.title: fixup: mm/memory: inline unmap_page_range() into __zap_vma_range()
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/damon/stat: return error if monitoring target region is invalid
[PATCH] mm/kasan: adjust documentation
[PATCH] mm/damon/stat: fix wrong argument to damon_set_regions()
[PATCH] mm/damon/core: fix wrong damon_set_regions() argument
[PATCH] tmp
[PATCH] fixup: mm/memory: inline unmap_page_range() into __zap_vma_range()
[PATCH 1/1] userfaultfd: allow registration of WP_ASYNC for any VMA

review.summary: A 7-patch series containing critical damon_set_regions() argument fixes (addr_unit passed as nr_ranges instead of 1), a 32-bit LPAE address overflow guard in damon/stat, a khugepaged threshold-checking refactoring that centralizes MADV_COLLAPSE vs khugepaged thresholds, a trivial variable rename in mm/memory.c, and a userfaultfd behavior restoration for WP_ASYNC on VMAs without vm_uffd_ops (e.g., SysV shared memory).
review.review_inline:
From: reviewer
To: linux-mm@kvack.org

Series: fixup: mm/memory: inline unmap_page_range() into __zap_vma_range()

Reviewed each patch against the actual source in mm/damon/stat.c,
mm/damon/core.c, mm/khugepaged.c, mm/memory.c, and mm/userfaultfd.c.

---

[PATCH] mm/damon/stat: fix wrong argument to damon_set_regions()
[PATCH] mm/damon/core: fix wrong damon_set_regions() argument

> -	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

Clear critical fix in both sites.  Passing addr_unit (e.g. PAGE_SIZE =
4096) as the nr_ranges argument would cause damon_set_regions() to
iterate 4095 ranges past the end of the single-element stack variable
addr_range.  Verified the callers propagate the error return correctly:
damon_stat_build_ctx() uses "goto free_out" -> damon_destroy_ctx(),
which frees the target already added to the context at that point.

---

[PATCH] mm/damon/stat: return error if monitoring target region is invalid

> +	if (addr_range.end <= addr_range.start)
> +		return -EINVAL;
>  	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

On 32-bit LPAE, resource_size_t is u64 but unsigned long is 32-bit.
damon_stat_res_to_core_addr() uses div_u64() precisely to handle this;
if the result of dividing (res.end + 1) by addr_unit exceeds ULONG_MAX
the truncation yields 0, making addr_range.end = 0 < addr_range.start.
The check catches that.  The -EINVAL propagates up through
damon_stat_build_ctx() via the existing "goto free_out" path, which is
symmetric with the success path.

---

[PATCH] tmp (khugepaged refactoring)

> +static int collapse_max_ptes_none(struct collapse_control *cc,
> +		struct vm_area_struct *vma)
> +{
> +	if (vma && userfaultfd_armed(vma))
> +		return 0;
> +	if (!cc->is_khugepaged)
> +		return HPAGE_PMD_NR;
> +	return khugepaged_max_ptes_none;
> +}

Verified all three helpers produce semantically equivalent results
for every branch.

__collapse_huge_page_isolate, none_or_zero path:
The old condition
  !userfaultfd_armed(vma) && (!cc->is_khugepaged || count <= limit)
maps exactly to the new count > max_ptes_none check:
- uffd armed:          max=0,           first increment triggers failure
- MADV_COLLAPSE:       max=HPAGE_PMD_NR, loop cap (HPAGE_PMD_NR iters)
                       means count > HPAGE_PMD_NR is never true
- khugepaged, no uffd: max=khugepaged_max_ptes_none, same threshold

The shared-PTE path follows the same logic with collapse_max_ptes_shared.

hpage_collapse_scan_pmd, swap-PTE path:
The restructuring checks the count limit first, then pte_swp_uffd_wp_any().
In the old code pte_swp_uffd_wp_any() was only evaluated when the limit
was not exceeded, which is identical to the new ordering.

hpage_collapse_scan_file (collapse_scan_file in current tree):
max_ptes_none is computed with vma=NULL, correctly skipping the
userfaultfd_armed() check that is only meaningful for anon VMAs.
For the end-of-scan "present < HPAGE_PMD_NR - max_ptes_none" check:
when !cc->is_khugepaged, max_ptes_none = HPAGE_PMD_NR, the threshold
collapses to 0 and present (non-negative) never falls below it,
matching the old "cc->is_khugepaged &&" guard.

One observation worth documenting: the file-scan swap counter uses
  swap += 1 << xas_get_order(&xas);
rather than a per-page increment.  The old code guarded with
!cc->is_khugepaged (unconditional pass for MADV_COLLAPSE); the new code
uses max_ptes_swap = HPAGE_PMD_NR for MADV_COLLAPSE.  A boundary-
spanning large swap folio whose xas order inflates the counter above
HPAGE_PMD_NR would now fail for MADV_COLLAPSE where it previously
succeeded.  In practice the scan window is exactly HPAGE_PMD_NR pages
so the total swap pages within the window cannot exceed HPAGE_PMD_NR,
making this impossible today.  A brief comment noting that
HPAGE_PMD_NR is chosen as "effectively unlimited" would help future
readers.

Also, the patch subject "tmp" should be replaced before merging.

---

[PATCH] fixup: mm/memory: inline unmap_page_range() into __zap_vma_range()

> -		unsigned long next, cur = start;
> +		unsigned long next, addr = start;
>  		...
> -		} while (pgd++, cur = next, cur != end);
> +		} while (pgd++, addr = next, addr != end);

Pure cosmetic rename; loop invariant and termination condition are
unchanged.

---

[PATCH 1/1] userfaultfd: allow registration of WP_ASYNC for any VMA

> -	/* only VMAs that implement vm_uffd_ops are supported */
> -	if (!ops)
> -		return false;
> -
> -	if (vma->vm_flags & VM_DROPPABLE)
> -		return false;
> -
>  	if (wp_async && (vm_flags == VM_UFFD_WP))
>  		return true;
>
> +	/* For any other mode reject VMAs that don't implement vm_uffd_ops */
> +	if (!ops)
> +		return false;
> +
> +	if (vma->vm_flags & VM_DROPPABLE)
> +		return false;

Verified via vma_uffd_ops(): anonymous VMAs always return &anon_uffd_ops
(non-NULL); shmem/SysV-shared VMAs backed by vm_ops that do not set
uffd_ops return NULL, so the old !ops check blocked them.  Moving the
WP_ASYNC+WP-only early return before that check restores the ability to
register those VMAs with WP_ASYNC, which is the stated regression fix.

The VM_DROPPABLE check is also bypassed for the WP_ASYNC+WP-only path.
WP_ASYNC write faults are resolved entirely by the generic fault handler
without any VMA-specific uffd dispatch, so skipping VM_DROPPABLE for
this path appears safe.  SysV shared memory (the primary target) does
not use VM_DROPPABLE today; confirming whether a hypothetical future
DROPPABLE+WP_ASYNC combination needs additional care in the fault path
would be a useful note in the commit message.

Overall the series is correct.  The two nits above (swap-counter comment
and "tmp" subject) are the only non-blocking observations.

series.title: mm/mempolicy: track user-defined mempolicy allocations
series.author: "JP Kobryn (Meta)" <jp.kobryn@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH v3] mm/mempolicy: track user-defined mempolicy allocations

review.summary: Adds per-node NUMA_MPOL_HIT/MISS/FOREIGN counters to track allocations driven by user-defined mempolicies, distinguishing intentional from fallback placements. The accounting function uses current->mempolicy to filter out non-user-policy allocations, but the actual allocation policy passed as pol can differ from current->mempolicy, causing both missed counts and phantom counts.
review.review_inline:
mm/mempolicy: track user-defined mempolicy allocations

The overall goal is clear and the placement of instrumentation inside
alloc_pages_mpol() is sensible.  One correctness issue below.

> +static void mpol_count_numa_alloc(struct mempolicy *pol, int intended_nid,
> +				  struct page *page, unsigned int order)
> +{
> +	int actual_nid = page_to_nid(page);
> +	long nr_pages = 1L << order;
> +	bool is_hit;
> +
> +	if (!current->mempolicy)
> +		return;

This guard tests the wrong object.  The function receives pol, which is
the policy that actually governed the allocation.  current->mempolicy is
the task's top-level policy, and the two can diverge in at least two
important ways:

(1) VMA-level mbind() policy.  vma_alloc_folio_noprof() calls
    get_vma_policy(), which returns the VMA's explicit mbind() policy
    when one is set.  If the allocating task has no task-level policy
    (current->mempolicy == NULL), the guard fires and returns early,
    silently dropping all VMA-bound allocations.  mbind() is just as
    much a user designation as set_mempolicy(), so these should be
    counted.

(2) Interrupt-context allocations.  alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() passes
    &default_policy (mode MPOL_LOCAL) when in_interrupt() is true, so
    pol == &default_policy.  But current is the interrupted task, which
    may have a non-NULL mempolicy.  The guard does not fire and the
    interrupt-context allocation gets attributed to the interrupted
    task's policy -- a phantom count with no connection to any user
    decision.

Both bugs share the same root: the filter must operate on pol itself,
not on current->mempolicy.  The existing mpol_put_task_policy() shows
the right idiom for identifying kernel-internal policies:

  if (pol == &default_policy ||
      (pol >= &preferred_node_policy[0] &&
       pol <= &preferred_node_policy[ARRAY_SIZE(preferred_node_policy)-1]))
          return;

Using that pattern (or a small inline helper wrapping it) instead of the
current->mempolicy test would correctly exclude &default_policy and the
per-node preferred_node_policy[] entries while still counting both
task-level and VMA-level user policies.

> +	if (pol->mode == MPOL_BIND || pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY)
> +		is_hit = node_isset(actual_nid, pol->nodes);
> +	else
> +		is_hit = (actual_nid == intended_nid);
> +
> +	if (is_hit) {
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_HIT, nr_pages);
> +	} else {
> +		/* account for miss on the fallback node */
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_MISS, nr_pages);
> +
> +		/* account for foreign on the intended node */
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(intended_nid), NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN, nr_pages);
> +	}

For MPOL_BIND without a home_node, intended_nid is the unmodified input
nid (typically numa_node_id()), which need not be inside pol->nodes.
When the bind nodemask is fully satisfied is_hit is true so intended_nid
is never consumed -- fine.  But when the allocator falls back outside the
bind nodemask, NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN lands on an effectively arbitrary node
(the running CPU's home node).  Is that the intended semantics?  A short
comment on what intended_nid represents for BIND would help readers.

The goto-out refactoring looks correct: alloc_pages_preferred_many(),
the THP THISNODE fast path (both the success and the !__GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM
NULL returns), and the general slow path all funnel through the single
out: label, and the if (page) guard ensures NULL-allocation paths are
not counted.

The AFS refcount fix is correct, but there is a concrete regression in io_remap_pfn_range_prepare/complete that double-applies the io_remap_pfn_range_pfn() transformation on MIPS Alchemy and SPARC.

series.title: zswap pool per-CPU acomp_ctx simplifications
series.author: "Kanchana P. Sridhar" <kanchanapsridhar2026@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/2] zswap pool per-CPU acomp_ctx simplifications
[PATCH v2 1/2] mm: zswap: Remove redundant checks in zswap_cpu_comp_dead().
[PATCH v2 2/2] mm: zswap: Tie per-CPU acomp_ctx lifetime to the pool.

review.summary: Patch 1 removes redundant IS_ERR_OR_NULL() checks in zswap_cpu_comp_dead() and adds comments on crypto API return value semantics. Patch 2 ties per-CPU acomp_ctx lifetime to the pool (no teardown on CPU offline) and refactors cleanup into acomp_ctx_free() with post-free NULL assignments.
review.review_inline:
From: <reviewer>
Subject: Re: [PATCH v2 0/2] zswap pool per-CPU acomp_ctx simplifications

[ patch 1 ]

> -	if (IS_ERR_OR_NULL(acomp_ctx))
> +	if (!acomp_ctx)
>  		return 0;

Verified: acomp_ctx comes from per_cpu_ptr(pool->acomp_ctx, cpu),
which can never be NULL or an ERR pointer since pool->acomp_ctx was
successfully returned by alloc_percpu_gfp().  Changing to !acomp_ctx
is correct and removes the misleading IS_ERR branch.

> -	if (!IS_ERR_OR_NULL(req))
> +	if (req)
>  		acomp_request_free(req);
>  	if (!IS_ERR_OR_NULL(acomp))
>  		crypto_free_acomp(acomp);

The asymmetry here is correct: acomp_request_alloc() returns NULL on
failure (never an ERR pointer), so plain !req is sufficient.  The
IS_ERR_OR_NULL check on acomp must stay because crypto_alloc_acomp_node()
returns an ERR pointer (never NULL) on failure; if zswap_cpu_comp_prepare()
stored that ERR pointer into acomp_ctx->acomp before the teardown call
ran, dropping to just !acomp would wrongly attempt to free an ERR
pointer.  Leaving it unchanged is the right call.

[ patch 2 ]

> +static void acomp_ctx_free(struct crypto_acomp_ctx *acomp_ctx)
> +{
> +	if (!acomp_ctx)
> +		return;

This guard is dead code: every call site passes per_cpu_ptr() which
never yields NULL.  Harmless, but could be dropped.

> +	if (acomp_ctx->req)
> +		acomp_request_free(acomp_ctx->req);
> +	acomp_ctx->req = NULL;
> +
> +	if (!IS_ERR_OR_NULL(acomp_ctx->acomp))
> +		crypto_free_acomp(acomp_ctx->acomp);
> +	acomp_ctx->acomp = NULL;
> +
> +	kfree(acomp_ctx->buffer);
> +	acomp_ctx->buffer = NULL;

Checked all call sites and orderings:

1. From zswap_cpu_comp_prepare() fail path: mutex_init() runs only
   at the end of a successful prepare, so the mutex is never held
   here; safe to free without locking.  After return, all fields are
   NULL, so a subsequent online event correctly re-enters the
   allocation path via the acomp_ctx->acomp == NULL check.

2. From zswap_pool_destroy(): called only from __zswap_pool_release(),
   which runs after percpu_ref_is_zero() is confirmed (WARN_ON there
   verifies it).  The percpu_ref is held by every caller of
   zswap_compress()/zswap_decompress() for the pool lifetime
   (zswap_pool_current_get() in the store path; the entry->pool ref
   kept alive by the xarray entry in the load path).  By the time
   __zswap_pool_release() runs, no thread can be inside
   mutex_lock(&acomp_ctx->mutex), so unlocked teardown is safe.

The removal of the CPU-offline teardown callback is sound under the
same reasoning: resources persist until pool destruction, and pool
destruction is gated on the percpu_ref reaching zero, which cannot
happen while any compress/decompress operation is in flight.

Looks good to me.

[PATCH 61/61] file: Drop unlikely() around IS_ERR_OR_NULL()

review.summary: Treewide Coccinelle-generated series converting manual IS_ERR() + NULL check combinations to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(), and removing incorrect likely()/unlikely() wrappers around it. The individual substitutions shown are all semantically equivalent, but the Coccinelle script has a documentation typo and patches 55-58 carry a disclosed semantic change worth explicit ack from affected maintainers.
review.review_inline:
From: Philipp Hahn <phahn-oss@avm.de>
Subject: [PATCH 00/61] treewide: Use IS_ERR_OR_NULL over manual NULL check

> +// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-only
> +///
> +/// Use IF_ERR_OR_NULL() instead of IS_ERR() plus a check for (not) NULL

Typo in the Coccinelle documentation comment: "IF_ERR_OR_NULL" should
be "IS_ERR_OR_NULL".

[ ... ]

> +@p2 depends on patch@
> +expression E;
> +@@
> +(
> +-	E == NULL || WARN_ON(IS_ERR(E))
> ++	WARN_ON(IS_ERR_OR_NULL(E))
> +|
> +-	E == NULL || WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ERR(E))
> ++	WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ERR_OR_NULL(E))
> +)

The cover letter calls out patches 55-58 as having a semantic change:
WARN_ON / WARN_ON_ONCE will now fire for NULL as well as for IS_ERR.
Before this change a NULL was silently tolerated; after it triggers a
warning. This needs an explicit ack from each affected subsystem
maintainer that NULL is also an unexpected/buggy condition at those
call sites, not a normal "not present" sentinel.

[ ... ]

diff --git a/fs/erofs/zdata.c b/fs/erofs/zdata.c

[ ... ]

> -		if (IS_ERR(page) || !page) {
> +		if (IS_ERR_OR_NULL(page)) {
>  			bvec->page = NULL;	/* clear the failure reason */
>  			err = page ? PTR_ERR(page) : -EIO;

Verified correct. IS_ERR_OR_NULL(page) is logically identical to
IS_ERR(page) || !page. Inside the block, the ternary "page ?
PTR_ERR(page) : -EIO" still works: IS_ERR pointers are non-zero
(truthy) so PTR_ERR is taken; NULL is zero (falsy) so -EIO is taken.
No functional change.

[ ... ]

diff --git a/fs/gfs2/glock.c b/fs/gfs2/glock.c

[ ... ]

> -		while ((gl = rhashtable_walk_next(&iter)) && !IS_ERR(gl)) {
> +		while (!IS_ERR_OR_NULL((gl = rhashtable_walk_next(&iter)))) {

The outer do-while retries when gl == ERR_PTR(-EAGAIN). I traced both
paths:
- rhashtable_walk_next returns ERR_PTR(-EAGAIN): the new condition is
  false (IS_ERR_OR_NULL true), gl is set to ERR_PTR(-EAGAIN), inner
  loop exits, outer do-while retries. Same as before.
- rhashtable_walk_next returns NULL (end of table): condition is false,
  gl = NULL, outer do-while sees NULL != ERR_PTR(-EAGAIN) and exits.
  Same as before.
The assignment-in-condition form preserves the gl value that the outer
loop depends on. Correct.

[ ... ]

diff --git a/fs/seq_file.c b/fs/seq_file.c

[ ... ]

> -		if (!p || IS_ERR(p))	// EOF or an error
> +		if (IS_ERR_OR_NULL(p))	// EOF or an error

Verified. PTR_ERR(NULL) = 0, so err = 0 correctly signals EOF when
p is NULL; the IS_ERR path still yields a negative errno. Both
occurrences in seq_read_iter are equivalent to the original.
m->op->stop(m, p) is called with p regardless (NULL or ERR), which
is the expected contract for ->stop().

[ ... ]

diff --git a/fs/btrfs/transaction.c b/fs/btrfs/transaction.c

[ ... ]

> -	if (unlikely(dir_item != NULL && !IS_ERR(dir_item))) {
> +	if (!IS_ERR_OR_NULL(dir_item)) {
>  		pending->error = -EEXIST;
>  		goto dir_item_existed;
>  	} else if (IS_ERR(dir_item)) {

The else-if clause still handles the error case correctly. When
dir_item is NULL: the new first condition is false, IS_ERR(NULL)
is also false (0 is not in the error range), so NULL falls through
identically to before. Verified in include/linux/err.h: IS_ERR_OR_NULL
uses unlikely(!ptr) internally, making the removed unlikely() wrapper
a no-op that checkpatch correctly flags.

Overall: all pattern substitutions in the patches shown are
semantically equivalent. The only substantive concern is the WARN_ON
semantic expansion in patches 55-58, which requires per-site ack that
NULL deserves the same warning as IS_ERR.

Note: patch 33/61 ("mm: Prefer IS_ERR_OR_NULL over manual NULL check")
was not included in the provided diff. From examining mm/, the only
IS_ERR + NULL combinations are in mm/kmemleak.c where they appear as
three-term conditions (kmemleak_enabled && ptr && !IS_ERR(ptr)) that
the author correctly excluded from the Coccinelle transform. If patch
33 touches other mm files, please send it separately for review.

series.title: RDMA: Enable operation with DMA debug enabled
series.author: Leon Romanovsky <leon@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 0/8] RDMA: Enable operation with DMA debug enabled
[PATCH v3 1/8] dma-debug: Allow multiple invocations of overlapping entries
[PATCH v3 2/8] dma-mapping: handle DMA_ATTR_CPU_CACHE_CLEAN in trace output
[PATCH v3 3/8] dma-mapping: Clarify valid conditions for CPU cache line overlap
[PATCH v3 4/8] dma-mapping: Introduce DMA require coherency attribute
[PATCH v3 5/8] dma-direct: prevent SWIOTLB path when DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT is set
[PATCH v3 6/8] iommu/dma: add support for DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT attribute
[PATCH v3 7/8] RDMA/umem: Tell DMA mapping that UMEM requires coherency
[PATCH v3 8/8] mm/hmm: Indicate that HMM requires DMA coherency

review.summary: This series introduces DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT to reject mappings on non-coherent or SWIOTLB systems, fixes a spurious WARN_ONCE for cache-clean overlapping mappings, renames DMA_ATTR_CPU_CACHE_CLEAN to DMA_ATTR_DEBUGGING_IGNORE_CACHELINES, and applies the new attribute to RDMA umem and HMM to prevent silent data corruption on incoherent systems.
review.review_inline:
On Mon, Mar 17 2026, Leon Romanovsky wrote:

Series: RDMA: Enable operation with DMA debug enabled

--- [PATCH v3 1/8] dma-debug: Allow multiple invocations of overlapping entries ---

> -static void active_cacheline_inc_overlap(phys_addr_t cln)
> +static void active_cacheline_inc_overlap(phys_addr_t cln, bool is_cache_clean)
>  {
>  	int overlap = active_cacheline_read_overlap(cln);
>  	overlap = active_cacheline_set_overlap(cln, ++overlap);
> -	WARN_ONCE(overlap > ACTIVE_CACHELINE_MAX_OVERLAP,
> +	WARN_ONCE(!is_cache_clean && overlap > ACTIVE_CACHELINE_MAX_OVERLAP,

Verified against the existing dma_debug_entry struct: is_cache_clean is
declared 'bool', so passing entry->is_cache_clean directly is correct.
The WARN suppression is consistent with add_dma_entry, which also gates
the overlap error on both sides carrying is_cache_clean.  Patch 1 and
the add_dma_entry check in patch 4 form a consistent pair.  Looks good.

--- [PATCH v3 3/8] dma-mapping: Clarify valid conditions for CPU cache line overlap ---

> -	entry->is_cache_clean = !!(attrs & DMA_ATTR_CPU_CACHE_CLEAN);
> +	entry->is_cache_clean = attrs & DMA_ATTR_DEBUGGING_IGNORE_CACHELINES;

The '!!' is dropped.  Since is_cache_clean is 'bool', C's implicit
conversion from any non-zero integer to 'true' makes this safe
(DMA_ATTR_DEBUGGING_IGNORE_CACHELINES = 1UL<<11 is non-zero).  Patch 4
extends this to OR two attr bits, so the value is still non-zero
whenever either attr is set.  Not a bug, but dropping '!!' from a
flag-mask assignment is an unusual style choice; the original was more
self-documenting.

--- [PATCH v3 4/8] dma-mapping: Introduce DMA require coherency attribute ---

> +	if (!dev_is_dma_coherent(dev) && (attrs & DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT))
> +		return -EOPNOTSUPP;
> +
> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(!dev->dma_mask))
> +		return 0;

(in __dma_map_sg_attrs)

In dma_map_phys the coherency check is placed *after* the dma_mask
WARN_ON_ONCE, but here it is placed *before* it.  Neither ordering is
wrong, but a non-coherent device with no dma_mask returns -EOPNOTSUPP
via this path while the phys path would return DMA_MAPPING_ERROR from
the WARN path.  A comment on the ordering choice would help future
readers.

More importantly: dma_map_sgtable() kdoc lists four documented error
codes (-EINVAL, -ENOMEM, -EIO, -EREMOTEIO).  The new early return
adds -EOPNOTSUPP to the set that can reach callers via dma_map_sgtable,
but the kdoc is not updated.  Could you add -EOPNOTSUPP there so that
callers know they may see it on a non-coherent system?

> +It must never be used in an in-kernel driver that only works with
> +kernal memory.
                  ^^^^^^

Tyo: "kernal" -> "kernel".

--- [PATCH v3 5/8] dma-direct: prevent SWIOTLB path ---

> -		if (is_swiotlb_active(dev))
> +		if (is_swiotlb_active(dev) &&
> +		    !(attrs & DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT))
>  			return swiotlb_map(dev, phys, size, dir, attrs);
>
>  		goto err_overflow;

Verified the full dma_direct_map_phys body: when DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT
is set and SWIOTLB would be needed, the code falls through to
err_overflow and returns DMA_MAPPING_ERROR.  Correct.

The symmetric change to dma_direct_unmap_phys (early return skipping
both dma_direct_sync_single_for_cpu and swiotlb_tbl_unmap_single) is
also correct: a successful map with this attribute guarantees no SWIOTLB
bounce was used and the device is coherent, so neither sync nor
swiotlb_tbl_unmap_single has work to do.

--- [PATCH v3 6/8] iommu/dma: add support for DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT ---

> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE((state->__size & DMA_IOVA_USE_SWIOTLB) &&
> +			 (attrs & DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT)))
> +		return -EOPNOTSUPP;
> +
> +	if (!dev_is_dma_coherent(dev) && (attrs & DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT))
> +		return -EOPNOTSUPP;

The DMA_IOVA_USE_SWIOTLB check is a good defensive assertion: if a
previous dma_iova_link call already took the SWIOTLB path, subsequent
calls with REQUIRE_COHERENT should never reach this function (the map-
level coherency check should have prevented it).  Making it a WARN_ON
that then returns -EOPNOTSUPP rather than continuing is the right call.

--- [PATCH v3 7/8] RDMA/umem: Tell DMA mapping that UMEM requires coherency ---

> -	if (dirty)
>  		ib_dma_unmap_sgtable_attrs(dev, &umem->sgt_append.sgt,
> -					   DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL, 0);
> +					   DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL,
> +					   DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT);

The map path uses 'dma_attr', which may also have DMA_ATTR_WEAK_ORDERING
ORed in (when IB_ACCESS_RELAXED_ORDERING is set).  The unmap path
hardcodes only DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT, so DMA_ATTR_WEAK_ORDERING is
absent at unmap time.  Tracing through dma_unmap_sg_attrs ->
dma_direct_unmap_sg: on a coherent device the sync helpers are no-ops
and DMA_ATTR_WEAK_ORDERING has no effect at unmap time, so this
asymmetry is harmless today.  That said, should the umem struct store
the dma_attr used at map time and replay it at unmap, rather than
hardcoding a subset?  Not a bug, but worth a note.

--- [PATCH v3 8/8] mm/hmm: Indicate that HMM requires DMA coherency ---

> -	unsigned long attrs = 0;
> +	unsigned long attrs = DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT;

(in both hmm_dma_map_pfn and hmm_dma_unmap_pfn)

In hmm_dma_map_pfn the PCI_P2PDMA_MAP_THRU_HOST_BRIDGE case ORs in
DMA_ATTR_MMIO, giving attrs = DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT | DMA_ATTR_MMIO.
On a non-coherent device the early check in dma_map_phys would return
DMA_MAPPING_ERROR.  However, hmm_dma_alloc() already gates entry on
'if (dma_need_sync || dma_addressing_limited(dev)) return -EOPNOTSUPP',
so hmm_dma_map_pfn is only reachable for coherent devices in practice.
For a coherent device with both attrs set, dma_direct_map_phys falls
into the DMA_ATTR_MMIO branch correctly and DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT
only takes effect in the SWIOTLB guard in the else branch, which is not
reached.  No regression here, but it may be worth a comment in
hmm_dma_map_pfn explaining why REQUIRE_COHERENT and MMIO can coexist.

Thanks,

series.title: mm: khugepaged cleanups and mTHP prerequisites
series.author: Nico Pache <npache@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH mm-unstable v3 0/5] mm: khugepaged cleanups and mTHP prerequisites
[PATCH mm-unstable v3 1/5] mm: consolidate anonymous folio PTE mapping into helpers
[PATCH mm-unstable v3 2/5] mm: introduce is_pmd_order helper
[PATCH mm-unstable v3 3/5] mm/khugepaged: define KHUGEPAGED_MAX_PTES_LIMIT as HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1
[PATCH mm-unstable v3 4/5] mm/khugepaged: rename hpage_collapse_* to collapse_*
[PATCH mm-unstable v3 5/5] mm/khugepaged: unify khugepaged and madv_collapse with collapse_single_pmd()

review.summary: This series refactors anonymous PTE mapping in do_anonymous_page() into two reusable helpers mirroring the PMD-level pattern, introduces is_pmd_order() and KHUGEPAGED_MAX_PTES_LIMIT constants, renames hpage_collapse_* functions, and unifies khugepaged and madvise_collapse PMD scanning via a new collapse_single_pmd() function.
review.review_inline:
Nico Pache <npache@redhat.com>

mm: khugepaged cleanups and mTHP prerequisites (v3, 5-patch series)

Patch 1 (consolidate anonymous folio PTE mapping)
--------------------------------------------------

> +void map_anon_folio_pte_nopf(struct folio *folio, pte_t *pte,
> +		struct vm_area_struct *vma, unsigned long addr,
> +		bool uffd_wp)
> +{
> +	unsigned int nr_pages = folio_nr_pages(folio);
> +	pte_t entry = folio_mk_pte(folio, vma->vm_page_prot);
> +
[ ... ]
> +	set_ptes(vma->vm_mm, addr, pte, entry, nr_pages);
> +	update_mmu_cache_range(NULL, vma, addr, pte, nr_pages);
> +}

Passing NULL for vmf is correct.  Checked every in-tree
update_mmu_cache_range() implementation (x86, arm64, arc, xtensa,
csky, parisc, nios2): none dereference the vmf pointer.  arm64
defines update_mmu_cache as update_mmu_cache_range(NULL, ...) already.

> +static void map_anon_folio_pte_pf(struct folio *folio, pte_t *pte,
> +		struct vm_area_struct *vma, unsigned long addr, bool uffd_wp)
> +{
> +	unsigned int order = folio_order(folio);
> +
> +	map_anon_folio_pte_nopf(folio, pte, vma, addr, uffd_wp);
> +	add_mm_counter(vma->vm_mm, MM_ANONPAGES, 1 << order);
> +	count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_ALLOC);
> +}

The MM_ANONPAGES delta is equivalent to the removed nr_pages:
folio_nr_pages(folio) == 1 << folio_order(folio).  Moving
add_mm_counter after set_ptes is fine; the counter is per-mm
accounting and is not observed under the PTE spinlock.

Verified: the PTE spinlock is held by the caller at the
map_anon_folio_pte_pf call site (after pte_offset_map_lock, after
vmf_pte_changed and userfaultfd_missing checks).  folio_ref_add,
folio_add_new_anon_rmap, folio_add_lru_vma, and set_ptes order all
match the original sequence.  The uffd_wp handling is preserved
identically in the helper.

Zero-page path: addr is still vmf->address at that point (before the
ALIGN_DOWN reassignment for the write path), and set_pte_at is
equivalent to set_ptes with nr=1 on all architectures.  The
update_mmu_cache_range in the zero-page path correctly continues to
pass vmf (not NULL) matching the original.

Patch 5 (collapse_single_pmd unification)
-----------------------------------------

> +	if (result == SCAN_PTE_MAPPED_HUGEPAGE) {
> +		mmap_read_lock(mm);
> +		if (collapse_test_exit_or_disable(mm))
> +			result = SCAN_ANY_PROCESS;
> +		else
> +			result = try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp(mm, addr,
> +							     !cc->is_khugepaged);
> +		if (result == SCAN_PMD_MAPPED)
> +			result = SCAN_SUCCEED;
> +		mmap_read_unlock(mm);
> +	}
> +end:
> +	if (cc->is_khugepaged && result == SCAN_SUCCEED)
> +		++khugepaged_pages_collapsed;

mmap_lock state and khugepaged_pages_collapsed accounting checked
against the pre-patch code:

For collapse_scan_mm_slot: when collapse_test_exit_or_disable is true
in the old SCAN_PTE_MAPPED_HUGEPAGE branch, the old code went to
breakouterloop (mmap_read_unlock + skip counter).  New code returns
SCAN_ANY_PROCESS with mmap already unlocked inside the helper, so
mmap_locked=false causes collapse_scan_mm_slot to jump to
breakouterloop_mmap_lock (no double-unlock).  Correct.

For madvise_collapse: SCAN_ANY_PROCESS falls to the default case,
sets last_fail, and goes to out_maybelock which re-acquires mmap_lock
if needed.  Correct.

khugepaged_pages_collapsed: only incremented for is_khugepaged &&
SCAN_SUCCEED.  The SCAN_PMD_MAPPED -> SCAN_SUCCEED conversion inside
the helper preserves the original increment path.  Correct.

lock_dropped semantics: the file path always releases mmap before
collapse_scan_file, so *mmap_locked is false on return from
collapse_single_pmd in all file cases; the !mmap_locked check in
madvise_collapse is equivalent to the original unconditional set.
For the anonymous path, *mmap_locked is false only when
collapse_huge_page was called, matching the original.

The noted behavioral change (skipping VMA revalidation on writeback
retry) is safe: collapse_scan_file does not require mmap_lock, pgoff
was captured before the lock was released, and if the mapping changed
the scan will fail on page state independently.  The commit message
explicitly documents this.

Patches 2-4 are mechanical renames and constant introductions with
no behavioral change.  No issues found across the series.

series.author: Mukesh Ojha <mukesh.ojha@oss.qualcomm.com>

[PATCH v2 02/25] init/version: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 03/25] mm/percpu: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 04/25] cpu: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 05/25] genirq/irqdesc: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 06/25] timers: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 07/25] kernel/fork: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 08/25] mm/page_alloc: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 09/25] mm/show_mem: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 10/25] mm/swapfile: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 11/25] kernel/vmcore_info: Register dynamic information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 12/25] kernel/configs: Register dynamic information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 13/25] mm/init-mm: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 14/25] panic: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 15/25] kallsyms: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 16/25] mm/mm_init: Annotate static information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 17/25] sched/core: Annotate runqueues into meminspect
[PATCH v2 18/25] mm/numa: Register node data information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 19/25] mm/sparse: Register information into meminspect
[PATCH v2 20/25] printk: Register information into meminspect

[PATCH v2 23/25] soc: qcom: smem: Add minidump platform device
[PATCH v2 24/25] dt-bindings: reserved-memory: Add Google Kinfo Pixel reserved memory
[PATCH v2 25/25] meminspect: Add debug kinfo compatible driver

review.summary: This series introduces meminspect, a mechanism that registers kernel memory regions into a static table for later inspection by drivers (e.g., crash dump, minidump). Patches 02-16 annotate existing kernel variables into that table using compile-time section macros or init-time API calls.
review.review_inline:
Author: Eugen Hristev <eugen.hristev@linaro.org>
         Mukesh Ojha <mukesh.ojha@oss.qualcomm.com>

[PATCH v2 00/25] Introduce meminspect

A few observations on the mm-touching patches follow.

--- [PATCH v2 03/25] mm/percpu: Annotate static information into
    meminspect ---

> diff --git a/mm/percpu.c b/mm/percpu.c
> --- a/mm/percpu.c
> +++ b/mm/percpu.c

[ ... ]

> +#endif	/* CONFIG_SMP */
>  
> +MEMINSPECT_SIMPLE_ENTRY(__per_cpu_offset);
>  /*
>   * pcpu_nr_pages - calculate total number of populated backing pages

The array __per_cpu_offset[NR_CPUS] is declared and defined inside the
#ifdef CONFIG_SMP block that ends at that exact #endif on the line just
above.  The MEMINSPECT_SIMPLE_ENTRY is placed after the #endif, meaning
it is compiled unconditionally.  On a UP (non-SMP) build __per_cpu_offset
does not exist, so this will produce a compile error.

The entry must be moved inside the CONFIG_SMP guard, e.g. placed directly
after the EXPORT_SYMBOL(__per_cpu_offset) line.

Furthermore, asm-generic/percpu.h guards the extern declaration with
#ifndef __per_cpu_offset, because some architectures define that symbol
as a preprocessor macro instead (arm64 does this for the KVM nVHE case).
To be safe the annotation should mirror that guard:

    #ifdef CONFIG_SMP
    #ifndef __per_cpu_offset
    MEMINSPECT_SIMPLE_ENTRY(__per_cpu_offset);
    #endif
    #endif

--- [PATCH v2 15/25] kallsyms: Annotate static information into
    meminspect ---

> diff --git a/kernel/kallsyms.c b/kernel/kallsyms.c
> --- a/kernel/kallsyms.c
> +++ b/kernel/kallsyms.c
> +MEMINSPECT_SIMPLE_ENTRY(kallsyms_num_syms);
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(kallsyms_offsets, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(kallsyms_names, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(kallsyms_token_table, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(kallsyms_token_index, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(kallsyms_markers, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(kallsyms_seqs_of_names, sizeof(void *));

The documentation for MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY describes the size argument
as the actual size of the region, "useful for arrays which do not have a
fixed size at compile time."  Using sizeof(void *) (8 bytes on 64-bit)
for tables like kallsyms_offsets, kallsyms_names, and kallsyms_token_table
-- which can be megabytes in size -- only registers the first 8 bytes of
each array.

If the intent is to record the runtime virtual address of each symbol so
a crash tool can apply KASLR correction against vmlinux, that should be
documented explicitly and the macro ought to be named something distinct
(e.g., MEMINSPECT_ADDR_ENTRY) to avoid confusion with uses like patch 08
where MEMINSPECT_SIMPLE_ENTRY(node_states) correctly captures the full
array via sizeof(node_states).

The same question applies to MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(swapper_pg_dir,
sizeof(void *)) in patch 13: is capturing 8 bytes of the PGD intentional,
or should the full page directory (one page, 4 KiB on most arches) be
registered?

Similarly for the text-section boundary symbols in patch 13:

> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(_sinittext, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(_einittext, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(_end, sizeof(void *));
> +MEMINSPECT_AREA_ENTRY(_text, sizeof(void *));

If these are meant solely to convey the address of each boundary (so a
crash tool can compute section ranges), sizeof(void *) bytes starting at
&_text is the first 8 bytes of kernel .text, not a pointer to it.
Please clarify the intended semantics, perhaps with a comment, so that
users of the API understand when sizeof(void *) is the right size to
pass.

--- [PATCH v2 12/25] kernel/configs: Register dynamic information
    into meminspect ---

> +	/* Register 8 bytes before and after, to catch the marker too */
> +	meminspect_lock_register_id_va(MEMINSPECT_ID_CONFIG,
> +				       (void *)&kernel_config_data - 8,
> +				       &kernel_config_data_end -
> +				       &kernel_config_data + 16);

The size arithmetic is correct: 8 bytes for "IKCFG_ST", the gz payload,
and 8 bytes for "IKCFG_ED", verified against the asm block in configs.c.

Two minor points:

1. ikconfig_init() is compiled only under #ifdef CONFIG_IKCONFIG_PROC,
   so the config data goes unregistered when CONFIG_IKCONFIG=y but
   CONFIG_IKCONFIG_PROC=n, even though the embedded data is present.
   Is that intentional?

2. The expression "(void *)&kernel_config_data - 8" relies on GCC's
   void* arithmetic extension (not standard C).  Prefer
   "(void *)((char *)&kernel_config_data - 8)" for clarity.

--- [PATCH v2 08/25] mm/page_alloc, [PATCH v2 09/25] mm/show_mem,
    [PATCH v2 10/25] mm/swapfile, [PATCH v2 13/25] mm/init-mm ---

The remaining mm/ patches (node_states, _totalram_pages, nr_swapfiles,
init_mm) are straightforward compile-time annotations that do not touch
any execution path.  Verified that:
- All annotated symbols exist at the point of annotation.
- MEMINSPECT_SIMPLE_ENTRY uses sizeof() of the actual object, which is
  correct for node_states (full nodemask_t array), _totalram_pages
  (atomic_long_t), nr_swapfiles (unsigned int), and init_mm (full
  struct mm_struct).
- No locking, control-flow, or refcount changes are introduced.

These hunks look fine modulo the sizeof(void *) design question raised
above for swapper_pg_dir.

series.title: mm: vmscan: add PID and cgroup ID to vmscan tracepoints
series.author: Thomas Ballasi <tballasi@linux.microsoft.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v8 0/3] mm: vmscan: add PID and cgroup ID to vmscan tracepoints
[PATCH v8 1/3] tracing: Add __event_in_*irq() helpers
[PATCH v8 2/3] mm: vmscan: add cgroup IDs to vmscan tracepoints
[PATCH v8 3/3] mm: vmscan: add PIDs to vmscan tracepoints

review.summary: This series adds PID, IRQ context, and memcg ID fields to key vmscan tracepoints, and introduces __event_in_irq() helpers that read the existing trace entry header flags. The tracing infrastructure changes look correct, but reordering existing TP_STRUCT__entry fields in patch 2/3 breaks the binary tracepoint ABI, and a cosmetic trailing-space issue exists in the TP_printk format strings.
review.review_inline:
Thomas Ballasi <tballasi@linux.microsoft.com>

mm: vmscan: add PID and cgroup ID to vmscan tracepoints

--- [PATCH v8 1/3] tracing: Add __event_in_*irq() helpers ---

Verified: TRACE_FLAG_HARDIRQ=0x08, TRACE_FLAG_SOFTIRQ=0x10 in
enum trace_flag_type (include/linux/trace_events.h). The hardcoded
values in stage7_class_define.h (0x8, 0x10, 0x18) match exactly,
and the comment explaining why the enum names are not used is
helpful. Patch 1 looks good.

> +#define __event_in_hardirq()    (__entry->ent.flags & TRACE_FLAG_HARDIRQ)
> +#define __event_in_softirq()    (__entry->ent.flags & TRACE_FLAG_SOFTIRQ)
> +#define __event_in_irq()        (__entry->ent.flags & (TRACE_FLAG_HARDIRQ | TRACE_FLAG_SOFTIRQ))

Access to __entry->ent.flags is valid in TP_printk context: every
trace event struct embeds struct trace_entry ent as its first member
and __entry is a typed pointer to that struct. __entry->ent.pid in
patch 3 follows the same logic.

--- [PATCH v8 2/3] mm: vmscan: add cgroup IDs to vmscan tracepoints ---

Verified: mem_cgroup_id() handles NULL safely. In the CONFIG_MEMCG=y
path it returns cgroup_id(memcg->css.cgroup) only after the NULL
check; in the CONFIG_MEMCG=n stub it returns 0 unconditionally. Call
sites passing integer 0 for the memcg argument (try_to_free_pages,
node_reclaim) are safe.

However, there is a binary ABI concern. The TP_STRUCT__entry layout
for mm_vmscan_direct_reclaim_begin_template is reordered:

> -	__field(	int,	order		)
> -	__field(	unsigned long,	gfp_flags	)
> +	__field(	unsigned long,	gfp_flags	)
> +	__field(	u64,	memcg_id	)
> +	__field(	int,	order		)

This swaps order and gfp_flags in the binary ring buffer record,
breaking any userspace tool or BPF program that accesses these fields
by compiled-in offset rather than by reading the format file at
runtime.

The same concern applies to mm_shrink_slab_start, where nid moves
from position 3 to position 9 in the struct:

> -	__field(struct shrinker *, shr)
> -	__field(void *, shrink)
> -	__field(int, nid)
> -	__field(long, nr_objects_to_shrink)
> +	__field(struct shrinker *, shr)
> +	__field(void *, shrink)
> +	__field(long, nr_objects_to_shrink)
> +	...
> +	__field(int, nid)
> +	__field(u64, memcg_id)

The existing code in this same file already documents this concern:

    /* classzone is previous name of the highest_zoneidx.
     * Reason not to change it is the ABI requirement of
     * the tracepoint. */

Could the new memcg_id fields simply be appended to the end of
TP_STRUCT__entry instead of being interleaved with existing fields?
Appending preserves the binary layout of all existing fields and
avoids breaking tools that have compiled-in offsets.

--- [PATCH v8 3/3] mm: vmscan: add PIDs to vmscan tracepoints ---

> +	TP_printk("order=%d gfp_flags=%s pid=%d memcg_id=%llu %s",
> +		__entry->order,
> +		show_gfp_flags(__entry->gfp_flags),
> +		__entry->ent.pid,
> +		__entry->memcg_id,
> +		__event_in_irq() ? "(in-irq)" : "")

When the event fires in task context, __event_in_irq() is false and
the last argument is "". The format string still has a space before
%s, so every non-IRQ trace line ends with a trailing space. The same
pattern appears in all four modified TP_printk calls
(direct_reclaim_begin, direct_reclaim_end, mm_shrink_slab_start,
mm_shrink_slab_end). A format like "%s%s" where the first string
is either " (in-irq)" or "" (empty, no leading space) is one way
to avoid the trailing space in the common case.

series.title: arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages
series.author: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/4] arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages
[PATCH 1/4] arm64: request contpte-sized folios for exec memory
[PATCH 2/4] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
[PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
[PATCH 4/4] mm: align file-backed mmap to exec folio order in thp_get_unmapped_area

review.summary: This series enables contpte-sized iTLB coalescing for executable file-backed mappings on arm64 16K and 64K pages by fixing exec_folio_order(), bypassing the mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead, aligning PIE load addresses to the folio boundary, and adding a folio-size alignment fallback in thp_get_unmapped_area_vmflags(). The series is mostly correct but patch 2 silently overrides THP readahead when VM_HUGEPAGE+VM_EXEC are both set on 4K/16K arm64, and patch 3 reduces ASLR entropy for all PIE binaries by a factor of up to 128 on 16K pages.
review.review_inline:
Author: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages

Overall the series is well-motivated and the benchmark numbers are
convincing.  I have two concerns worth discussing before merging.

---
[PATCH 1/4] arm64: request contpte-sized folios for exec memory

> -#define exec_folio_order() ilog2(SZ_64K >> PAGE_SHIFT)
> +#define exec_folio_order() ilog2(CONT_PTES)

Verified: CONT_PTES = 1 << CONFIG_ARM64_CONT_PTE_SHIFT.
For 4K pages (shift=4): ilog2(16)=4 (64K). Unchanged.
For 16K pages (shift=7): ilog2(128)=7 (2M). Correct new value.
For 64K pages (shift=5): ilog2(32)=5 (2M). Correct new value.
The old ilog2(SZ_64K >> PAGE_SHIFT) gave orders 2 and 0 for 16K/64K
respectively, so the fix is correct and the math checks out.

---
[PATCH 2/4] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead

> +	if (vm_flags & VM_EXEC) {
> +		[ ... ]
> +		ra->order = exec_folio_order();
> +		ra->start = round_down(vmf->pgoff, 1UL << ra->order);
> +		ra->start = max(ra->start, start);
> +		ra_end = round_up(ra->start + ra->ra_pages, 1UL << ra->order);
> +		ra_end = min(ra_end, end);
> +		ra->size = ra_end - ra->start;
> +		ra->async_size = 0;
> +		goto do_readahead;
> +	}

Moving the VM_EXEC block above the mmap_miss check correctly fixes the
mmap_miss runaway described in the cover letter.  The VMA-boundary
clamping logic and the goto are correct.

However, the new VM_EXEC block now also precedes the
force_thp_readahead handling.  force_thp_readahead is set when:

    (vm_flags & VM_HUGEPAGE) && HPAGE_PMD_ORDER <= MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER

With XA_CHUNK_SHIFT=6, MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER=min(11, HPAGE_PMD_ORDER).
On arm64 4K pages HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=9, so force_thp_readahead can be
true.  On arm64 16K pages HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=11, same story.  (On 64K
pages HPAGE_PMD_ORDER=13 > MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER=11, so
force_thp_readahead is always false there -- the primary target of
this series is not affected.)

For a file-backed exec VMA with madvise(MADV_HUGEPAGE) applied on
4K or 16K arm64, the pre-patch code reaches the force_thp_readahead
block and issues a PMD-order readahead window (HPAGE_PMD_NR * 2 pages
with PG_readahead markers).  Post-patch, the new VM_EXEC block fires
first and issues a single contpte-sized folio (order 4 or 7) with
async_size=0, silently bypassing THP readahead entirely.

Is this intentional?  The commit message does not mention this
interaction.  Replacing a 2x-PMD prefetch window with a single-folio
targeted read for exec+hugepage VMAs is a meaningful change in
readahead behavior on 4K and 16K configurations.

One option would be to guard the new VM_EXEC block:

    if (!force_thp_readahead && (vm_flags & VM_EXEC))

so that the existing THP readahead path is preserved for VMAs that
have explicitly requested hugepage treatment.

---
[PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping

> +			if (exec_folio_order())
> +				alignment = max(alignment,
> +					(unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());

The placement (after maximum_alignment(), before the PIE handling
split) is correct for both the interpreter and static-PIE branches.
For static PIE (ld.so, no PT_INTERP), the new alignment exceeds
ELF_MIN_ALIGN=PAGE_SIZE, which correctly triggers the existing
preliminary-mmap alignment path.

For the interpreter (PIE program) branch, load_bias is computed as:

    load_bias = ELF_ET_DYN_BASE + arch_mmap_rnd();
    if (alignment)
        load_bias &= ~(alignment - 1);

With alignment bumped to 2M on 64K pages, the load address is
truncated to 2M granularity.  This divides the number of distinct
PIE load addresses by 32 (PAGE_SIZE=64K, folio=2M, ratio=32).
On 16K pages the folio is also 2M but PAGE_SIZE is 16K, so the
factor is 128.

This ASLR entropy reduction affects every PIE binary on the system,
not only those whose executable segments benefit from contpte mapping.
Has this tradeoff been reviewed from a security perspective?  It would
help to quantify the loss in bits in the commit message -- for example,
on a typical arm64 64K distro kernel with mmap_rnd_bits=18, effective
PIE load-address entropy drops from 18 to 13 bits.

---
[PATCH 4/4] mm: align file-backed mmap to exec folio order in
            thp_get_unmapped_area

> +	if (exec_folio_order()) {
> +		unsigned long folio_size = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
> +
> +		ret = __thp_get_unmapped_area(filp, addr, len, off, flags,
> +					      folio_size, vm_flags);
> +		if (ret)
> +			return ret;
> +	}

The fallback is tried only after PMD_SIZE alignment fails (ret==0),
so it is purely additive.  __thp_get_unmapped_area() already handles
the 32-bit and in_compat_syscall() early-return cases, and the
overflow guards (len_pad, off_end) are inside that helper.  On
architectures where exec_folio_order() returns 0 (the generic
default) the block is dead.  Looks correct.

As the commit message notes, this aligns all file-backed mmaps, not
just exec ones.  The commit subject says "exec folio order" but the
change touches every mmap routed through thp_get_unmapped_area,
including read-only data segments.  Worth making that explicit in
the subject or first paragraph to avoid surprising reviewers of
future bisections.

series.title: elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
series.author: WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

patches.title: [PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping

review.summary: The patch adds exec_folio_order()-based alignment to the ET_DYN load-bias calculation in load_elf_binary(), bumping minimum alignment to 64K on arm64 with 4K pages so contiguous-PTE (contpte) mappings can be used for PIE and static-PIE executables. WANG Rui raises a valid question about whether the alignment bump should be gated on the segment already being huge-aligned, to avoid ASLR entropy reduction and unnecessary probe-mmap overhead for binaries that cannot benefit.
review.review_inline:
WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping

> +			if (exec_folio_order())
> +				alignment = max(alignment,
> +					(unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());

Your concern about ASLR entropy is well-founded, and I think the
situation is actually worse than just entropy loss: for a binary whose
first PT_LOAD has a p_vaddr that is not huge-aligned (e.g. a typical
4K-aligned binary), forcing load_bias to 64K alignment does not make
the actual mapping huge-aligned.  The real mapping starts at

  load_bias + p_vaddr

and if p_vaddr is 0x1000 while load_bias is masked to 64K, the result
is still only 4K-aligned, so contpte will not engage.  We pay the
ASLR entropy cost for all PIE binaries (4 bits lost on arm64 with 4K
pages) with no benefit for binaries not linked with huge-page
alignment.

Note also that on the static-PIE path (ET_DYN without PT_INTERP) in
load_elf_binary():

[ ... ]

> 			if (alignment > ELF_MIN_ALIGN) {
> 				load_bias = elf_load(bprm->file, 0, elf_ppnt,
> 					     elf_prot, elf_flags, total_size);

With the new alignment (64K) always exceeding ELF_MIN_ALIGN (PAGE_SIZE
= 4K on arm64/4K), this branch is taken for every static-PIE binary,
not just those linked with a large p_align.  Every ld.so or static-PIE
executable now incurs a probe mmap at address 0 followed by
vm_munmap(), with the subsequent remap using MAP_FIXED_NOREPLACE.  For
a binary not linked with 64K alignment, load_bias + p_vaddr is still
not 64K-aligned after masking, so no contpte benefit accrues and the
extra probe overhead is pure waste.

For binaries that ARE linked with 64K-aligned segments -- p_align >=
64K, with p_vaddr and p_offset both 64K-aligned -- maximum_alignment()
already returns 64K, so the existing code in load_elf_binary() already
handles the beneficial case correctly without this addition.

Minor: exec_folio_order() is evaluated twice in the snippet.  Storing
the result in a local avoids the double call.

Your suggested constraint -- only bumping alignment when the segment
vaddr, filesz/memsz, and file offset are all huge-aligned -- is
exactly what is needed to avoid both the ASLR regression and the
spurious probe-mmap regression while still benefiting binaries that
can actually use contpte.

series.title: locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
series.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/2] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
[PATCH v2 1/2] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
[PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

review.summary: This series adds a contended_release tracepoint that fires on the lock-holder side when a contended lock is released, complementing the existing contention_begin/contention_end waiter-side tracepoints; it also refactors percpu_up_read()'s slowpath into a new __percpu_up_read() helper to avoid inlining the tracepoint call.
review.review_inline:
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

I traced through all eight call sites against the current tree.  The
placement in mutex, semaphore, rwsem (up_read/up_write/downgrade),
rtmutex, and rwbase read-unlock all fire only when waiters are
