confirmed present.  The __percpu_up_read() placement (between smp_mb()
and this_cpu_dec) is fine: the existing smp_mb() already orders the
critical section stores before the counter decrement; the tracepoint
call is a compiler barrier and does not weaken that ordering.  The
patch 1 extraction is correct: lockdep_assert_preemption_disabled()
matches the calling context (preempt_disable'd in percpu_up_read), and
the EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL is consistent with Peter's request.

One site stands out:

> diff --git a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
> --- a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
> +++ b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
> @@ -263,6 +263,8 @@ void percpu_up_write(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
>  {
>  	rwsem_release(&sem->dep_map, _RET_IP_);
>
> +	trace_contended_release(sem);
> +
>  	/*
>  	 * Signal the writer is done, no fast path yet.

This fires on every percpu_up_write() call, even when sem->waiters is
empty.  Every other site guards with a confirmed-waiters check:

  - mutex:    inside if (waiter)
  - semaphore: inside if (!wake_q_empty(&wake_q))
  - rwsem:    inside if (unlikely(tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS))
  - rtmutex:  after while (!rt_mutex_has_waiters(lock)) exits
  - rwbase write unlock:

> +	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rt_mutex_has_waiters(rtm))
> +		trace_contended_release(rwb);

The cover letter says percpu_up_write "always wakes via __wake_up()",
but __wake_up() with an empty sem->waiters is a no-op -- there need
not be any waiters.  A write lock can be acquired and released without
contention even while sem->block is set.  The result is that
trace_contended_release fires on every write-side unlock, making the
event misleading for tooling trying to identify genuinely contended
release events.

An analogous fix using the same pattern as rwbase_write_unlock would
be:

  if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && waitqueue_active(&sem->waiters))
          trace_contended_release(sem);

This check is safe before atomic_set_release(&sem->block, 0): while
sem->block is still 1 no new waiters can drain off sem->waiters, so
the read of waitqueue_active() is stable.

Could the placement of trace_contended_release(sem) in
percpu_up_write() be moved inside such a guard to match all other
call sites?

series.title: Docs/mm: document the OOM killer
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

review.summary: Documentation patch filling in the oom.rst stub with an overview of OOM killer scoring, constraint handling, the OOM reaper, process_mrelease, and sysctl knobs. The description is accurate in most parts but names a non-existent function and omits swap from the cpuset/mempolicy totalpages descriptions.
review.review_inline:
Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

Thanks for filling in this stub.  I cross-checked the descriptions
against mm/oom_kill.c.  Two inaccuracies to fix:

> +The reaper gives the victim a short window to exit naturally before
> +intervening.  It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls
> +``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.

`unmap_page_range()` does not exist in mm/oom_kill.c or mm/memory.c.
The function actually invoked by __oom_reap_task_mm() is
`zap_vma_for_reaping()`, which zaps page table entries per-VMA
without blocking.  Please correct the function name.

> +The ``totalpages`` baseline depends on the allocation constraint:
> +
> +- **Unconstrained**: all RAM plus swap.
> +- **Cpuset**: memory on nodes in the current cpuset.
> +- **Memory policy**: memory on nodes in the current mempolicy.
> +- **Memory cgroup**: the cgroup's memory limit.

For CONSTRAINT_CPUSET and CONSTRAINT_MEMORY_POLICY, constrained_alloc()
initialises oc->totalpages to total_swap_pages first, then adds
node_present_pages() for each node in the relevant mask.  Swap is
included in those two cases as well.  The bullets should read something
like:

  - **Cpuset**: swap plus RAM on nodes in the current cpuset.
  - **Memory policy**: swap plus RAM on nodes in the mempolicy nodemask.

Everything else checked out: the oom_badness() scoring formula (rss +
swapents + pgtable pages, adjusted by oom_score_adj * totalpages /
1000), oom_unkillable_task() excluding global init and kthreads, the
MMF_UNSTABLE/MMF_OOM_SKIP ordering in __oom_reap_task_mm() and
oom_reap_task(), the process_mrelease() requirement that the target
already be exiting (task_will_free_mem()), and all three sysctl default
values.

series.title: ACPI: Add support for ACPI RAS2 feature table
series.author: <shiju.jose@huawei.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v17 0/2] ACPI: Add support for ACPI RAS2 feature table
[PATCH v17 1/2] ACPI:RAS2: Add driver for the ACPI RAS2 feature table
[PATCH v17 2/2] ras: mem: Add ACPI RAS2 memory driver

review.summary: This series adds a new ACPI RAS2 feature table driver and an auxiliary driver for RAS2 hardware-based memory scrubbing, including an extension to the EDAC scrub interface with a new 'enable_demand' attribute for on-demand scrub control.
review.review_inline:
Author: Shiju Jose <shiju.jose@huawei.com>

[PATCH v17 1/2] ACPI:RAS2: Add driver for the ACPI RAS2 feature table
[PATCH v17 2/2] ras: mem: Add ACPI RAS2 memory driver

Verified: bus.c placement is consistent with the void-return pattern
of acpi_viot_init(), acpi_hest_init(), acpi_debugger_init() and other
optional ACPI init calls in acpi_init().  The EDAC scrub enum extension
with SCRUB_ENABLE_DEMAND uses designated initializers throughout
scrub_create_desc() and scrub_attr_visible(), so the index shift is
automatically correct.  PCC_STATUS_CMD_COMPLETE (BIT(0) from pcc.h)
matches ACPI_RAS2_COMMAND_COMPLETE (1) from actbl2.h.  The ras2_sspcc
fields pcc_mrtt, pcc_mpar, deadline_us, last_cmd_cmpl_time,
last_mpar_reset, mpar_count mirror the CPPC driver's proven design.

> diff --git a/drivers/acpi/ras2.c b/drivers/acpi/ras2.c
> new file mode 100644
> +static int check_pcc_chan(struct ras2_sspcc *sspcc)
> +{
> +	struct acpi_ras2_shmem __iomem *gen_comm_base = sspcc->comm_addr;
> +	u32 cap_status;
> +	u16 status;
> +	int rc;
> +
> +	rc = readw_relaxed_poll_timeout(&gen_comm_base->status, status,
> +					status & PCC_STATUS_CMD_COMPLETE,
> +					PCC_MIN_POLL_USECS, sspcc->deadline_us);
> +	if (rc) {
> +		pr_warn("PCC ID: 0x%x: PCC check channel timeout for last command: 0x%x rc=%d\n",
> +			sspcc->pcc_id, sspcc->last_cmd, rc);
> +		return rc;
> +	}
> +
> +	if (status &
[... truncated ...]

The cap_status variable is declared as u32, which matches the
set_caps_status field (u32 at offset 44) in struct acpi_ras2_shmem
from actbl2.h.  The v7 changelog says "add read, clear and check
RAS2 set_cap_status outside if (status & PCC_STATUS_ERROR) check".
Can you confirm that set_caps_status is both read AND written back
to zero in the non-error path?  If the register is not cleared after
reading, firmware may see stale capability status on the next command.

Separately: actbl2.h defines ACPI_RAS2_STATUS as (0x1F<<3), covering
bits [7:3] of the status field, which carries the command completion
code (SUCCESS, NOT_VALID, BUSY, FAILED, INVALID_DATA, etc.).  Does
check_pcc_chan() decode these bits and return an error for non-SUCCESS
values, or is that left to per-command callers reading the parameter
block directly?

> +struct ras2_sspcc {
> +	struct mbox_client		mbox_client;
> +	struct pcc_mbox_chan		*pcc_chan;

[ ... ]

> +	bool				pcc_chnl_acq;
> +};

Does the mbox_client initializer set knows_txdone = true?  The CPPC
driver relies on this flag to signal that the client itself will call
mbox_client_txdone() after polling completion, rather than having the
mailbox framework do it autonomously.  Without knows_txdone = true on
a polled PCC channel the TX-done accounting can misfire.

> +#define RAS2_MAX_NUM_PCC_DESCS 100

The acpi_table_ras2.num_pcc_descs field is u16 (0..65535).  If the
table advertises more than 100 descriptors, is there a pr_warn() so
that admins on oversized platforms know descriptors are being skipped?

> diff --git a/drivers/acpi/bus.c b/drivers/acpi/bus.c
> +	acpi_ras2_init();
> +
>  	return 0;

Verified: acpi_init_pcc() is called at the top of acpi_init() before
acpi_bus_init(), so PCC is fully set up before acpi_ras2_init()
attempts pcc_mbox_request_channel().  Ordering is correct.

> diff --git a/drivers/edac/scrub.c b/drivers/edac/scrub.c

[ ... ]

Verified: scrub_dev_attr[], scrub_attrs[], and the loop bound in
scrub_create_desc() all use SCRUB_MAX_ATTRS symbolically, so adding
SCRUB_ENABLE_DEMAND to the enum automatically resizes all three.
The designated initializer syntax in dev_attr[] (e.g.
[SCRUB_ENABLE_BACKGROUND] = ...) guarantees the correct slot is
filled regardless of the enum value shift.  The scrub_attr_visible()
switch default returns 0 for unknown attr_id, so a missing case for
SCRUB_ENABLE_DEMAND would hide the attribute; please double-check the
switch is updated.

series.title: slab: support memoryless nodes with sheaves
series.author: "Vlastimil Babka (SUSE)" <vbabka@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/3] slab: support memoryless nodes with sheaves
[PATCH 1/3] slab: decouple pointer to barn from kmem_cache_node
[PATCH 2/3] slab: create barns for online memoryless nodes
[PATCH 3/3] slab: free remote objects to sheaves on memoryless nodes

review.summary: Three-patch series decoupling barn storage from kmem_cache_node, extending barn allocation to all online nodes including memoryless ones, and adapting the free-path locality check so CPUs on memoryless nodes can use the percpu-sheaf fast path instead of always falling to the slowpath.
review.review_inline:
From: reviewer
Subject: Re: [PATCH 0/3] slab: support memoryless nodes with sheaves

Overall the series is logically sound and addresses a real performance
problem.  A few questions and observations below.

--- [PATCH 1/3] slab: decouple pointer to barn from kmem_cache_node ---

> +struct kmem_cache_per_node_ptrs {
> +	struct node_barn *barn;
> +	struct kmem_cache_node *node;
> +};

> -	struct kmem_cache_node *node[MAX_NUMNODES];
> +	struct kmem_cache_per_node_ptrs per_node[MAX_NUMNODES];

The diff was truncated before reaching kmem_cache_init(), so I cannot
verify the create_boot_cache() call for kmem_cache was updated.  Today
it reads:

  create_boot_cache(kmem_cache, "kmem_cache",
          offsetof(struct kmem_cache, node) +
                  nr_node_ids * sizeof(struct kmem_cache_node *), ...)

After the rename the field is per_node and each element is
struct kmem_cache_per_node_ptrs (two pointers, not one), so the size
calculation must become:

  offsetof(struct kmem_cache, per_node) +
          nr_node_ids * sizeof(struct kmem_cache_per_node_ptrs)

Did that update land in the truncated portion?  An undersized boot
cache for kmem_cache would be a serious boot-time corruption.

Similarly, slab_mem_going_online_callback() calls
init_kmem_cache_node(n, barn) and assigns s->node[nid]; both must be
converted.  The compile would catch the wrong signature, but can you
confirm the barn allocation path in that function still works after
the restructuring?

> -static void
> -init_kmem_cache_node(struct kmem_cache_node *n, struct node_barn *barn)
> +init_kmem_cache_node(struct kmem_cache_node *n)
>  {
> -	n->barn = barn;
> -	if (barn)
> -		barn_init(barn);
>  }

Verified: every visible barn_init() call site in the diff is followed
immediately by s->per_node[node].barn = barn before any consumer can
race with it.  The error paths in init_kmem_cache_nodes() kfree the
barn before returning 0, symmetric with the allocation.

> -	for_each_kmem_cache_node(s, node, n) {
> -		if (n->barn) {
> +	for_each_node(node) {
> +		struct node_barn *barn = get_barn_node(s, node);
>  		...
> +	for_each_kmem_cache_node(s, node, n) {
> +		s->per_node[node].node = NULL;

The two-pass approach (barns first, then kmem_cache_nodes) is correct
since barns are now independent of kmem_cache_node lifetime.  No use-
after-free: barn pointers are NULLed before the kmem_cache_node is
freed, and nothing reads per_node[].barn after free_kmem_cache_nodes()
returns.

--- [PATCH 2/3] slab: create barns for online memoryless nodes ---

> +static int slub_cpu_setup(unsigned int cpu)
> +{
> +	int nid = cpu_to_node(cpu);
> +	...
> +	if (node_isset(nid, slab_barn_nodes))
> +		return 0;
> +
> +	mutex_lock(&slab_mutex);
> +
> +	if (node_isset(nid, slab_barn_nodes))
> +		goto out;

The lockless pre-check is justified by the comment that nid bits are
never cleared from slab_barn_nodes.  I checked the entire file and
confirmed there is no node_clear() on slab_barn_nodes anywhere, so
the double-check pattern is safe.

> +		if (!cache_has_sheaves(s) || get_barn_node(s, nid))
> +			continue;

If a previous slub_cpu_setup() for the same nid failed partway through
slab_caches, some caches already have barns but slab_barn_nodes is not
set.  The next CPU hotplug event for the same nid will correctly skip
those already-allocated barns and allocate only the remaining ones.
No barn leak on partial failure: free_kmem_cache_nodes() iterates
for_each_node() and frees all per_node[].barn entries.

> -		rcu_sheaf->node = numa_mem_id();
> +		rcu_sheaf->node = numa_node_id();

Consistent with get_barn() switching from numa_mem_id() to
numa_node_id() in patch 1: the sheaf is returned to the barn of the
cpu's actual node rather than the nearest memory node.  Correct.

> +	for_each_online_node(node)
> +		node_set(node, slab_barn_nodes);

> -	for_each_node_mask(node, slab_nodes) {
> +	for_each_node_mask(node, slab_barn_nodes) {
>  		struct node_barn *barn;
>  		barn = kmalloc_node(sizeof(*barn), GFP_KERNEL, node);

bootstrap_cache_sheaves() now allocates barns for all online nodes
(not just memory nodes) during init.  slab_barn_nodes is fully
populated before this call, so no barns are missed.

--- [PATCH 3/3] slab: free remote objects to sheaves on memoryless nodes ---

> +static __always_inline bool can_free_to_pcs(struct slab *slab)
> +{
> +	...
> +#else
> +	numa_node = numa_node_id();
> +
> +	if (likely(slab_node == numa_node))
> +		goto check_pfmemalloc;
> +
> +	if (unlikely(!node_isset(numa_node, slab_nodes)))
> +		goto check_pfmemalloc;
> +#endif

The !CONFIG_HAVE_MEMORYLESS_NODES branch reads slab_nodes locklessly
in the hot free path.  slab_nodes is only ever grown (bits are set on
node-online, never cleared), so a stale read can at worst cause a
transient missed optimization, not a correctness problem.

One incidental behavior change worth noting: kfree_nolock() previously
did NOT check slab_test_pfmemalloc() before calling free_to_pcs(),
while slab_free() did.  can_free_to_pcs() adds that check uniformly
via the check_pfmemalloc label, so pfmemalloc objects in kfree_nolock()
now go to defer_free() instead of the percpu sheaves.  This closes a
pre-existing asymmetry and looks like a welcome fix, but it is worth
calling out explicitly in the commit message.

> -	int node = numa_mem_id();
>  next_remote_batch:
>  	while (i < size) {
>  		...
> -		if (unlikely((IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_NUMA) && slab_nid(slab) != node)
> -			     || slab_test_pfmemalloc(slab))) {
> +		if (unlikely(!can_free_to_pcs(slab))) {

The old code cached node = numa_mem_id() once per invocation of
free_to_pcs_bulk().  The new code calls numa_node_id() per object.
If the thread migrates between iterations the routing decision changes
per object; that is harmless since each object ends up in the barn for
the current cpu's node.  The next_remote_batch re-entry is correct
because i retains its last value and can_free_to_pcs() is stateless.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Page Allocation
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Page Allocation

review.summary: Fills in the page_allocation.rst documentation stub with a high-level description of the buddy allocator, migratetypes, per-CPU pagesets, GFP flags, allocation paths, and watermarks. The content is broadly accurate but contains two factual errors.
review.review_inline:
Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Page Allocation

Good start at filling in this stub.  I spotted two factual issues
while cross-referencing against the source.

> +When a per-CPU list is empty, a batch of pages is taken from the buddy.
> +When it exceeds its high watermark, excess pages are returned.
> +``lru_add_drain()`` and ``drain_all_pages()`` flush per-CPU lists when
> +the system needs an accurate count of free pages, such as during memory
> +hot-remove.

lru_add_drain() drains the LRU addition batches (cpu_fbatches in
mm/swap.c), not the PCP page lists.  The function that drains PCP
lists is drain_all_pages() (and its single-CPU variant
drain_local_pages()).  lru_add_drain() should be removed from this
sentence or given its own separate, accurate description.

> +Each pageblock (typically 2MB on x86) carries a migratetype tag that
> +describes the kind of allocations it serves:
> +
> +- **MIGRATE_UNMOVABLE**: kernel allocations that cannot be relocated
> +  (slab objects, page tables).
> +- **MIGRATE_MOVABLE**: user pages and other content that can be migrated
> +  or reclaimed (used by compaction and memory hot-remove).
> +- **MIGRATE_RECLAIMABLE**: caches that can be dropped under pressure
> +  (page cache, dentries).
> +- **MIGRATE_CMA**: reserved for the contiguous memory allocator;

MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC (== MIGRATE_PCPTYPES in include/linux/mmzone.h) is
missing from the list.  It sits between MIGRATE_RECLAIMABLE and
MIGRATE_CMA and is used for high-order atomic reserves; it cannot be
requested directly via GFP flags but is part of the migratetype
taxonomy and worth mentioning for completeness.

series.title: pid_namespace: make init creation more flexible
series.author: Pavel Tikhomirov <ptikhomirov@virtuozzo.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v5 0/4] pid_namespace: make init creation more flexible
[PATCH v5 1/4] pid_namespace: avoid optimization of accesses to ->child_reaper
[PATCH v5 2/4] pid: check init is created first after idr alloc
[PATCH v5 3/4] pid_namespace: allow opening pid_for_children before init was created
[PATCH v5 4/4] selftests: Add tests for creating pidns init via setns

review.summary: This series annotates pid_namespace->child_reaper accesses with READ/WRITE_ONCE, hardens the "init must be pid 1" invariant by moving the check to after IDR allocation (still under pidmap_lock), and then exploits that invariant to allow setns(pid_for_children) before the namespace's init process is created.
review.review_inline:
Pavel Tikhomirov <ptikhomirov@virtuozzo.com>

pid_namespace: make init creation more flexible (v5)

I read the full implementations of alloc_pid(), free_pid(),
copy_process(), find_child_reaper(), pidns_for_children_get(), and
traced the pid_allocated state machine.  Everything checks out.

--- [PATCH v5 1/4] pid_namespace: avoid optimization of accesses to ->child_reaper ---

> diff --git a/kernel/pid.c b/kernel/pid.c
> @@ -128,7 +128,7 @@ void free_pid(struct pid *pid)
>  			 * is the reaper wake up the reaper.  The reaper
>  			 * may be sleeping in zap_pid_ns_processes().
>  			 */
> -			wake_up_process(ns->child_reaper);
> +			wake_up_process(READ_ONCE(ns->child_reaper));

Verified: free_pid holds pidmap_lock (a spinlock), not tasklist_lock.
The WRITE_ONCE sites in copy_process() and find_child_reaper() both
run under write_lock_irq(&tasklist_lock) -- a different lock.
READ_ONCE is the correct pairing to prevent compiler tearing.

A NULL dereference here is impossible: cases 1 and 2 in the switch
are only reachable after disable_pid_allocation() clears the
PIDNS_ADDING bit (called only from zap_pid_ns_processes(), which
runs when init is dying), at which point child_reaper is set.

> @@ -218,7 +218,7 @@ struct pid *alloc_pid(...)
> -			if (tid != 1 && !tmp->child_reaper)
> +			if (tid != 1 && !READ_ONCE(tmp->child_reaper))

This loop runs before idr_preload/spin_lock(&pidmap_lock), so no
lock stabilises child_reaper here.  READ_ONCE is correct.

The ASSERT_EXCLUSIVE_WRITER annotations in fork.c and exit.c are
also correct: both write sites hold write_lock_irq(&tasklist_lock),
so they are mutually exclusive by construction.

[ ... ]

--- [PATCH v5 2/4] pid: check init is created first after idr alloc ---

> diff --git a/kernel/pid.c b/kernel/pid.c
> -			if (tid != 1 && !READ_ONCE(tmp->child_reaper))
> -				goto out_abort;
>
> +		/*
> +		 * PID 1 (init) must be created first.
> +		 */
> +		if (!READ_ONCE(tmp->child_reaper) && nr != 1) {
> +			retval = -EINVAL;
> +			goto out_free;
> +		}

The new check runs inside spin_lock(&pidmap_lock), after storing
nr into pid->numbers[i] and decrementing i.  The out_free cleanup
loop starts at ++i (recovering the just-decremented value) and
walks back to ns->level, correctly removing every IDR entry that
was allocated before the check fired.

The cursor reset "if (ns->pid_allocated == PIDNS_ADDING)" fires
correctly here because pid_allocated has not yet been incremented
when we branch to out_free -- the next caller can start from PID 1.

For the non-set_tid path on a fresh namespace: idr_get_cursor is 0,
pid_min is set to 1, so idr_alloc_cyclic returns 1 on the first
call.  nr=1 means the check never triggers.  No regression.

For set_tid with tid != 1 and no init: the IDR entry is allocated
then immediately freed via out_free, returning EINVAL.  The old
code avoided the allocation entirely, but the caller sees the same
error either way.

[ ... ]

--- [PATCH v5 3/4] pid_namespace: allow opening pid_for_children before init was created ---

> diff --git a/kernel/pid_namespace.c b/kernel/pid_namespace.c
> -	if (ns) {
> -		read_lock(&tasklist_lock);
> -		if (!ns->child_reaper) {
> -			put_pid_ns(ns);
> -			ns = NULL;
> -		}
> -		read_unlock(&tasklist_lock);
> -	}

Safe to remove.  The invariant is now enforced inside alloc_pid():
the first PID allocated in any namespace must have nr=1 (checked
post-IDR under pidmap_lock).  idr_alloc serialises the race between
multiple callers trying to become init: only one gets nr=1; all
others receive EEXIST and fail.  A caller that races and observes
child_reaper==NULL with nr>1 gets EINVAL and can retry.

Checked wake_up_process(READ_ONCE(ns->child_reaper)) in free_pid:
values 1 and 2 (with PIDNS_ADDING bit clear) are only reachable
after disable_pid_allocation() runs from zap_pid_ns_processes(),
which only runs when init is exiting -- child_reaper is non-NULL
at that point.  No NULL dereference is possible.

Looks good overall.

series.title: mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
series.author: <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v1] mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE

review.summary: The patch guards DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE enum values and their sysfs/vaddr references with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE ifdefs, preventing these actions from being exposed when THP is not compiled in. The approach is correct but the kdoc comment block in damon.h still references the conditionally-compiled enum members without any guard annotation.
review.review_inline:
From: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE

Verified: sysfs action_store()/action_show() use string matching against
damos_sysfs_action_names[], not numeric enum values, so the renumbering
of DAMOS_LRU_PRIO and later members when THP=n does not affect the
sysfs ABI. fvaddr shares damon_va_apply_scheme() with vaddr, so the
vaddr.c guard covers both operation sets. NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS is defined
but not used for array sizing or range checks anywhere in tree, so its
value change under THP=n is benign. The three modified sites (damon.h,
sysfs-schemes.c, vaddr.c) are exhaustive -- no other files outside the
damon directory reference DAMOS_HUGEPAGE or DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE.

One thing to address:

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
> --- a/include/linux/damon.h
> +++ b/include/linux/damon.h
> @@ -138,8 +138,10 @@ enum damos_action {
>  	DAMOS_WILLNEED,
>  	DAMOS_COLD,
>  	DAMOS_PAGEOUT,
> +#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
>  	DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
>  	DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
> +#endif

The kdoc comment block above the enum still contains:

  * @DAMOS_HUGEPAGE:   Call ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_HUGEPAGE.
  * @DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_NOHUGEPAGE.

without any CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE annotation. When THP is
disabled, kernel-doc sees these @-tags referencing enum members that
no longer exist in the enum body and will emit warnings during
"make htmldocs". Could these two lines note the dependency explicitly,
e.g. "(only available when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is enabled)"?
That keeps the documentation accurate under all configs without needing
an ifdef inside the comment block itself.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Boot Memory
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

review.summary: This patch fills in the bootmem.rst documentation stub with a high-level description of the memblock allocator, its transition to the buddy allocator, named reservations, and zone/struct-page initialization. The content is broadly accurate but contains one factual error in the iterator description that will mislead readers.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

I checked the main claims against mm/memblock.c and
include/linux/memblock.h.

> +Memblock tracks physical memory as two arrays of regions: ``memory`` (all
> +usable RAM reported by firmware) and ``reserved`` (memory already allocated
> +or otherwise unavailable).  A free page is one that appears in ``memory``
> +but not in ``reserved``.

Correct.  The struct memblock definition in memblock.h confirms both
arrays and the semantics match for_each_free_mem_range().

> +- **NOMAP**: memory that should not be directly mapped by the kernel
> +  (e.g., firmware-reserved ranges that are usable but not mappable).

The kernel header describes MEMBLOCK_NOMAP as "don't add to kernel
direct mapping and treat as reserved in the memory map".  NOMAP regions
are not usable as RAM; the parenthetical "usable but not mappable"
overstates it.  Could this be rephrased closer to "memory excluded from
the kernel direct map and treated as reserved in the memory map"?

> +- ``for_each_mem_range()`` iterates over free ranges (memory minus
> +  reserved).

This is incorrect.  The actual definition in memblock.h is:

  #define for_each_mem_range(i, p_start, p_end) \
      __for_each_mem_range(i, &memblock.memory, NULL, NUMA_NO_NODE, \
                           MEMBLOCK_HOTPLUG | MEMBLOCK_DRIVER_MANAGED, \
                           p_start, p_end, NULL)

The type_b argument is NULL, so reserved regions are never subtracted.
for_each_mem_range() walks the memory array while skipping NOMAP (and
HOTPLUG under movable_node), nothing more.

The macro that subtracts reserved is for_each_free_mem_range(), which
passes &memblock.reserved as type_b.

> +These iterators handle the subtraction of reserved regions from memory
> +regions internally, presenting the caller with a simple sequence of
> +available ranges.

This paragraph reinforces the error above.  Subtraction of reserved
only happens in for_each_free_mem_range() and its reverse variant, not
in for_each_mem_range().

Could the iterator list be corrected to something like:

  - for_each_mem_range() walks the memory array, skipping NOMAP and
    (with movable_node) HOTPLUG regions; it does not subtract reserved.
  - for_each_free_mem_range() iterates truly free ranges (memory minus
    reserved).
  - for_each_reserved_mem_region() iterates reserved regions directly.
  - for_each_mem_pfn_range() iterates by page frame number.

[ ... ]

> +memblock arrays themselves are returned to the page allocator via
> +``memblock_discard()``

Small note: memblock_discard() only frees arrays that were dynamically
resized past their static initial buffers; if no resize occurred it is a
no-op.  Worth a brief qualifier so readers do not assume the static
buffers are always reclaimed.

No other regressions found.  The memblock_add()/memblock_reserve()
descriptions, the top-down/bottom-up allocation direction, the
memblock_free_all() transition, and the named-reservation and
zone-topology sections all look accurate against the source.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Virtually Contiguous Memory Allocation
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Virtually Contiguous Memory Allocation

review.summary: This patch fills in the vmalloc.rst documentation stub with an overview of vmalloc internals: virtual address management, guard pages, huge page support, lazy TLB flushing, NUMA awareness, and KASAN integration. The content is broadly accurate but contains two factual errors verifiable against mm/vmalloc.c.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Virtually Contiguous Memory Allocation

Cross-referenced against mm/vmalloc.c and include/linux/vmalloc.h.
Two factual inaccuracies found below.

> +Guard Pages
> +-----------
> +
> +By default, each vmalloc area is surrounded by a guard page — an unmapped
> +page that causes an immediate fault if code overruns the allocation.  This
> +costs one page of virtual address space (not physical memory) per
> +allocation.

The word "surrounded" implies guard pages on both sides of the
allocation.  Looking at __get_vm_area_node() in mm/vmalloc.c:

    if (!(flags & VM_NO_GUARD))
        size += PAGE_SIZE;

Only one PAGE_SIZE is added to the virtual range, which means there
is a single trailing guard page (after the allocation), not pages on
both sides.  "surrounded" should be "followed by a guard page" or
"has a trailing guard page".

> +Freeing
> +=======
> +
> +``vfree()`` can be called from any context, including interrupt handlers.

This is not quite right.  vfree() explicitly forbids NMI context:

    BUG_ON(in_nmi());

And vfree_atomic(), which vfree() delegates to when in_interrupt(),
carries the same restriction:

    BUG_ON(in_nmi());

The kernel-doc comment for vfree_atomic() already states "except
NMIs".  This sentence should say something like "vfree() can be
called from any context except NMI, including hard interrupt
handlers."

Everything else checked out:
- vmap_area two-tree description (free_vmap_area_root augmented with
  subtree_max_size, vmap_area_root for busy areas) matches the data
  structure definitions.
- vm_struct fields (addr, size, flags, pages array, caller) match
  include/linux/vmalloc.h.
- vmalloc_huge() wrapper exists in include/linux/vmalloc.h.
- arch_vmap_pmd_supported() / arch_vmap_pud_supported() callbacks
  exist and are used in the huge-page mapping path.
- vm_map_ram / vm_unmap_ram descriptions match their implementations.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim

review.summary: This patch fills in the page_reclaim.rst documentation stub, covering kswapd vs. direct reclaim, scan priority, scan_control, classic and multi-gen LRU, shrinkers, memcg reclaim, and NUMA demotion. All key technical claims were verified against the current source tree.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim

Pure documentation patch; no code changes. Verified the key factual
claims against mm/vmscan.c, mm/swap.c, mm/shrinker.c, and
include/linux/mmzone.h.

> +Reclaim Priority
> +================
> +
> +The reclaim path operates at decreasing priority levels (from
> +``DEF_PRIORITY`` down to 0).  At each level, a larger fraction of the
> +LRU lists is scanned.  At the default priority, only 1/4096th of
> +pages are considered; at priority 0, the entire list is scanned.

DEF_PRIORITY is 12 (include/linux/mmzone.h) and scan_control carries
the comment "Scan (total_size >> priority) pages at once", so 2^12 =
4096 -- the 1/4096th claim is correct.

> +- **kswapd**: a per-node kernel thread that runs in the background
> +  when free pages in any zone drop below the low watermark.  It
> +  reclaims until free pages reach the high watermark, then sleeps.

Verified: the slow-path allocator calls wake_all_kswapds() when
ALLOC_WMARK_LOW fails, and kswapd sleeps in kswapd_try_to_sleep()
only when pgdat_balanced() returns true, which checks
high_wmark_pages() -- both halves of this sentence are correct.

> +Each reclaim invocation is parameterized by a ``struct scan_control``
> +that captures the allocation context: which GFP flags were used, how
> +many pages are needed, which node or memory cgroup to reclaim from,
> +and whether writeback or swap are allowed.

Cross-checked against struct scan_control (mm/vmscan.c): gfp_mask,
nr_to_reclaim, nodemask/target_mem_cgroup, may_writepage, may_swap --
all present and match the description.

> +Besides page cache and anonymous pages, kernel caches (dentries,
> +inodes, and driver-specific caches) are reclaimed through the
> +shrinker interface (``mm/shrinker.c``).  A shrinker registers two
> +callbacks:
> +
> +- ``count_objects()``: report how many objects are reclaimable.
> +- ``scan_objects()``: free up to a requested number of objects.

mm/shrinker.c exists; struct shrinker in include/linux/shrinker.h
declares exactly these two function pointers. Correct.

> +``try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages()`` is the entry point for
> +cgroup-scoped reclaim.

Confirmed in mm/vmscan.c.

No regressions found. Locking, error paths, and refcounts are not
applicable to a documentation-only patch.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Swap
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Swap

review.summary: This patch fills in the swap.rst documentation stub with an overview of swap entries, devices, cluster allocation, the swap cache, zswap, and zero-filled page handling. One factual error was found in the cluster size description.
review.review_inline:
Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Swap

Thanks for filling in this stub.  The overall structure is good and the
high-level descriptions are accurate.  One factual error found:

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/swap.rst b/Documentation/mm/swap.rst

[ ... ]

> +Swap space is allocated in clusters (groups of contiguous slots, typically
> +32 pages).  Each cluster tracks which slots are free and whether it has

The cluster size is not 32 pages.  mm/swap.h defines:

    #ifdef CONFIG_THP_SWAP
    #define SWAPFILE_CLUSTER    HPAGE_PMD_NR   /* 512 on x86-64 */
    #else
    #define SWAPFILE_CLUSTER    256
    #endif

So the cluster size is 256 or 512 pages depending on CONFIG_THP_SWAP,
never 32.  Please correct this to reflect the actual values.

[ ... ]

> +``mm/page_io.c`` maintains a bitmap (``swap_zeromap``) tracking swap slots
> +that contained zero-filled

Minor nit: the bitmap is the zeromap field inside struct swap_info_struct
(include/linux/swap.h).  The functions that read and write it live in
mm/page_io.c, but it is not a standalone object defined there.  Consider
wording it as: "Each swap device tracks zero-filled slots in a bitmap
(the zeromap field of swap_info_struct); mm/page_io.c sets and clears
entries as pages are written out and read back."

Everything else checked out: the swap_extent rbtree description is
correct (rb_root in swap_info_struct), zswap's per-CPU acomp_ctx usage
is confirmed, the LRU-based eviction path is accurate, and the
Documentation/mm/swap-table.rst cross-reference points to a file that
exists in the tree.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem

review.summary: Documentation-only patch filling in the shmfs.rst stub with an overview of tmpfs/shmem internals including page cache integration, swap, huge pages, accounting, and fallocate. Several technical claims do not match the actual kernel implementation.
review.review_inline:
On Mon, Mar 17 2026, Kit Dallege wrote:

> Fill in the shmfs.rst stub created in commit 481cc97349d6
> ("mm,doc: Add new documentation structure") as part of
> the structured memory management documentation following
> Mel Gorman's book outline.

Thank you for filling out this stub.  A few technical details do not
match the implementation in mm/shmem.c and should be corrected before
merge.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst

[ ... ]

> +- ``never``: only base pages (default).
> +- ``always``: attempt huge page allocation for every new page.
> +- ``within_size``: use huge pages only within the file's current size.
> +- ``advise``: use huge pages only for mappings with ``MADV_HUGEPAGE``.

Two issues here.

First, the default is not unconditionally "never".  mm/shmem.c selects
TMPFS_HUGE_DEFAULT based on CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_TMPFS_HUGE_*.
The fallback when no config option selects a value is SHMEM_HUGE_NEVER,
but this varies by build configuration.  Please qualify: "never (unless
overridden by kernel configuration)" or similar.

Second, the within_size description is incomplete.  The code comment
for SHMEM_HUGE_WITHIN_SIZE in mm/shmem.c says:

  "only allocate huge pages if the page will be fully within i_size,
   also respect madvise() hints;"

The implementation in shmem_allowable_huge_orders() confirms this:
huge_shmem_orders_madvise is OR'd into the result mask when VM_HUGEPAGE
is set, even under the within_size policy.  The description should note
that within_size also honours MADV_HUGEPAGE/MADV_NOHUGEPAGE hints.

[ ... ]

> +When a huge page is allocated but only partially used (e.g., a file is
> +smaller than a huge page), memory is wasted.  To mitigate this, tmpfs
> +registers a shrinker that identifies huge pages where the file has been
> +truncated or punched below the huge page boundary, and splits them back
> +into base pages so the unused portion can be reclaimed.

This is inaccurate.  There is no shmem-specific shrinker in mm/shmem.c.
Grepping for "shrinker" and "split_huge_page" in mm/shmem.c returns no
matches.  The mechanism that reclaims partially-used huge pages is the
generic deferred split shrinker (deferred_split_shrinker) registered in
mm/huge_memory.c, which is not shmem-specific.

Please correct this to describe the actual mechanism -- the THP
deferred split infrastructure -- or remove the paragraph if the detail
cannot be verified accurately.

[ ... ]

> +tmpfs supports ``fallocate()`` to preallocate space for a file.
> +Preallocated pages are allocated and inserted into the page cache
> +immediately, guaranteeing that subsequent writes will not fail with
> +``ENOSPC``.
> +
> +``FALLOC_FL_PUNCH_HOLE`` is also supported: it removes pages from a range
> +of the file and returns them to the filesystem's free pool.

shmem_fallocate() also accepts FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE (the mode validation
is `mode & ~(FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE | FALLOC_FL_PUNCH_HOLE)`).  It is
worth mentioning that preallocating with FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE is
supported and does not extend i_size, which is used by memfd consumers
that want to guarantee space without changing the visible file size.

series.title: Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

review.summary: Documentation-only patch adding a cross-referenced note to design.rst and the three DAMON special-purpose module docs (lru_sort, reclaim, stat) explaining that these modules run exclusively and return -EBUSY if another is already active.
review.review_inline:
From: Liew Rui Yan <aethernet65535@gmail.com>

Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

Verified against the kernel source:

- reclaim.c, lru_sort.c, and stat.c all call damon_start() with
  exclusive=true.
- damon_start() in core.c returns -EBUSY when (exclusive &&
  nr_running_ctxs) or (!exclusive && running_exclusive_ctxs),
  which is exactly the behaviour described.
- The RST label damon_design_special_purpose_modules_exclusivity
  in design.rst matches the :ref: targets in all three user docs.

> +.. _damon_design_special_purpose_modules_exclusivity:
> +
> +Note that these modules currently run in an exclusive manner.  If one of those
> +is already running, others will return ``-EBUSY`` upon start requests.

Accurate.

One minor nit: the commit message says the note is added "at the end
of their respective Example sections", but in stat.rst the note lands
inside the `enabled` subsection (stat.rst has no Examples section).
Harmless, but the commit message is slightly imprecise.

Otherwise looks good.

series.title: binfmt_elf: Align eligible read-only PT_LOAD segments to PMD_SIZE for THP
series.author: WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

patches.title: [PATCH v5] binfmt_elf: Align eligible read-only PT_LOAD segments to PMD_SIZE for THP

review.summary: The patch adds a should_align_to_pmd() helper that bumps p_align to PMD_SIZE in maximum_alignment() for read-only PT_LOAD segments whose p_vaddr and p_offset are already PMD_SIZE-aligned and whose file size covers at least one huge page, so that the ET_DYN load_bias is aligned to PMD_SIZE and THP-collapsing becomes possible.
review.review_inline:
WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

binfmt_elf: Align eligible read-only PT_LOAD segments to PMD_SIZE for THP

Verified the alignment logic, load_bias computation, and both ET_DYN
loading paths.  The patch looks correct.

> +static inline bool should_align_to_pmd(const struct elf_phdr *cmd)
> +{
> +	if (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE))
> +		return false;
> +
> +	if (PMD_SIZE > SZ_32M)
> +		return false;
> +
> +	if (!IS_ALIGNED(cmd->p_vaddr | cmd->p_offset, PMD_SIZE))
> +		return false;

The IS_ALIGNED(a | b, n) trick is correct: OR-ing two values produces a
result with no low bits only if both operands individually have no low
bits, so this is equivalent to IS_ALIGNED(p_vaddr, PMD_SIZE) &&
IS_ALIGNED(p_offset, PMD_SIZE) without the extra branch.

> +		if (p_align < PMD_SIZE && should_align_to_pmd(&cmds[i]))
> +			p_align = PMD_SIZE;

maximum_alignment() is called only inside the ET_DYN branch
(else if (elf_ex->e_type == ET_DYN)), so this cannot affect ET_EXEC
binaries whose load addresses are fixed by the linker.

For ET_DYN with PT_INTERP (PIE + dynamic linker), the returned
alignment feeds directly into:

    load_bias &= ~(alignment - 1);

With alignment == PMD_SIZE, load_bias is PMD_SIZE-aligned.  Since
p_vaddr is also PMD_SIZE-aligned, the final address p_vaddr + load_bias
is PMD_SIZE-aligned, matching p_offset, satisfying THP eligibility.

For ET_DYN without PT_INTERP (static PIE / ld.so), the existing
alignment > ELF_MIN_ALIGN probe-and-realign mechanism already handles
any large alignment; PMD_SIZE flows through that path correctly.

The p_filesz (not p_memsz) check is right: THP eligibility depends on
the file-backed range, not the BSS tail.  The PF_W exclusion is correct
since writable segments are not eligible for file-backed PMD collapsing.

No new allocations, error paths, or locking changes.

series.title: mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue
series.author: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH v3] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

review.summary: The patch fixes NUMA migration of THPs by re-queueing the destination folio on the deferred split list when the source was on it, since __folio_migrate_mapping() unqueues the source but never re-queues the destination. The state (partially_mapped or just underused) is recorded before move_to_new_folio() removes the source and then forwarded to deferred_split_folio() on the destination.
review.review_inline:
Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

Verified the following:

> +	if (folio_order(src) > 1 &&
> +	    !data_race(list_empty(&src->_deferred_list))) {
> +		src_deferred_split = true;
> +		src_partially_mapped = folio_test_partially_mapped(src);
> +	}

The data_race() annotation here mirrors the identical idiom in the
folio_unqueue_deferred_split() inline wrapper in mm/internal.h,
which also reads _deferred_list racily as a fast-path guard before
calling __folio_unqueue_deferred_split().  Both the _deferred_list
membership and PG_partially_mapped are read without the
split_queue_lock, but the race consequences are bounded: a false
positive re-queues dst unnecessarily (harmless, shrinker will try
to split or skip), and a false negative leaves dst off the queue in
a tiny window no worse than the pre-existing bug.  The
folio_order > 1 guard matches deferred_split_folio()'s own
early-return for order <= 1.

The removed folio_test_large_rmappable() guard (v2->v3) is correct:
only large_rmappable folios are ever added to the deferred list, so
a non-empty _deferred_list already implies rmappability.

> +	if (src_deferred_split)
> +		deferred_split_folio(dst, src_partially_mapped);

Placement is correct: success path only, after folio_add_lru(dst)
and remove_migration_ptes(), before dst is unlocked.
folio_migrate_flags() does not copy PG_partially_mapped to dst, so
deferred_split_folio() is the right place to set it on dst.
deferred_split_folio() guards against double-queueing via its own
list_empty() check under split_queue_lock, and dst is freshly
allocated so VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO(folio_test_partially_mapped(dst))
cannot fire on the partially_mapped=false arm.

Looks good to me.

series.title: Refactor reserved memory regions handling code
series.author: Marek Szyprowski <m.szyprowski@samsung.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/7] Refactor reserved memory regions handling code
[PATCH 1/7] of: reserved_mem: remove fdt node from the structure
[PATCH 2/7] of: reserved_mem: use -ENODEV instead of -ENOENT
[PATCH 3/7] of: reserved_mem: switch to ops based OF_DECLARE()
[PATCH 4/7] of: reserved_mem: replace CMA quirks by generic methods
[PATCH 5/7] of: reserved_mem: rearrange code a bit
[PATCH 6/7] of: reserved_mem: clarify fdt_scan_reserved_mem*() functions
[PATCH 7/7] of: reserved_mem: rework fdt_init_reserved_mem_node()

review.summary: This 7-patch series refactors the reserved memory framework to remove fdt_node from struct reserved_mem, unify init callbacks into ops, and replace CMA-specific quirks with generic node_validate/node_fixup callbacks. Two concrete bugs were found: a wrong variable (node vs child) in fdt_scan_reserved_mem_late() silently breaking the cma= cmdline override, and a typo in the !CONFIG_OF_RESERVED_MEM stub macro that would cause build failures.
review.review_inline:
Author: Marek Szyprowski <m.szyprowski@samsung.com>

[PATCH 3/7] of: reserved_mem: switch to ops based OF_DECLARE()

The stub macro in the #else branch (!CONFIG_OF_RESERVED_MEM) has a
typo: the 4th argument passed to _OF_DECLARE_STUB is the literal token
'init', but the macro parameter was renamed to 'ops'.

> -#define RESERVEDMEM_OF_DECLARE(name, compat, init)			\
> -	_OF_DECLARE_STUB(reservedmem, name, compat, init, reservedmem_of_init_fn)
> +#define RESERVEDMEM_OF_DECLARE(name, compat, ops)			\
> +	_OF_DECLARE_STUB(reservedmem, name, compat, init, void *)

'init' on the new line is not the macro parameter -- the parameter is
'ops'.  _OF_DECLARE_STUB expands its fn argument into the expression
'(fn == (fn_type)NULL) ? fn : fn' for compile-time type checking, so
'init' must be a visible identifier at every call site, or the
translation unit fails to compile.

drivers/memory/tegra/tegra210-emc-table.c uses RESERVEDMEM_OF_DECLARE
without any surrounding #ifdef CONFIG_OF_RESERVED_MEM guard, so a
configuration with CONFIG_ARCH_TEGRA but without CONFIG_OF_RESERVED_MEM
would fail to build.

Should be:

	_OF_DECLARE_STUB(reservedmem, name, compat, ops, void *)

---

[PATCH 4/7] of: reserved_mem: replace CMA quirks by generic methods

> 	fdt_for_each_subnode(child, fdt, node) {
>  		const char *uname;
> -		bool default_cma = of_get_flat_dt_prop(child,
> -				"linux,cma-default", NULL);
>  		u64 b, s;
> +		int ret;
>
>  		if (!of_fdt_device_is_available(fdt, child))
>  			continue;
> -		if (default_cma && cma_skip_dt_default_reserved_mem())
> -			continue;
>
>  		if (!of_flat_dt_get_addr_size(child, "reg", &b, &s))
>  			continue;
>
> +		ret = fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node(node, NULL);
> +		if (ret && ret != -ENODEV)
> +			continue;

The call passes 'node' (the /reserved-memory parent) instead of 'child'
(the region being iterated).  'node' carries no 'compatible' property
matching any __reservedmem_of_table entry, so
fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node() always returns -ENODEV here, and
'ret && ret != -ENODEV' is always false.  The skip never fires.

The removed code read 'linux,cma-default' from 'child' and skipped it
when cma_skip_dt_default_reserved_mem() was true (i.e. when the user
passed 'cma=' on the kernel command line).  With this bug,
fdt_scan_reserved_mem_late() (renamed in patch 6/7) always enqueues
the DT default CMA region even when 'cma=' overrides it, silently
breaking the behaviour that commit 0fd17e598333 was meant to preserve.

The equivalent calls in __reserved_mem_reserve_reg() and
__reserved_mem_alloc_size() both correctly pass the child/region node;
only this one site is wrong.

Should be:

	ret = fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node(child, NULL);

series.title: rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS
series.author: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS

review.summary: Wraps BINDER_VM_OPS in AssertSync<vm_operations_struct> to fix a Rust compiler Sync error introduced when the userfaultfd patch adds a non-function pointer field to vm_operations_struct. This reuses the existing AssertSync pattern already applied to rust_binder_fops.
review.review_inline:
Author: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>

rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS

Verified the following:

> -static BINDER_VM_OPS: bindings::vm_operations_struct = pin_init::zeroed();
> +static BINDER_VM_OPS: AssertSync<bindings::vm_operations_struct> = AssertSync(pin_init::zeroed());

AssertSync<T> is #[repr(transparent)], so &BINDER_VM_OPS.0 is at the
same address as the outer object.  The pointer identity check in
check_vma and the assignment in register_with_vma both use
&BINDER_VM_OPS.0, so they still agree with each other.

> +// SAFETY: Used only to insert C bindings types into globals, which is safe.
>  unsafe impl<T> Sync for AssertSync<T> {}

BINDER_VM_OPS is a zero-initialized static that is never mutated after
module load, so asserting Sync for it is sound.  The updated SAFETY
comment accurately covers both the file_operations and
vm_operations_struct uses.

Looks good to me.

series.title: slub: clarify kmem_cache_refill_sheaf() failure behavior
series.author: Hao Li <hao.li@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH] slub: clarify kmem_cache_refill_sheaf() failure behavior

review.summary: Documentation-only patch that rewrites the comment for kmem_cache_refill_sheaf() to distinguish its two failure modes more precisely, and softens "always" to "usually" for the refill-to-capacity claim.
review.review_inline:
Hao Li <hao.li@linux.dev>

slub: clarify kmem_cache_refill_sheaf() failure behavior

Comment-only change; no functional delta. I traced both failure paths
to verify the new description is accurate.

> +++ b/mm/slub.c

[ ... ]

> + *     1. If the requested size fits within the current sheaf's capacity, the
> + *     refill is done in place. In that case, a failed refill may still fill
> + *     some additional objects into the existing sheaf before returning -ENOMEM.

Verified: the in-place path goes through __prefill_sheaf_pfmemalloc()
-> refill_sheaf(), which does 'sheaf->size += filled' before checking
'filled < to_fill', so partial progress is committed to the sheaf
before -ENOMEM is returned. Accurate.

> + *     2. If the requested size exceeds the current sheaf's capacity, a new
> + *     larger sheaf may be allocated to replace the original one. In that case,
> + *     if allocation of the replacement sheaf fails, the original sheaf is left
> + *     unchanged.

Verified: the oversize path calls kmem_cache_prefill_sheaf() and
returns -ENOMEM immediately on failure, before kmem_cache_return_sheaf()
or the *sheafp update, so the caller's sheaf pointer is untouched.
Accurate.

> - * In practice we always refill to full sheaf's capacity.
> + * In practice we usually refill to the sheaf's full capacity.

'usually' is appropriate: the API accepts an arbitrary size argument,
even if primary callers pass s->sheaf_capacity.

Looks good to me.

series.title: mm/damon: support addr_unit on default monitoring targets for modules
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/5] mm/damon: support addr_unit on default monitoring targets for modules
[PATCH 1/5] mm/damon/core: fix wrong end address assignment on walk_system_ram()
[PATCH 2/5] mm/damon/core: support addr_unit on damon_find_biggest_system_ram()
[PATCH 3/5] mm/damon/core: receive addr_unit on damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default()
[PATCH 4/5] mm/damon/reclaim: respect addr_unit on default monitoring region setup
[PATCH 5/5] mm/damon/lru_sort: respect addr_unit on default monitoring region setup

review.summary: This series extends DAMON's addr_unit support to the default monitoring target path used by DAMON_RECLAIM and DAMON_LRU_SORT, fixing a minor end-address off-by-one bug along the way. Patch 3 contains a concrete bug: addr_unit is passed as the nr_ranges argument to damon_set_regions(), causing out-of-bounds access when addr_unit > 1.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon: support addr_unit on default monitoring targets for modules

Patches 1, 2, 4, and 5 look correct.  There is one concrete bug in
patch 3 and one minor logic issue in patch 2 worth considering.

--- [PATCH 2/5] mm/damon/core: support addr_unit on damon_find_biggest_system_ram() ---

> +	walk_system_ram_res(0, -1, &res, walk_system_ram);
> +	if (res.end < res.start)
> +		return false;

The previous code used 'struct damon_addr_range arg = {}' with the
check 'arg.end <= arg.start', which correctly returned false when
no System RAM resource was found (both fields zero after
zero-initialization).  The new check 'res.end < res.start' does not
catch that case: a zero-initialized 'struct resource' has start=0
and end=0, so '0 < 0' is false and the function falls through to
compute *start=0, *end=1 (or 0 when addr_unit>1), returning true
when it should return false.

In practice a running kernel always has at least one System RAM
resource so this path cannot be reached, but the logic is
inconsistent with the old behaviour.  A check such as tracking
whether walk_system_ram was ever invoked would restore the original
semantics.

--- [PATCH 3/5] mm/damon/core: receive addr_unit on damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default() ---

> -	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);

This is a bug.  The third parameter of damon_set_regions() is
nr_ranges -- the number of elements in the 'ranges' array:

    int damon_set_regions(struct damon_target *t,
            struct damon_addr_range *ranges,
            unsigned int nr_ranges,
            unsigned long min_region_sz)

'addr_range' is a single 'struct damon_addr_range' declared on the
stack, not an array of addr_unit elements.  When addr_unit > 1,
passing it as nr_ranges causes damon_set_regions() to iterate and
dereference 'ranges[1]' through 'ranges[addr_unit-1]' inside both
the region-removal loop and the region-addition loop, reading beyond
the single valid element -- a stack out-of-bounds access.

The addr_unit conversion is already applied correctly upstream in
damon_find_biggest_system_ram(), where res.start and res.end+1 are
each divided by addr_unit via damon_res_to_core_addr() before being
stored in *start and *end.  By the time damon_set_regions() is
called, addr_range already holds the correctly scaled core
addresses.  The nr_ranges argument should remain 1:

    return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

Patches 4 and 5 (removing the 'if (!monitor_region_start &&
!monitor_region_end) addr_unit = 1' resets in reclaim.c and
lru_sort.c) are correct and consistent with the updated behaviour
of damon_find_biggest_system_ram() now that it honours addr_unit.

series.title: selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled
series.author: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 1/7] selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled
[PATCH v3 2/7] selftests/cgroup: avoid OOM in test_swapin_nozswap
[PATCH v3 3/7] selftests/cgroup: use runtime page size for zswpin check
[PATCH v3 4/7] selftests/cgroup: rename PAGE_SIZE to BUF_SIZE in cgroup_util
[PATCH v3 5/7] selftests/cgroup: replace hardcoded page size values in test_zswap
[PATCH v3 6/7] selftest/cgroup: fix zswap test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink on large pagesize system
[PATCH v3 7/7] selftest/cgroup: fix zswap attempt_writeback() on 64K pagesize system

review.summary: This 7-patch series fixes zswap cgroup selftests to skip when zswap is globally disabled at runtime, avoids OOM in test_swapin_nozswap, and makes page-size calculations runtime-aware so the tests pass on 64K-page systems (arm64/ppc64le).
review.review_inline:
Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled (v3 series)

General approach looks correct. A few things worth checking:

--- [PATCH v3 1/7] selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled ---

> +static void check_zswap_enabled(void)
> +{
> +	char value[2];
> +
> +	if (access("/sys/module/zswap", F_OK))
> +		ksft_exit_skip("zswap isn't configured\n");
> +
> +	if (read_text("/sys/module/zswap/parameters/enabled", value,
> +					sizeof(value)) <= 0)
> +		ksft_exit_fail_msg("Failed to read "
> +			"/sys/module/zswap/parameters/enabled\n");
> +
> +	if (value[0] == 'N')
> +		ksft_exit_skip("zswap is disabled (hint: echo 1 > "
> +			"/sys/module/zswap/parameters/enabled)\n");
> +}

Verified: read_text() with max_len=2 reads at most 1 byte, which is
enough for the 'Y'/'N' that kernel module bool params produce via
sysfs. The <= 0 check also catches a successful but empty read.
Looks correct.

--- [PATCH v3 2/7] selftests/cgroup: avoid OOM in test_swapin_nozswap ---

> +	min_swap = control_allocation_size / 4;
> +	snprintf(mem_max_buf, sizeof(mem_max_buf), "%zu",
> +		control_allocation_size * 3/4);

[ ... ]

> +	if (swap_peak < min_swap) {
> +		ksft_print_msg("at least %ldMB of memory should be swapped out\n",
> +				min_swap / MB(1));

On a 4K page system: control_allocation_size = 4096 * 512 = 2MB, so
min_swap = 512KB, and min_swap / MB(1) = 0 due to integer division.
The error message would read "at least 0MB of memory should be swapped
out" which is misleading. Consider printing in KB instead:

    ksft_print_msg("at least %ldKB of memory should be swapped out\n",
                    min_swap / 1024);

--- [PATCH v3 4/7] selftests/cgroup: rename PAGE_SIZE to BUF_SIZE in cgroup_util ---

> -#ifndef PAGE_SIZE
> -#define PAGE_SIZE 4096
> +#ifndef BUF_SIZE
> +#define BUF_SIZE 4096
>  #endif

After this rename, cgroup_util.h no longer defines PAGE_SIZE.
However, allocate_random_and_wait() in test_zswap.c still references
PAGE_SIZE directly:

    for (size_t i = 0; i < size; i += PAGE_SIZE)
        mem[i] = mem[i];

Neither patch 4 nor patch 5 touches this occurrence. Whether this
compiles depends on whether the platform's system headers happen to
define PAGE_SIZE. test_zswap.c does not include <sys/param.h>
explicitly, making this fragile. Could you fold this reference into
the patch 5 hunk that replaces other hardcoded page-size values with
the global pagesize variable?

--- [PATCH v3 5/7] selftests/cgroup: replace hardcoded page size values in test_zswap ---

> +static size_t pagesize;

[ ... ]

> +	pagesize = sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE);

Verified: pagesize is initialized in main() before any cg_run() call.
Forked children inherit the global via fork(), so all callbacks
(allocate_and_read_bytes, allocate_bytes, attempt_writeback,
no_kmem_bypass_child) see the correct value. The char buf[pagesize]
VLA in attempt_writeback was already a VLA when pagesize was a local
variable; no new concern there.

--- [PATCH v3 7/7] selftest/cgroup: fix zswap attempt_writeback() on 64K pagesize system ---

> -	size_t memsize = MB(4);
> +	size_t memsize = pagesize * 1024;

Verified that test_zswap_writeback() sets no memory.max on the test
cgroup, so the 64MB allocation on 64K systems does not risk OOM.
The intent of keeping 1024 pages of pressure regardless of page size
is sound.

series.title: mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
series.author: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 00/11] mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
[PATCH 01/11] mm/zsmalloc: Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy
[PATCH 02/11] mm/zsmalloc: Make all obj_idx unsigned ints
[PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
[PATCH 04/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce objcgs pointer in struct zspage
[PATCH 05/11] mm/zsmalloc: Store obj_cgroup pointer in zspage
[PATCH 06/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Redirect zswap_entry->objcg to zspage
[PATCH 07/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Handle objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc
[PATCH 08/11] mm/memcontrol: Track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes
[PATCH 09/11] mm/vmstat, memcontrol: Track ZSWAP_B, ZSWAPPED_B per-memcg-lruvec
[PATCH 10/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle single object charge migration in migrate_zspage
[PATCH 11/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle charge migration in zpdesc substitution

review.summary: This series moves per-memcg zswap accounting from the consumer layer (zswap/zram) into zsmalloc by adding a per-zspage array of obj_cgroup pointers, redirecting zswap_entry to look up the objcg from the zspage, and finally migrating charging and lifetime management entirely into zsmalloc. The overall design is sound and the locking is consistent with existing zsmalloc patterns.
review.review_inline:
Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting

I verified: the locking pattern in zs_lookup_objcg (read_lock
pool->lock, acquire zspage_read_lock, then release pool->lock) is
consistent with zs_obj_write, zs_obj_read_begin, and zs_obj_read_sg_begin,
so migration cannot move pages under a reader.  alloc_zspage's goto-err
path in patch 4 correctly frees objcgs before cache_free_zspage.
migrate_zspage in patch 5 correctly clears the source slot via
zs_migrate_objcg before obj_free runs, so patch 7's uncharge-on-free
does not fire for migrated objects.  A few things to discuss:

> diff --git a/mm/zsmalloc.c b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> +struct obj_cgroup *zs_lookup_objcg(struct zs_pool *pool,
> +				     unsigned long handle)
> +{
> +	read_lock(&pool->lock);
> +	obj = handle_to_obj(handle);
> +	obj_to_location(obj, &zpdesc, &obj_idx);
> +	zspage = get_zspage(zpdesc);
> +	zspage_read_lock(zspage);
> +	read_unlock(&pool->lock);
> +	objcg = zspage->objcgs[obj_idx];
> +	zspage_read_unlock(zspage);
> +	return objcg;
> +}

The function is declared in zsmalloc.h as part of the public API, but
the diff does not show EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(zs_lookup_objcg).  Every other
zs_* public function in this file has one.  CONFIG_ZSWAP is bool so
there is no immediate link failure, but zsmalloc is used by module
consumers (zram) and the convention should be consistent.  Is the
export intentionally absent?

> diff --git a/mm/zswap.c b/mm/zswap.c

[ ... ]

> +	objcg = zs_lookup_objcg(entry->pool->zs_pool, entry->handle);
> +	if (objcg)
> +		count_objcg_events(objcg, ZSWPWB, 1);
>  	zswap_entry_free(entry);

zswap_entry_free calls zs_lookup_objcg again for the same handle to
pass the objcg into zswap_lru_del.  That is two round trips through
read_lock(pool->lock) and zspage_read_lock for the same entry in the
writeback path.  Could the objcg resolved here be threaded into
zswap_entry_free (or a __zswap_entry_free variant that accepts a
pre-resolved objcg) to avoid the redundant lookup?  The same pattern
appears in zswap_load, where the ZSWPIN event is counted using
entry->objcg (patch 6 converts this to a zs_lookup_objcg call) and
then zswap_entry_free is called immediately after, triggering a
second lookup.

> diff --git a/mm/zsmalloc.c b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> +	if (zspage->pool->memcg_aware)
> +		zspage->objcgs[f_objidx] = NULL;

This is in obj_free (patch 5).  In the migrate_zspage path,
zs_migrate_objcg already sets src_zspage->objcgs[s_idx] = NULL before
obj_free is called on the source slot, so this write is redundant
during migration.  It is harmless, but a comment noting that the slot
is already NULL in the migration caller, and that the NULL here is
what prevents patch 7's uncharge-on-free from firing for the moved
object, would help future readers.

[ patch 7: obj_cgroup charging and lifetime move to zsmalloc ]

Patch 7 changes the charge unit from entry->length (actual compressed
bytes) to class->size (the slot size).  The commit message calls this
out as intentional -- the slot is exclusively consumed by the object
regardless of how much of it is used.  That said, class->size can be
noticeably larger than entry->length due to size-class rounding, which
inflates MEMCG_ZSWAP_B compared to the pre-series value and could push
a cgroup over its zswap limit sooner on workloads with good compression
ratios.  Could you quantify the worst-case inflation in the commit
message, or at minimum spell out the new semantic explicitly so that
users monitoring per-cgroup zswap stats know what changed?

> diff --git a/include/linux/zsmalloc.h b/include/linux/zsmalloc.h
> +struct obj_cgroup *zs_lookup_objcg(struct zs_pool *pool,
> +				    unsigned long handle);

The function returns a bare pointer with no reference taken.  Its
safety depends on the caller holding an external reference to the
objcg -- in patches 5+6 via the obj_cgroup_get in zswap_store_page,
and after patch 7 via zsmalloc's internal get in zs_obj_write.  A
brief kerneldoc comment stating that the pointer is only valid while
the caller's reference is live (i.e. before zs_free is called for the
same handle) would prevent a future caller from accidentally using the
return value after zs_free drops the last ref.

series.title: kho: add support for deferred struct page init
series.author: Michal Clapinski <mclapinski@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v6 0/2] kho: add support for deferred struct page init
[PATCH v6 1/2] kho: fix deferred init of kho scratch
[PATCH v6 2/2] kho: make preserved pages compatible with deferred struct page init

review.summary: This two-patch series makes KHO compatible with CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT by initializing kho scratch and preserved struct pages early (before any code writes metadata to them) and guarding the deferred-init paths from re-initializing or clobbering the migratetype of pages that KHO already set up.

series.title: Demotion cleanup and fixes
series.author: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/4] Demotion cleanup and fixes
[PATCH 1/4] mm: Move demotion related functions in memory-tiers.c
[PATCH 2/4] mm: Rename node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit
[PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE
[PATCH 4/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by preserving initial gfp reclaim policy

review.summary: Four-patch series that moves demotion helpers into memory-tiers.c, renames node_get_allowed_targets(), and fixes two GFP flag bugs (wrong order of GFP_TRANSHUGE vs ~__GFP_RECLAIM, and htlb_modify_alloc_mask discarding the caller's reclaim policy). Patches 2-4 are correct; patch 1 introduces a regression in alloc_demote_folio that was already fixed in vmscan.c.

series.title: lib/maple_tree: fix potential NULL dereference in mas_pop_node()
series.author: Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] lib/maple_tree: fix potential NULL dereference in mas_pop_node()

review.summary: The patch adds a NULL check after kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf() in mas_pop_node() to prevent a NULL dereference in the subsequent memset(). The crash path is real, but the root cause diagnosis and completeness of the fix both need discussion.
review.review_inline:
Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

lib/maple_tree: fix potential NULL dereference in mas_pop_node()

The NULL dereference at memset() is real -- if the sheaf is empty,
kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf() returns NULL and we crash.  But there
are a few things worth discussing before this lands.

> If kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf() returns NULL (possible under
> GFP_NOWAIT pressure), mas_pop_node() falls through to the out label
> and dereferences the NULL pointer in memset(ret, 0, sizeof(*ret)).

The commit message attributes the NULL return to GFP_NOWAIT pressure,
but that does not match the implementation.  Looking at
kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf_noprof() in mm/slub.c:

    if (sheaf->size == 0)
        goto out;
    ret = kfence_alloc(s, s->object_size, gfp);
    if (likely(!ret))
        ret = sheaf->objects[--sheaf->size];
    slab_post_alloc_hook(s, NULL, gfp | __GFP_NOFAIL, ...);

The sheaf objects are pre-allocated by mas_alloc_nodes().  GFP_NOWAIT
only affects kfence_alloc() (which returns NULL for normal objects in
the common case) and slab_post_alloc_hook() (which forces __GFP_NOFAIL
for memcg charging).  The function returns NULL exclusively because
sheaf->size == 0, i.e., the sheaf is empty -- not because of memory
pressure at the point of this call.

An empty sheaf here means either mas_alloc_nodes() was not called
before mas_pop_node(), or more nodes were popped than were requested
via node_request.  That is a programming error, analogous to the
existing WARN_ON_ONCE(!mas->sheaf) guard above it.  Dropping
WARN_ON_ONCE in v2 removes the only diagnostic signal for that class
of bug.

> diff --git a/lib/maple_tree.c b/lib/maple_tree.c
> --- a/lib/maple_tree.c
> +++ b/lib/maple_tree.c

[ ... ]

>  	ret = kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf(maple_node_cache, GFP_NOWAIT, mas->sheaf);
> +	if (!ret)
> +		return NULL;
>
>  out:
>  	memset(ret, 0, sizeof(*ret));

The fix prevents the crash inside mas_pop_node() itself, but every
caller dereferences the return value without a NULL check.  For
example, mas_root_expand():

    node = mas_pop_node(mas);
    pivots = ma_pivots(node, type);   /* NULL deref moves here */
    node->parent = ...;

The same pattern appears in mas_wr_node_copy(), the 3-way split setup
loop, and mas_dup_build().  Returning NULL here just moves the crash
site, it does not make the error recoverable.

Given that the callers already cannot handle NULL (the existing
WARN_ON_ONCE + return NULL path for !mas->sheaf also results in a
subsequent crash at the call site), would it make more sense to
reinstate WARN_ON_ONCE here so the empty-sheaf case is at least
diagnosed before the inevitable crash?  Silently returning NULL on a
programming-error misuse of the sheaf API makes it significantly
harder to find the root cause in the field.

Could you also double-check whether there is a legitimate non-error
path that can reach kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf() with an empty
sheaf after a successful mas_alloc_nodes() call?  If such a path
exists, the callers need NULL handling too; if it does not, the
commit message framing needs to be updated.

series.title: mm/mempolicy: add sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth
series.author: YeeLi <seven.yi.lee@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/mempolicy: add sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth

review.summary: This patch adds an optional CONFIG_NUMA_BW_MANUAL_OVERRIDE sysfs interface under weighted_interleave/ to let userspace override per-node bandwidth values fed into mempolicy_set_node_perf(). Several correctness bugs are present in the new code.
review.review_inline:
From: <reviewer>

mm/mempolicy: add sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth

Thanks for the patch.  A few issues found by cross-referencing the new
code against the existing sysfs_wi_node_delete() pattern and the
wi_state_lock comment at the node_bw_table declaration.

> +static ssize_t bw_node_show(struct kobject *kobj,
> +			    struct kobj_attribute *attr,
> +			    char *buf)
> +{
> +	/*A Node without CDAT or HMAT*/
> +	if (!node_bw_table)
> +		return sprintf(buf, "N/A\n");
> +
> +	if (!node_bw_table[node_attr->nid])
> +		return sprintf(buf, "0\n");
> +
> +	return sprintf(buf, "%u(MB/s)\n", node_bw_table[node_attr->nid]);
> +}

Two problems here.

First, node_bw_table is protected by wi_state_lock per its declaration
comment:

  /* wi_state_lock protects both wi_state and node_bw_table. */

mempolicy_set_node_perf() assigns a fresh array to node_bw_table under
wi_state_lock, then frees the old one after dropping the lock (the
kfree(old_bw) at the bottom of that function runs unlocked).
bw_node_show() reads node_bw_table without holding wi_state_lock, so a
concurrent mempolicy_set_node_perf() can free the old table between the
!node_bw_table check and the node_bw_table[node_attr->nid] dereference.
This is a use-after-free.  The show handler needs to acquire
wi_state_lock (or snapshot the value under the lock) before
dereferencing node_bw_table.

Second, sysfs show callbacks must use sysfs_emit() instead of
sprintf().  sprintf() has no bounds check against the PAGE_SIZE sysfs
buffer.  Every other show function in this file uses sysfs_emit().

> +static ssize_t bw_node_store(struct kobject *kobj,
> +			     struct kobj_attribute *attr,
> +			     const char *buf, size_t count)
> +{
[ ... ]
> +	ret = kstrtoul(buf, 0, &val);
> +
> +	coords.read_bandwidth = val;
> +	coords.write_bandwidth = val;
> +
> +	if (ret)
> +		return ret;

coords is populated from val before the kstrtoul() return value is
checked.  If kstrtoul() fails, val is indeterminate.  The assignments
to coords.read_bandwidth and coords.write_bandwidth should move to
after the 'if (ret)' early-return check.

> +static void sysfs_bw_node_delete(int nid)
> +{
> +	mutex_lock(&wi_group->kobj_lock);
> +	attr = wi_group->bw_attrs[nid];
> +
> +	if (attr) {
> +		sysfs_remove_file(&wi_group->wi_kobj, &attr->kobj_attr.attr);
> +		kfree(attr->kobj_attr.attr.name);
> +		kfree(attr);
> +		wi_group->nattrs[nid] = NULL;
> +	}
> +	mutex_unlock(&wi_group->kobj_lock);
> +}

Two problems here.

First, the wrong slot is cleared on delete.  The function reads
wi_group->bw_attrs[nid] but then clears wi_group->nattrs[nid].  After
the delete, bw_attrs[nid] still holds the freed pointer.  A subsequent
sysfs_bw_node_add() for the same nid finds bw_attrs[nid] non-NULL and
returns -EEXIST without creating the file.  A subsequent
sysfs_bw_node_delete() passes the stale pointer to sysfs_remove_file()
and kfree().  The assignment should be:

    wi_group->bw_attrs[nid] = NULL;

Second, the locking pattern is inconsistent with sysfs_wi_node_delete().
That function nulls nattrs[nid] under the lock, drops the lock, and
only then calls sysfs_remove_file().  sysfs_remove_file() may block
waiting for an in-progress show/store callback to complete; holding
kobj_lock across that wait diverges from the established pattern.
Please follow sysfs_wi_node_delete(): null bw_attrs[nid] under the
lock, unlock, then call sysfs_remove_file() and kfree().

series.title: mm/damon: Support hot application detections
series.author: <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH v2 0/4] mm/damon: Support hot application detections
[RFC PATCH v2 1/4] mm/damon: Generic context creation for modules
[RFC PATCH v2 2/4] mm/damon: Support for synchrounous huge pages collapse
[RFC PATCH v2 3/4] mm/damon: New module with hot application detection
[RFC PATCH v2 4/4] DAMON_HOT_HUGEPAGE documentation

review.summary: This four-patch RFC series introduces DAMON_HOT_HUGEPAGE, a new kernel module that monitors user-specified PIDs via virtual-address DAMON, detects hot regions, and collapses them synchronously into huge pages via a new DAMOS_COLLAPSE action backed by MADV_COLLAPSE. Patch 1 is a clean refactoring; patches 2-3 have a mutex initialization bug, a Kconfig gap, watermark/documentation mismatches, and a stale docs section describing removed v1 behavior.
review.review_inline:
Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

mm/damon: Support hot application detections [RFC PATCH v2 0/4]

[ Patch 1/4 - modules-common refactoring ]

Patch 1 is a clean refactor.  Both existing callers in reclaim.c and
lru_sort.c pass DAMON_OPS_PADDR, so behavior is unchanged.  The error
path (damon_destroy_ctx on damon_select_ops failure) is preserved
identically.  No issues.

[ Patch 2/4 - DAMOS_COLLAPSE action ]

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
> @@ -142,6 +142,7 @@ enum damos_action {
>  	DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT,
>  	DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD,
> +	DAMOS_COLLAPSE,
>  	DAMOS_STAT,		/* Do nothing but only record the stat */
>  	NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS,
>  };

All kernel-internal accesses use the enum symbol, so shifting DAMOS_STAT
from 9 to 10 is fine.  However, every other damos_action value is
registered in damos_sysfs_action_names[] in sysfs-schemes.c so users
can select it via the sysfs action knob.  DAMOS_COLLAPSE is not added
there.  Is the omission intentional?  If DAMOS_COLLAPSE is meant to be
a general-purpose action (like DAMOS_HUGEPAGE) and not purely
module-private, it should get an entry alongside the others.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/vaddr.c b/mm/damon/vaddr.c
> +	case DAMOS_COLLAPSE:
> +		madv_action = MADV_COLLAPSE;
> +		break;

This routes to damos_madvise() -> do_madvise(..., MADV_COLLAPSE).
That path is gated in madvise.c:

    #ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
    case MADV_HUGEPAGE:
    case MADV_NOHUGEPAGE:
    case MADV_COLLAPSE:
    #endif

so madvise_behavior_valid() rejects MADV_COLLAPSE when
CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is not set, and damos_madvise() silently
returns 0.  The new Kconfig entry only says:

> +config DAMON_HOT_HUGEPAGE
> +	bool "Build DAMON-based collapse of hot regions (DAMON_HOT_HUGEPAGE)"
> +	depends on DAMON_VADDR

Should this also say "depends on TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE"?  Without that
dependency the module builds and runs on a THP-less kernel, but
DAMOS_COLLAPSE never applies anything and the user gets no indication
of why.

[ Patch 3/4 - hugepage.c new module ]

> +struct mutex enable_disable_lock;

This global mutex is zero-initialized (BSS), not properly initialized.
DEFINE_MUTEX() (which expands to __MUTEX_INITIALIZER including
LIST_HEAD_INIT on wait_list) is required for global mutexes.  A
BSS-zero-initialized wait_list has .next = .prev = NULL instead of
pointing to the list head itself.  If mutex_lock() ever blocks because
two threads race on enable/disable, __mutex_lock_slowpath() calls
list_add() on that NULL-pointer list_head and panics.  Please use
DEFINE_MUTEX(enable_disable_lock).

> +static struct damos_watermarks damon_hugepage_wmarks = {
> +	.metric = DAMOS_WMARK_FREE_MEM_RATE,
> +	.interval = 5000000, /* 5 seconds */
> +	.high = 900, /* 90 percent */
> +	.mid = 800, /* 80 percent */
> +	.low = 50, /* 5 percent */
> +};

The documentation for this module (patch 4) says the defaults are:

    wmarks_high: "200 (20%) by default"
    wmarks_mid:  "150 (15%) by default"

The code and docs disagree on the watermark defaults.  Which is
correct?  With high=900 the scheme is inactive only when free memory
exceeds 90%, meaning it runs on nearly any loaded system.  With the
documented high=200 it would be suppressed on lightly-loaded systems
with >20% free memory, which seems wrong for a THP-promotion use case.
