The code value (900) is probably closer to the right intent, but the
documentation must be updated to match.

> +static struct damos_quota damon_hugepage_quota = {
> +	/* use up to 10 ms time, reclaim up to 128 MiB per 1 sec by default */
> +	.ms = 10,
> +	.sz = 0,

The comment says "reclaim up to 128 MiB" but .sz = 0 (no size quota)
and this is a collapse module, not reclaim.  This is a copy-paste from
damon_reclaim_quota that was not updated.

[ Patch 4/4 - Documentation ]

The "How It Works?" section describes the v1 behavior that was removed
based on SeongJae Park's feedback:

    "The monitor thread will calculate the moving average of the sum of
     utimes of all threads ... Then, pick the top three and launch a
     damon process to monitor the hot regions in those tasks."
    "If a task turns cold, the monitor thread will detect it (it will
     not fall in the top three hot tasks), and stop the damon thread."

But v2 removes automatic task selection entirely -- the user provides
PIDs via the monitored_pids parameter.  The "How It Works?" section
needs to be rewritten to describe the actual v2 behavior.

Additionally, the Interface section references the module as
"damon_dynamic_hotpages" (old name) in the sysfs path:

    /sys/module/damon_dynamic_hotpages/parameters/<parameter>

but the module file is now mm/damon/hugepage.c.  Please verify the
correct module name and update the path accordingly.

series.title: lib/idr: Fixes for infinite loop and memory leak
series.author: Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 0/2] lib/idr: Fixes for infinite loop and memory leak
[PATCH v3 1/2] lib/idr: fix infinite loop in idr_get_next()
[PATCH v3 2/2] lib/idr: fix memory leak in ida_alloc_range() error path

review.summary: Two patches for lib/idr: the first sets *nextid=INT_MAX in idr_get_next() when an out-of-range ID is found; the second claims to fix a real memory leak in ida_alloc_range() but the actual lib/idr.c change is entirely absent from the diff.
review.review_inline:
Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

lib/idr: Fixes for infinite loop and memory leak (v3 0/2)

Thanks for the series.  I have concerns with both patches.

--- Patch 1/2: fix infinite loop in idr_get_next() ---

> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(id > INT_MAX)) {
> +		*nextid = INT_MAX;
>  		return NULL;
> +	}

The commit message says this fixes a soft lockup for callers using
idr_for_each_entry().  Looking at the macro:

  #define idr_for_each_entry(idr, entry, id)                      \
          for (id = 0; ((entry) = idr_get_next(idr, &(id))) != NULL; id += 1U)

The increment `id += 1U` is only reached when entry is non-NULL.  When
idr_get_next() returns NULL on the WARN path, the loop condition fails
immediately and the loop exits.  The same is true for
idr_for_each_entry_continue() and idr_for_each_entry_continue_ul() --
all terminate on a NULL return.  What concrete caller and call sequence
produces the claimed soft lockup?

Setting *nextid = INT_MAX when the actual found ID is e.g. 0x80000001
is also questionable.  A caller resuming from *nextid = INT_MAX would
call idr_get_next_ul() from INT_MAX, find the same out-of-range entry
(since 0x80000001 > INT_MAX), WARN again, and return NULL again.  It
does not advance past the bad entry.

> -		BUG_ON(sid != 0);
> +		BUG_ON(sid != INT_MAX);

The original assertion documented the prior contract: *nextid is
unchanged on the error path.  If that contract is changing, please
explain in the commit message why INT_MAX is the right sentinel and
why changing it is safe for all existing callers.

--- Patch 2/2: fix memory leak in ida_alloc_range() error path ---

The commit message says:

  Reorder the error handling to ensure `alloc` is properly freed
  when an XArray error occurs.

But the diff is:

  diff --git a/tools/testing/radix-tree/idr-test.c ...

Only idr-test.c is modified.  The change to lib/idr.c is missing.

To confirm the leak is real: in ida_alloc_range(), the path is

  alloc: kzalloc_obj()  -->  retry  -->  xa_is_value branch:
    bitmap = alloc;
    xas_store(&xas, bitmap);   /* can fail if xarray needs to grow */
    if (xas_error(&xas)) {
        bitmap->bitmap[0] = 0;
        goto out;              /* bitmap == alloc here */
    }

  out:
    if (bitmap != alloc)       /* FALSE -- kfree() is skipped */
        kfree(alloc);
    if (xas_error(&xas))
        return xas_error(&xas); /* alloc leaked */

The same issue exists in the else-branch when bit >= BITS_PER_XA_VALUE
and xas_store() fails after setting bitmap = alloc.  The lib/idr.c fix
must be included in this patch.

> +static void ida_check_leak(void)
> +{
> +	DEFINE_IDA(ida);
> +
> +	/* Allocate up to 128 to ensure we need a new bitmap */
> +	ida_alloc_range(&ida, 0, 128, GFP_KERNEL);
> +	
> +	/* Force a failure by providing an invalid range */
> +	ida_alloc_range(&ida, 0, 0, GFP_KERNEL);

This does not exercise the described error path.  With ID 0 already
allocated, the second call finds the existing bitmap, sees bit 0 set,
searches for the next zero bit, finds it exceeds max=0, and jumps to
nospc -- which correctly calls kfree(alloc).  No XArray insertion
failure occurs.  A test covering the actual leak would need to fail
xas_store() during the xa_is_value-to-bitmap conversion, which this
test does not do.

> +	ida_destroy(&ida);
> +}
> +
>  static void *ida_leak_fn(void *arg)

ida_check_leak() is declared static and defined here, after
user_ida_checks() which calls it.  In C11 there is no implicit
function declaration; calling an undeclared static function is a
constraint violation.  This will not compile.  Either add a forward
declaration before user_ida_checks(), or move ida_check_leak() above
it.

Please respin with:
  1. A concrete reproduction scenario for the Patch 1 infinite loop
     claim, or a revised commit message that accurately describes
     what is being fixed.
  2. The missing lib/idr.c change in Patch 2.
  3. A test that actually triggers the xas_store() failure in the
     xa_is_value conversion path.
  4. The forward declaration or reordering of ida_check_leak().

series.title: tools/testing/vma: add unit tests for vma_flags_[empty, diff_pair, and]()
series.author: "Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)" <ljs@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 02/20] tools/testing/vma: add unit tests for vma_flags_[empty, diff_pair, and]()

review.summary: Adds unit tests for vma_flags_empty(), vma_flags_diff_pair(), vma_flags_and_mask(), and vma_flags_and(), plus temporary test-only helpers vma_flags_same_mask()/vma_flags_same() in custom.h that a later patch in the series promotes to mm.h.
review.review_inline:
Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>

tools/testing/vma: add unit tests for vma_flags_[empty, diff_pair, and]()

Checked the include ordering in vma_internal.h (dup.h at line 61,
custom.h at line 62).  The vma_flags_same_mask()/vma_flags_same()
added here do not conflict with the copies in dup.h/mm.h because those
are introduced by a later patch in the series and the custom.h version
is removed at the same time.  The comment "Place here until needed in
the kernel code" correctly describes the intermediate state.

> +/* Place here until needed in the kernel code. */
> +static __always_inline bool vma_flags_same_mask(vma_flags_t *flags,
> +						vma_flags_t flags_other)

Minor nit: the first parameter drops const compared to the eventual
mm.h version.  The function only reads through the pointer, so const
would be cleaner even for this temporary helper, though it is not a
bug.

> +	/* Should be the same even if re-ordered. */
> +	diff = vma_flags_diff_pair(&flags2, &flags1);
> +#if NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS > 64
> +	ASSERT_FLAGS_SAME(&diff, VMA_MAYWRITE_BIT, VMA_MAYEXEC_BIT, 66, 67);
> +#else
> +	ASSERT_FLAGS_SAME(&diff, VMA_MAYWRITE_BIT, VMA_MAYEXEC_BIT);
> +#endif

Verified against vma_flags_diff_pair() which uses bitmap_xor():
swapping operands does not change XOR, so the symmetry assertion is
correct.

Verified the ASSERT_FLAGS_EMPTY() calls for vma_flags_and_mask() with
disjoint flag sets: flags1={READ,WRITE,EXEC,64,65} and
flags3={IO,MAYBE_GUARD,68,69} share no bits, so AND is empty as
asserted.

Verified the vma_flags_empty() test: clearing all set bits in sequence
and asserting empty at each stage is correct.

All three new tests are registered in run_vma_tests().

Commit message typo: "In additional to" should be "In addition to".

series.title: lib/idr: fix infinite loop in idr_get_next()
series.author: Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] lib/idr: fix infinite loop in idr_get_next()

review.summary: Adds `*nextid = INT_MAX` to the WARN_ON_ONCE branch of idr_get_next() so callers have a defined cursor value when an out-of-range ID is detected. The commit message incorrectly describes the impact as a soft lockup/infinite loop.
review.review_inline:
From: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>
Subject: [PATCH v2] lib/idr: fix infinite loop in idr_get_next()

The commit message claims that not updating *nextid causes a soft lockup
because the caller's index fails to advance and the loop repeats
forever.  I traced the iteration macros in idr.h and cannot reproduce
that scenario.

> diff --git a/lib/idr.c b/lib/idr.c
> @@ -268,8 +268,10 @@ void *idr_get_next(struct idr *idr, int *nextid)
>  	unsigned long id = *nextid;
>  	void *entry = idr_get_next_ul(idr, &id);
>  
> -	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(id > INT_MAX))
> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(id > INT_MAX)) {
> +		*nextid = INT_MAX;
>  		return NULL;
> +	}

The standard iteration macro is:

    for (id = 0; ((entry) = idr_get_next(idr, &(id))) != NULL; id += 1U)

The `id += 1U` increment lives in the for-loop's third clause: it runs
only when the condition was true in the previous iteration.  When
idr_get_next() returns NULL -- which it does in both the old and new
code -- the condition evaluates to false and the loop exits immediately;
`id += 1U` is never reached.  The same holds for
idr_for_each_entry_continue() and idr_for_each_entry_ul(), both of
which break on a NULL return.  Could you point to a concrete call site
where the old code actually loops forever?

Beyond the commit message concern, there is a correctness question about
which execution path triggers the WARN.  There are two distinct cases:

(a) idr_get_next_ul() finds a real entry at id > INT_MAX and returns it;
    idr_get_next() discards the entry and returns NULL.  Setting
    *nextid = INT_MAX here is reasonable as a sentinel.

(b) idr_get_next_ul() returns NULL (no entry found) and leaves the
    local `id` unchanged.  If the caller passed a negative int -- which
    idr_for_each_entry does after INT_MAX + 1U wraps to INT_MIN on a
    two's-complement system -- the unsigned long conversion of *nextid
    produces a value far above INT_MAX, WARN fires, and the function
    returns NULL even though no out-of-range entry exists.

    In case (b) the patch sets *nextid = INT_MAX even though
    idr_get_next_ul() returned NULL.  A caller that saves *nextid and
    resumes from it would then re-visit the entry at INT_MAX that was
    already processed.

Finally, note that if there were a loop that did not exit on NULL,
setting *nextid = INT_MAX would not prevent it from spinning: the next
`id += 1U` would wrap to INT_MIN again, sign-extend to a value above
INT_MAX, and re-trigger the WARN on every subsequent iteration.

Could the commit message be updated to describe the actual observable
behaviour (WARN fires, entry is silently dropped, cursor is left
unchanged), and could case (b) above be addressed or ruled out?

series.title: lib/idr: fix memory leak in ida_alloc_range() error path
series.author: Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] lib/idr: fix memory leak in ida_alloc_range() error path

review.summary: ida_alloc_range() leaks the 'alloc' bitmap when xas_store() fails and bitmap==alloc, because the original 'if (bitmap != alloc) kfree(alloc)' guard evaluates false in that case. The patch reorders the checks so xas_error is tested first, freeing alloc unconditionally on failure.

series.title: Fix race condition in the memory management system
series.author: Hubert Mazur <hmazur@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v1 0/1] Fix race condition in the memory management system
[PATCH v1 1/1] mm: fix race condition in the memory management

review.summary: The patch fixes a real race in execmem_cache_alloc() where a concurrent thread can consume the freshly-populated free block between execmem_cache_populate() and the second __execmem_cache_alloc() call, by allocating directly into busy_areas. However, it introduces a memory leak: only 'size' bytes are recorded in busy_areas while the actual vmalloc allocation is 'alloc_size' (round_up(size, PMD_SIZE)) bytes, causing the tail to be permanently untracked.

series.title: mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series.author: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

review.summary: Switches the deferred split THP shrinker from a custom per-memcg linked list (struct deferred_split embedded in mem_cgroup and pglist_data) to a generic list_lru, gaining proper per-node per-cgroup intersection tracking and eliminating the shrinker-bit imprecision that caused cross-node splitting. New caller-side locking helpers (list_lru_lock/unlock, __list_lru_add/del) allow atomically coupling the folio_test_partially_mapped() flag with list membership.
review.review_inline:
Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

The overall design is sound and addresses a real problem.  I have one
concrete question about the dying-memcg race, and a smaller question
about node-level accounting in the new caller-locked path.

> diff --git a/include/linux/list_lru.h b/include/linux/list_lru.h

[ ... ]

> +struct list_lru_one *list_lru_lock(struct list_lru *lru, int nid,
> +				   struct mem_cgroup *memcg);
> +void list_lru_unlock(struct list_lru_one *l);
> +struct list_lru_one *list_lru_lock_irqsave(struct list_lru *lru, int nid,
> +					   struct mem_cgroup *memcg,
> +					   unsigned long *irq_flags);
> + * Return: the locked list_lru_one, or NULL on failure

The existing lock_list_lru_of_memcg() has two modes.  With
skip_empty=false it falls back to the parent memcg (and fires
VM_WARN_ON if the cgroup is not dying).  With skip_empty=true it
returns NULL.  The "or NULL on failure" wording implies list_lru_lock()
and list_lru_lock_irqsave() use skip_empty=true.

The old folio_split_queue_lock_irqsave() had an explicit dying-memcg
retry loop that guaranteed callers always landed on a live queue:

  retry:
    queue = memcg_split_queue(nid, memcg);
    spin_lock_irqsave(&queue->split_queue_lock, *flags);
    if (unlikely(memcg_is_dying(memcg))) {
        spin_unlock_irqrestore(...);
        memcg = parent_mem_cgroup(memcg);
        goto retry;
    }

In the new code, after memcg_reparent_list_lrus() runs:
  1. The mlru is removed from the XArray (xas_store NULL).
  2. memcg_reparent_list_lru_one() splices every per-node
     src->list into the parent dst->list and sets
     src->nr_items = LONG_MIN.
  3. kvfree_rcu(mlru, rcu) schedules the free.

At that point folio->_deferred_list is non-empty (it now lives on
the parent's list), but folio_memcg() still points at the dying
cgroup -- the charge is released only after unqueue, per the existing
comment above __folio_unqueue_deferred_split().

If __folio_unqueue_deferred_split() calls list_lru_lock_irqsave()
with that dying memcg, xa_load() will return NULL (once the RCU
grace period passes) or lock_list_lru() will return false
(nr_items == LONG_MIN), so list_lru_lock_irqsave() returns NULL.
If the caller treats NULL as "nothing to do", the folio is freed
with _deferred_list still linked into the parent's list_lru_one.
A subsequent deferred_split_scan() walk of the parent list then
reaches freed memory.

The same concern applies to split_huge_page_to_list_to_order(),
which acquires the deferred queue lock before folio_ref_freeze() to
exclude the scan from touching _refcount.  If list_lru_lock_irqsave()
returns NULL there (dying memcg, slot already reparented), the split
proceeds while the folio remains linked in the parent's list with
stale pointers.

How does the new code handle this case?  Does list_lru_lock_irqsave()
fall back to the parent when the slot is dead (skip_empty=false),
matching the old retry loop?  Or does the unqueue path use
list_lru_del() directly (which uses skip_empty=false) and handle
the flag separately?

> +bool __list_lru_add(struct list_lru *lru, struct list_lru_one *l,
> +		    struct list_head *item, int nid,
> +		    struct mem_cgroup *memcg);
> +bool __list_lru_del(struct list_lru *lru, struct list_lru_one *l,
> +		    struct list_head *item, int nid);

The standard list_lru_del() decrements both l->nr_items (under the
sublist spinlock) and nlru->nr_items (atomic, outside the lock).
__list_lru_del() is called with the lock already held by the caller.
Does it also perform atomic_long_dec on nlru->nr_items, or is the
caller expected to do it?  If neither does, list_lru_count_node() --
used by deferred_split_count() -- will undercount after every
caller-locked removal.

[ ... ]

Checked:
- memcg_init_list_lru_one() allocates space for all nr_node_ids
  nodes in one allocation, confirming the commit message claim that
  migration between nodes needs no special care.
- list_lru_add() calls set_shrinker_bit when nr_items goes 0->1,
  replacing the open-coded set_shrinker_bit in the old
  deferred_split_folio().  Correct.
- memcg_reparent_list_lrus() in mem_cgroup_css_offline() subsumes
  the explicit reparent_deferred_split_queue() call.  The ordering
  relative to memcg_reparent_objcgs() is preserved per the comment.
- The folio_order <= 1 early return and swapcache exclusion in
  deferred_split_folio() are orthogonal to the queue change and
  carry over correctly.

series.title: slab: remove alloc_full_sheaf()
series.author: "Vlastimil Babka (SUSE)" <vbabka@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] slab: remove alloc_full_sheaf()

review.summary: The patch removes alloc_full_sheaf() by inlining its alloc_empty_sheaf()+refill_sheaf() sequence directly into __pcs_replace_empty_main(), unifying the two previously separate code paths (empty!=NULL and empty==NULL) into one. No functional change.

series.title: selftests/mm: pagemap_ioctl: remove hungarian notation
series.author: Mike Rapoport <rppt@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] selftests/mm: pagemap_ioctl: remove hungarian notation

review.summary: Pure rename of function parameters in pagemap_ioctl selftest from Hungarian notation (lpBaseAddress, dwRegionSize) to plain C names (addr, size). No functional changes.
review.review_inline:
From: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

selftests/mm: pagemap_ioctl: remove hungarian notation

Pure cosmetic rename; no functional changes.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/pagemap_ioctl.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/pagemap_ioctl.c

[ ... ]

> -int wp_init(void *lpBaseAddress, long dwRegionSize)
> +int wp_init(void *addr, long size)

Verified: all four renamed parameters (addr, size) are used consistently
throughout wp_init(), wp_free(), and wp_addr_range().  The casts and
struct field assignments are unchanged.  No logic, control flow, or
ABI differences.  Looks good.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>

series.title: mm: prevent droppable mappings from being locked
series.author: Anthony Yznaga <anthony.yznaga@oracle.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 1/2] mm: prevent droppable mappings from being locked
[PATCH v2 2/2] selftests/mm: verify droppable mappings cannot be locked

review.summary: The patch introduces vma_supports_mlock() to consolidate VMA lockability checks and uses it in both mlock_fixup() and __mmap_complete() to fix droppable mappings being silently locked under MCL_FUTURE. The selftest patch verifies both mlock() and mlockall(MCL_FUTURE) paths.
review.review_inline:
Anthony Yznaga <anthony.yznaga@oracle.com>

mm: prevent droppable mappings from being locked

The kernel changes are correct. One minor test issue noted below.

> diff --git a/mm/internal.h b/mm/internal.h
> +static inline bool vma_supports_mlock(const struct vm_area_struct *vma)
> +{
> +	if (vma->vm_flags & (VM_SPECIAL | VM_DROPPABLE))
> +		return false;
> +	if (vma_is_dax(vma) || is_vm_hugetlb_page(vma))
> +		return false;
> +	return vma != get_gate_vma(current->mm);
> +}

Verified the gate-VMA check uses current->mm rather than a
caller-supplied mm.  This is safe because the only caller of
__mmap_complete() is __mmap_region(), which opens with
"struct mm_struct *mm = current->mm" and stores that into
map->mm, so both are identical at the call site.

> diff --git a/mm/mlock.c b/mm/mlock.c
> -	if (newflags == oldflags || (oldflags & VM_SPECIAL) ||
> -	    is_vm_hugetlb_page(vma) || vma == get_gate_vma(current->mm) ||
> -	    vma_is_dax(vma) || vma_is_secretmem(vma) || (oldflags & VM_DROPPABLE))
> +	if (newflags == oldflags || vma_is_secretmem(vma) ||
> +	    !vma_supports_mlock(vma))

All conditions from the old check are accounted for: VM_SPECIAL,
hugetlb, gate VMA, DAX, and VM_DROPPABLE are captured inside
vma_supports_mlock(); secretmem is kept as a separate predicate,
which is correct since it must block both lock and unlock regardless
of the other conditions.  No behaviour change for any pre-existing
VMA type.

> diff --git a/mm/vma.c b/mm/vma.c
> -		if ((vm_flags & VM_SPECIAL) || vma_is_dax(vma) ||
> -				is_vm_hugetlb_page(vma) ||
> -				vma == get_gate_vma(mm))
> +		if (!vma_supports_mlock(vma))

This is the actual bug fix: VM_DROPPABLE was absent from the
__mmap_complete() filter, allowing droppable VMAs to carry VM_LOCKED
when MCL_FUTURE was active.  The new check covers every condition the
old code checked plus VM_DROPPABLE.  Also verified: secretmem is not
regressed here because secretmem does not set VM_SPECIAL but does set
VM_LOCKED explicitly in secretmem_mmap_prepare(), and the
vma_supports_mlock() path correctly counts it in locked_vm, which is
unchanged from the old behaviour.

[ ... ]

> +static void test_mlockall_future_droppable(void)
> +{
> +	char *map;
> +	unsigned long page_size = getpagesize();
> +
> +	if (mlockall(MCL_CURRENT | MCL_FUTURE))
> +		ksft_exit_fail_msg(...);
> +
> +	map = mmap(NULL, 2 * page_size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,
> +		   MAP_ANONYMOUS | MAP_DROPPABLE, -1, 0);
> +
> +	ksft_test_result(!unlock_lock_check(map, false), ...);

The mmap() return value is not checked against MAP_FAILED before
being passed to unlock_lock_check().  If mmap() fails (e.g. on a
kernel built without MAP_DROPPABLE support), map == (char *)-1.
is_vmflag_set() will search /proc/self/smaps for the address
0xffffffffffffffff, find nothing, return 0, and the test silently
passes having verified nothing.

The companion function test_mlock_droppable() correctly guards with:

  if (map == MAP_FAILED)
          ksft_exit_fail_msg("mmap error: %s", strerror(errno));

Please add the same check here.

series.title: slab: fix memory leak when refill_sheaf() fails
series.author: Qing Wang <wangqing7171@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] slab: fix memory leak when refill_sheaf() fails

review.summary: refill_sheaf() can return -ENOMEM after partially filling a sheaf (sheaf->size > 0), but alloc_full_sheaf() and __pcs_replace_empty_main() called free_empty_sheaf() directly on failure, which only kfree()s the sheaf struct without releasing the objects in sheaf->objects[], leaking them. The patch fixes this by calling sheaf_flush_unused() before free_empty_sheaf() in both failure paths, matching the pattern already used in all other error paths in the file.
review.review_inline:
Author: Qing Wang <wangqing7171@gmail.com>

slab: fix memory leak when refill_sheaf() fails

Verified the bug: refill_sheaf() updates sheaf->size with however many
objects it managed to allocate before returning -ENOMEM, so sheaf->size
can be > 0 on failure. free_empty_sheaf() only does kfree(sheaf), so
the partially allocated objects were lost.

> diff --git a/mm/slub.c b/mm/slub.c
> @@ -2828,6 +2829,7 @@ static int refill_sheaf(...
> +static void sheaf_flush_unused(struct kmem_cache *s, struct slab_sheaf *sheaf);

Forward declaration is necessary: alloc_full_sheaf() is defined before
sheaf_flush_unused() in the file. This is the right approach.

> @@ -2837,6 +2839,7 @@ static struct slab_sheaf *alloc_full_sheaf(...
>  	if (refill_sheaf(s, sheaf, gfp | __GFP_NOMEMALLOC | __GFP_NOWARN)) {
> +		sheaf_flush_unused(s, sheaf);
>  		free_empty_sheaf(s, sheaf);

Checked: alloc_full_sheaf() holds no lock itself. sheaf_flush_unused()
calls __kmem_cache_free_bulk(), consistent with every other error path
in the file (kmem_cache_prefill_sheaf(), the barn flush path, etc.).

> @@ -4623,6 +4626,7 @@ __pcs_replace_empty_main(...
> +			sheaf_flush_unused(s, empty);
>  			free_empty_sheaf(s, empty);

Checked: local_unlock(&s->cpu_sheaves->lock) is called before this
branch, so no lock is held here. Correct.

Also checked that the new WARN_ON(sheaf->size > 0) in free_empty_sheaf()
does not trigger on any pre-existing caller:
- The empty_list loop in the barn flush iterates barn->sheaves_empty,
  which holds sheaves with size == 0 by construction.
- The kmem_cache_destroy() path is guarded by
  if (!WARN_ON(pcs->main->size)), reaching free_empty_sheaf() only
  when size == 0.
- All other callers already precede free_empty_sheaf() with
  sheaf_flush_unused().

The fix is correct and complete.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>

series.title: record non-slab shrinkers for non-root memcgs when kmem is disabled
series.author: Haifeng Xu <haifeng.xu@shopee.com>

patches.title: [PATCH V3 0/4] record non-slab shrinkers for non-root memcgs when kmem is disabled
[PATCH V3 1/4] mm: shrinker: add one more parameter in shrinker_id()
[PATCH V3 2/4] mm: shrinker: move shrinker_id() code block below memcg_kmem_online()
[PATCH V3 3/4] mm: shrinker: optimize the allocation of shrinker_info when setting cgroup_memory_nokmem
[PATCH V3 4/4] mm: shrinker: remove unnecessary check in shrink_slab_memcg()

review.summary: This series optimizes memcg shrinker_info allocation under cgroup_memory_nokmem by introducing a second IDR (shrinker_nonslab_idr) so non-root memcgs only track non-slab shrinkers; however the diff is truncated and the most critical path -- the shrinker IDR lookup in shrink_slab_memcg() -- cannot be verified, and reparent_shrinker_deferred() has an id-scheme mismatch that would corrupt deferred work accounting when reparenting to root.
review.review_inline:
Haifeng Xu <haifeng.xu@shopee.com>

mm: shrinker: record non-slab shrinkers for non-root memcgs when kmem
is disabled (V3, 4 patches)

The optimization goal is clear and the performance win is substantial.
The two-IDR design is conceptually reasonable.  Issues below.


--- [PATCH V3 3/4] mm: shrinker: optimize the allocation of
    shrinker_info when setting cgroup_memory_nokmem ---

> diff --git a/mm/shrinker.c b/mm/shrinker.c

[ ... ]

> +static DEFINE_IDR(shrinker_nonslab_idr);
> +
>  static int shrinker_memcg_alloc(struct shrinker *shrinker)
>  {
>  	int id, ret = -ENOMEM;
> +	bool kmem_online;
>
>  	if (mem_cgroup_disabled())
>  		return -ENOSYS;
>
> +	kmem_online = memcg_kmem_online();
>  	mutex_lock(&shrinker_mutex);

The V3 changelog says "read shrinker_nonslab_nr_max and shrinker_nr_max
after acquiring shrinker_mutex", but kmem_online is sampled before the
lock.  If cgroup_memory_nokmem transitions between the read and the
lock, expand_shrinker_info() gets stale full/root flags and expands all
non-root memcgs for a shrinker that should only expand root.  In
practice this is likely a one-time boot decision, but a comment
explaining the assumption would help.

The base code has:

    if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled() && !(shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_NONSLAB))
        return -ENOSYS;

This early return is removed by the patch, so non-NONSLAB slab
shrinkers now succeed through shrinker_memcg_alloc() and become
SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE even when kmem is disabled.  Previously they fell
through to non_memcg: in shrinker_alloc() and used shrinker->nr_deferred
for all accounting.  After the patch the global shrink path for root
memcg calls xchg_nr_deferred_memcg() with root_mem_cgroup instead of
the per-shrinker nr_deferred[].  Is this behavioural change intentional
and tested?

[ ... ]

> +	shrinker->nonslab_id = -1;
> +	if (!kmem_online && shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_NONSLAB) {
> +		int nonslab_id;
> +
> +		nonslab_id = idr_alloc(&shrinker_nonslab_idr, shrinker,
> +				       0, 0, GFP_KERNEL);

This introduces two independent ID spaces: shrinker_idr (shrinker->id)
for all memcgs and shrinker_nonslab_idr (shrinker->nonslab_id) for
non-root memcgs when kmem is disabled.  The non-root memcg bitmap then
stores nonslab_id as the bit position via shrinker_id(memcg, shrinker).

For this scheme to be correct, shrink_slab_memcg() must use
shrinker_nonslab_idr when doing the shrinker lookup for non-root memcgs
with kmem disabled.  The base code at the lookup site reads:

    shrinker_id = calc_shrinker_id(index, offset);
    ...
    shrinker = idr_find(&shrinker_idr, shrinker_id);

When kmem is disabled, shrinker_id here is actually a nonslab_id from
shrinker_nonslab_idr.  Passing it to shrinker_idr finds the wrong
shrinker (or NULL if the slot is empty), which would either invoke the
wrong ->scan_objects callback or silently clear the bit and skip
shrinking.

The diff is truncated and the body of shrink_slab_memcg() after patch 3
is not visible.  Could you resend with the full hunk, or at least
explain whether shrink_slab_memcg() switches to shrinker_nonslab_idr
when !memcg_kmem_online()?

[ ... ]

> +static int expand_shrinker_info(int new_id, bool full, bool root)

The bool pair (full, root) encodes three states and the fourth
combination (full=true, root=true) is invalid but not guarded.  Could
this be replaced with an enum to make the three cases explicit and
prevent a future caller from passing an ill-formed combination?

[ ... ]

reparent_shrinker_deferred() concern:

The current (unpatched) reparent_shrinker_deferred() copies nr_deferred
values from child to parent by raw index/offset, assuming both use the
same ID scheme.  After this patch:

  - non-root child: unit[index][offset] encodes nonslab_id
  - root parent:    unit[index][offset] encodes shrinker->id

When a non-root memcg is destroyed and reparented to root_mem_cgroup,
the deferred count at nonslab_id position is added into root's slot at
the same index/offset.  That slot in root's shrinker_info corresponds
to shrinker_id == nonslab_id, which is a completely different shrinker.
This matters because the global shrink path calls xchg_nr_deferred_memcg()
with root_mem_cgroup for MEMCG_AWARE shrinkers, so the corrupted slot
will affect deferred work accounting for whichever unrelated shrinker
occupies shrinker_id == nonslab_id in shrinker_idr.

Does the truncated portion of patch 3 modify reparent_shrinker_deferred()
to translate between the two ID schemes when parent is root_mem_cgroup?


--- [PATCH V3 4/4] mm: shrinker: remove unnecessary check in
    shrink_slab_memcg() ---

> -			/* Call non-slab shrinkers even though kmem is disabled */
> -			if (!memcg_kmem_online() &&
> -			    !(shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_NONSLAB))
> -				continue;

This removal is correct IF the non-root memcg bitmap exclusively holds
nonslab_id-indexed bits for SHRINKER_NONSLAB shrinkers, so that a slab
shrinker bit can never appear there.  That invariant holds if the IDR
lookup in shrink_slab_memcg() is switched to shrinker_nonslab_idr for
the nokmem path.  Patch 4 is therefore correct conditional on patch 3
being complete -- which cannot be verified from the truncated diff.

series.title: mm/swap: strengthen locking assertions and invariants in cluster allocation
series.author: Hui Zhu <hui.zhu@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH v5] mm/swap: strengthen locking assertions and invariants in cluster allocation

review.summary: The patch strengthens locking assertions in swap_cluster_alloc_table() and adds a VM_WARN_ON_ONCE() in isolate_lock_cluster() to formalize the invariant that table allocation only occurs for free clusters isolated from the allocator path, not from the reclaim path.
review.review_inline:
From: Hui Zhu <zhuhui@kylinos.cn>

mm/swap: strengthen locking assertions and invariants in cluster allocation

Verified the three changes against the actual call paths in swapfile.c.

> diff --git a/mm/swapfile.c b/mm/swapfile.c

[ ... ]

> -	lockdep_assert_held(&ci->lock);
>  	lockdep_assert_held(&this_cpu_ptr(&percpu_swap_cluster)->lock);
> +	if (!(si->flags & SWP_SOLIDSTATE))
> +		lockdep_assert_held(&si->global_cluster_lock);
> +	lockdep_assert_held(&ci->lock);

The reordering and new global_cluster_lock assertion look correct.
cluster_alloc_swap_entry() acquires global_cluster_lock for non-SSD
at the top of the function (before any alloc_swap_scan_list() calls),
so the lock is always held when isolate_lock_cluster() ->
swap_cluster_alloc_table() is reached from that path.

For swap_reclaim_work() -> swap_reclaim_full_clusters() ->
isolate_lock_cluster(), neither global_cluster_lock nor the percpu
lock is held. But full_clusters entries always have their tables
already allocated, so cluster_table_is_alloced() returns true and
swap_cluster_alloc_table() is never reached from that path. The
VM_WARN_ON_ONCE additions below guard this invariant explicitly.

> +		flags = ci->flags;
>  		ci->flags = CLUSTER_FLAG_NONE;

[ ... ]

> +		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(flags != CLUSTER_FLAG_FREE);
>  		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(list != &si->free_clusters);

The new check fires before the existing list-pointer check and gives
a more specific diagnostic; both guard the same invariant from
different angles. The u8 flags = CLUSTER_FLAG_NONE initializer
ensures a defined value before the loop body runs; it is always
overwritten before use inside the if (found && ...) block.

Looks good to me.

series.title: selftest: memcg: Skip memcg_sock test if address family not supported
series.author: Waiman Long <longman@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v3] selftest: memcg: Skip memcg_sock test if address family not supported

review.summary: The patch adds a write(ctl_fd, &errno, sizeof(errno)) in tcp_server() when socket() fails, then checks for EAFNOSUPPORT in test_memcg_sock() to return KSFT_SKIP instead of KSFT_FAIL when IPv6 is not available on the system.
review.review_inline:
On Wed, Mar 17 2026, Waiman Long wrote:
> selftest: memcg: Skip memcg_sock test if address family not supported

The overall approach is correct: pass errno back over the control pipe
and let the parent decide whether to skip.  I verified the pipe
protocol (write int in server, read int in parent), the existing
EADDRINUSE retry pattern that it follows, and that cg_destroy() is not
blocked because the server process has already called do_exit() and
been removed from the cgroup by the time goto cleanup runs.

A couple of things worth raising:

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_memcontrol.c b/...
[ ... ]
> +	if (sk < 0) {
> +		/* Pass back errno to the ctl_fd */
> +		write(ctl_fd, &errno, sizeof(errno));
> +		return ret;
> +	}

Passing &errno as the write buffer is consistent with the existing
bind() failure path just below, which already does
write(ctl_fd, &errno, sizeof(errno)).  No issue here.

> +		/* Skip if address family not supported by protocol */
> +		if (err == EAFNOSUPPORT) {
> +			ret = KSFT_SKIP;
> +			goto cleanup;
> +		}

The commit message says the test fails when IPv6 is "not configured to
support SOCK_STREAM".  In that case socket(AF_INET6, SOCK_STREAM, 0)
may return EPROTONOSUPPORT rather than EAFNOSUPPORT, depending on how
the kernel was configured.  Should EPROTONOSUPPORT also be treated as
a skip condition here, or is the intent limited to the case where the
AF_INET6 family itself is absent (CONFIG_IPV6=n)?

Also, when EAFNOSUPPORT causes goto cleanup inside the bind_retries
loop, there is no waitpid(pid) before cg_destroy().  The child has
already exited at that point (it returned after writing errno), so
cg_destroy() succeeds, and the zombie is reaped when the test binary
exits.  The same missing waitpid exists in the err != EADDRINUSE path
at the same loop level, so this is pre-existing behaviour rather than
a regression introduced by this patch.

series.title: mm: improve map count checks
series.author: "Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)" <ljs@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/3] mm: improve map count checks
[PATCH 1/3] mm/mremap: correct invalid map count check
[PATCH 2/3] mm: abstract reading sysctl_max_map_count, and READ_ONCE()
[PATCH 3/3] mm/mremap: check map count under mmap write lock and abstract

review.summary: This series fixes an overly conservative map count headroom in mremap() move operations, adds READ_ONCE() access to sysctl_max_map_count via a helper, and moves the map count check under the mmap write lock to eliminate a TOCTOU race. Patches 1 and 2 are correct; patch 3 introduces a minor behavioral regression by applying the early map count check unconditionally to mremap() paths that have no early destination unmaps.
review.review_inline:
Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>

mm: improve map count checks

Patches 1 and 2 look correct to me.

The headroom correction in patch 1 is well-justified.  After commit
659ace584e7a, __split_vma() explicitly bypasses the map_count check
(confirmed in mm/vma.c: "__split_vma() bypasses sysctl_max_map_count
checking"), so the unmap path can only produce at most a net +1 VMA
(split both ends, remove middle).  Combined with the +1 from copying
the VMA if no merge occurs, headroom of 2 is the right bound in
prep_move_vma().

The WRITE_ONCE claim in patch 2 is verified: proc_dointvec_minmax()
reaches proc_int_conv() with k_ptr_range_check=true, and that branch
unconditionally calls WRITE_ONCE(*k_ptr, tmp_k) regardless of whether
extra2 is set.  The READ_ONCE() in get_sysctl_max_map_count() pairs
correctly with that.

Patch 3 has one concern.

> diff --git a/mm/mremap.c b/mm/mremap.c
> --- a/mm/mremap.c
> +++ b/mm/mremap.c

[ ... ]

> +static bool __check_map_count_against_split(struct mm_struct *mm,
> +					    bool before_unmaps)
> +{
> +	const int sys_map_count = get_sysctl_max_map_count();
> +	int map_count = mm->map_count;
> +
> +	mmap_assert_write_locked(mm);

Good -- the assert correctly enforces the locking requirement.

[ ... ]

> +	if (!check_map_count_against_split_early()) {
> +		mmap_write_unlock(mm);
> +		return -ENOMEM;
> +	}
> +
> +	if (vrm_move_only(vrm)) {
> +		res = remap_move(vrm);
> +	} else {
> +		...
> +		res = vrm_implies_new_addr(vrm) ? mremap_to(vrm) : mremap_at(vrm);
> +	}

check_map_count_against_split_early() is now applied unconditionally
to all mremap() operations.  However, the mremap_at() code path
(!vrm_implies_new_addr, !vrm_move_only) does not perform any early
destination unmaps.

Consider:

  mremap(addr, old_len, new_len, MREMAP_MAYMOVE)  /* expand */

With neither MREMAP_FIXED nor MREMAP_DONTUNMAP set,
vrm_implies_new_addr() returns false, so mremap_at() is called.  If
the VMA can be expanded in-place via expand_vma_in_place(), no
additional VMA is created -- zero headroom is needed.

Yet check_map_count_against_split_early() (before_unmaps=true) rejects
this call when map_count + 4 > sysctl_max_map_count.  Before this
patch, check_mremap_params() returned 0 early for the
!vrm_implies_new_addr() case, so no map-count check was applied to
in-place expansions at all.  This is a behavioral regression: a process
at map_count == sys_max - 3 that previously succeeded with a simple
in-place expand will now receive -ENOMEM.

The same applies to the pure-shrink path through mremap_at(): shrink
uses __split_vma() internally which intentionally bypasses the
map_count limit, yet the unconditional early check can now reject it
before anything is attempted.

For the mremap_at() EXPAND path that falls back to move_vma(), the
existing check_map_count_against_split() inside prep_move_vma() already
handles the limit correctly with headroom of 2 -- no additional early
check is needed.

Would it be correct to gate check_map_count_against_split_early() on
only the paths that perform early destination unmaps?

  if (vrm_move_only(vrm) || vrm_implies_new_addr(vrm)) {
      if (!check_map_count_against_split_early()) {
          mmap_write_unlock(mm);
          return -ENOMEM;
      }
  }

series.title: mm: migrate: transfer large_rmappable flag in folio_migrate_flags()
series.author: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm: migrate: transfer large_rmappable flag in folio_migrate_flags()

review.summary: The patch fixes a use-after-free in the deferred split shrinker by transferring the large_rmappable flag during folio migration. Without this fix, a migrated folio can be added to the deferred split queue but escape cleanup at free time, leaving a stale queue entry that causes use-after-free when the shrinker walks the list.
review.review_inline:
From: <reviewer>
Subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: migrate: transfer large_rmappable flag in folio_migrate_flags()

On <date>, Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:

> folio_migrate_flags() transfers folio state from source to destination
> during migration, but does not transfer the large_rmappable flag.

Verified the bug and the fix. Here is what I checked:

alloc_migration_target() calls __folio_alloc() for large folios without
wrapping the result in page_rmappable_folio(), so the destination folio
indeed never gets large_rmappable set. Same for alloc_misplaced_dst_folio(),
which calls __folio_alloc_node() directly.

> diff --git a/mm/migrate.c b/mm/migrate.c
> --- a/mm/migrate.c
> +++ b/mm/migrate.c
> @@ -846,6 +846,9 @@ void folio_migrate_flags(struct folio *newfolio, struct folio *folio)
>  	folio_copy_owner(newfolio, folio);
>  	pgalloc_tag_swap(newfolio, folio);
>  
> +	if (folio_test_large_rmappable(folio))
> +		folio_set_large_rmappable(newfolio);
> +
>  	mem_cgroup_migrate(folio, newfolio);
>  }

The ordering is correct. In migrate_folio_move(), move_to_new_folio() ---
which calls folio_migrate_flags() --- runs before the
deferred_split_folio(dst, src_partially_mapped) call, so large_rmappable
is set on dst before it is enqueued.

I traced the full UAF path: deferred_split_folio() does not check
large_rmappable before adding to the queue, but folio_unqueue_deferred_split()
does:

  if (folio_order(folio) <= 1 || !folio_test_large_rmappable(folio))
      return false;

Without large_rmappable on dst, this early-return skips the dequeue,
leaving a stale entry. The shrinker then walks freed memory.

Also verified that __folio_migrate_mapping() calls
folio_unqueue_deferred_split() on the source folio (which has large_rmappable
set), so the source side is correctly cleaned up before migration completes.

Looks good to me.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>

series.title: kasan: x86: arm64: KASAN tag-based mode for x86
series.author: Maciej Wieczor-Retman <m.wieczorretman@pm.me>

patches.title: [PATCH v11 00/15] kasan: x86: arm64: KASAN tag-based mode for x86
[PATCH v11 01/15] kasan: sw_tags: Use arithmetic shift for shadow computation
[PATCH v11 02/15] kasan: arm64: x86: Make special tags arch specific
[PATCH v11 04/15] x86/kasan: Add arch specific kasan functions
[PATCH v11 06/15] kasan: arm64: x86: Make page_to_virt() KASAN aware
[PATCH v11 07/15] mm/execmem: Untag addresses in EXECMEM_ROX related pointer arithmetic

review.summary: This series adds KASAN software tag-based mode for x86 using LAM bits [60:57], including an arithmetic-shift kasan_mem_to_shadow() change, arch-specific 4-bit tag constants, x86 arch tag helpers, a generic KASAN-aware page_to_virt(), and tag-reset fixes in execmem/vmalloc to prevent tagged pointers from corrupting maple-tree range arithmetic.
review.review_inline:
Author: Maciej Wieczor-Retman <m.wieczorretman@pm.me>

kasan: x86: arm64: KASAN tag-based mode for x86 (patches 01,02,04,06,07)

I read the full implementations of every modified function and traced
kasan_mem_to_shadow(), kasan_shadow_to_mem(), vm_reset_perms(),
execmem_vmalloc(), and page_to_virt() against the tree.  The overall
design is correct.  A few points below.

--- [PATCH v11 01/15] kasan: sw_tags: Use arithmetic shift ---

> -static inline void *kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
> -{
> -	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
> -		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
> -}

Verified: with the arithmetic (signed) right-shift the inverse
kasan_shadow_to_mem() formula (shadow - offset) << shift still holds
because the change is only in what KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET denotes
(KASAN_SHADOW_END vs. the old bias).  The generic kasan_shadow_to_mem()
in mm/kasan/kasan.h is updated in tandem.  No issue.

--- [PATCH v11 02/15] kasan: arm64: x86: Make special tags arch specific ---

> +++ b/arch/arm64/include/asm/kasan-tags.h
> +#define KASAN_TAG_KERNEL	0xFF /* native kernel pointers tag */
> +#ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_HW_TAGS
> +#define KASAN_TAG_MIN		0xF0 /* minimum value for random tags */
> +#define KASAN_TAG_WIDTH		4
> +#else
> +#define KASAN_TAG_WIDTH		8
> +#endif

Verified: for arm64 HW_TAGS all random tags live in [0xF0, 0xFF], so
XOR-with-0xFF maps them to [0x00, 0x0F], which fits in 4 page->flags
bits.  The round-trip is lossless.  OK.

The mm.h hunk for this patch (6 +++---) must replace the hardcoded 0xff
in page_kasan_tag() and page_kasan_tag_set() with KASAN_TAG_KERNEL.
That replacement is essential for x86 where KASAN_TAG_KERNEL=0xF: a
zeroed page slot XOR'd with 0xff yields 0xFF, but the expected kernel
tag on x86 is 0xF.  Without the change every tag comparison for
uninitialized pages would be wrong.  Could you confirm that hunk is
present?  It was not visible in the provided diff.

--- [PATCH v11 04/15] x86/kasan: Add arch specific kasan functions ---

> +#define __tag_reset(addr)		(sign_extend64((u64)(addr), 56))

Verified: bit 56 is always 1 in a kernel address and is never touched
by a tag stored in bits [60:57].  sign_extend64(addr, 56) copies bit 56
to all of [63:57], restoring the canonical form for both 4-level
(canonical from bit 47) and 5-level (canonical from bit 56) paging.
OK.

> +static inline void *__tag_set(const void *__addr, u8 tag)
> +{
> +	u64 addr = (u64)__addr;
> +	addr &= ~__tag_shifted(KASAN_TAG_BITS_MASK);
> +	addr |= __tag_shifted(tag & KASAN_TAG_BITS_MASK);

When CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS is not set, KASAN_TAG_WIDTH defaults to 0 so
KASAN_TAG_BITS_MASK == 0, __tag_shifted(0) == 0, and __tag_set becomes
a no-op.  OK.

--- [PATCH v11 06/15] mm: Make page_to_virt() KASAN aware ---

> +#define page_to_virt(x) ({						\
> +	void *__addr = __va(PFN_PHYS(page_to_pfn((struct page *)x)));	\
> +	kasan_set_tag(__addr, page_kasan_tag(x));			\
> +})

The cast (struct page *)x silently discards const and accepts any
pointer type without a diagnostic.  The removed arm64 debug version
used __typeof__(x) __page = x, which preserved the type.  Could you
use __typeof__ here to restore the type check?  For example:

  __typeof__(x) __page = (x);
  void *__addr = __va(PFN_PHYS(page_to_pfn(__page)));
  kasan_set_tag(__addr, page_kasan_tag(__page));

> diff --git a/arch/arm64/include/asm/memory.h b/arch/arm64/include/asm/memory.h
> -#if defined(CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL)
> -#define page_to_virt(x)	({					\
> -	__typeof__(x) __page = x;				\
> -	void *__addr = __va(page_to_phys(__page));		\
> -	(void *)__tag_set((const void *)__addr,			\
> -			  page_kasan_tag(__page));		\
> -})
>  #define virt_to_page(x)		pfn_to_page(virt_to_pfn(x))

After this removal, arm64 + CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL falls through to the
new generic page_to_virt().  The generic path uses
PFN_PHYS(page_to_pfn()) rather than page_to_phys().  On arm64 these
are equivalent, so there is no functional regression.  Intentional?

--- [PATCH v11 07/15] mm/execmem: Untag addresses ---

> +	/*
> +	 * Resetting the tag here is necessary to avoid the tagged address
> +	 * ending up in the maple tree structure. There it's linear address
> +	 * can be incorrectly compared with other addresses which can result in

Nit: "it's" should be "its" (possessive, not contraction).

> -	return p;
> +	return kasan_reset_tag(p);

Verified: __vmalloc_node_range() returns a SW_TAGS-tagged pointer.
Without this reset, execmem_cache_populate() passes a tagged address
to execmem_cache_add_locked() which converts it to an unsigned long
and uses it as a maple-tree key.  A later mas_walk() with an untagged
index misses the entry and returns a wrong address.  Fix is correct.

Note: for the !use_cache path, execmem_alloc() calls kasan_reset_tag()
again on the return value of execmem_vmalloc().  Double reset is
idempotent but redundant; the call in execmem_alloc() could now be
dropped for that path, though leaving it is harmless.

> diff --git a/mm/vmalloc.c b/mm/vmalloc.c
> +		unsigned long addr =
> +		    (unsigned long)kasan_reset_tag(page_address(area->pages[i]));

Verified: on x86, page_address() calls lowmem_page_address() ->
page_to_virt(), which after patch 06 applies the per-page KASAN tag.
Different pages can have different random tags, so without the reset
min()/max() would compare addresses with mismatched high bits and
compute a wrong [start, end) range for _vm_unmap_aliases().  Fix is
correct.

series.title: mm/userfaultfd: fix hugetlb fault mutex hash calculation
series.author: Jianhui Zhou <jianhuizzzzz@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v4] mm/userfaultfd: fix hugetlb fault mutex hash calculation

review.summary: The patch fixes a unit mismatch in mfill_atomic_hugetlb() where linear_page_index() (which returns PAGE_SIZE units) was passed to hugetlb_fault_mutex_hash() which expects huge-page units, potentially hashing different threads working on the same huge page to different mutexes. The fix introduces hugetlb_linear_page_index() which is algebraically identical to the existing static vma_hugecache_offset().
review.review_inline:
Jianhui Zhou <jianhuizzzzz@gmail.com>

mm/userfaultfd: fix hugetlb fault mutex hash calculation

Verified the bug and the fix by reading linear_page_index(),
vma_hugecache_offset(), hugetlb_fault_mutex_hash(), and the
mfill_atomic_hugetlb() loop.

> diff --git a/include/linux/hugetlb.h b/include/linux/hugetlb.h
> +static inline pgoff_t hugetlb_linear_page_index(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
> +		unsigned long address)
> +{
> +	struct hstate *h = hstate_vma(vma);
> +
> +	return ((address - vma->vm_start) >> huge_page_shift(h)) +
> +		(vma->vm_pgoff >> huge_page_order(h));
> +}

This is algebraically identical to the static vma_hugecache_offset() in
mm/hugetlb.c, confirming it produces huge-page-granularity offsets.
All other callers of hugetlb_fault_mutex_hash() already pass an idx
computed via vma_hugecache_offset() or vmf->pgoff (set from
vma_hugecache_offset() in the fault path), so the new function is
consistent with existing practice.

> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> -		idx = linear_page_index(dst_vma, dst_addr);
> +		idx = hugetlb_linear_page_index(dst_vma, dst_addr);

dst_addr is guaranteed huge-page-aligned at entry (checked via
dst_start & (vma_hpagesize - 1) == 0) and the loop advances it by
vma_hpagesize, so the shift in hugetlb_linear_page_index() has no
rounding ambiguity.

Looks good to me.

series.title: kthread, exit: clean up kernel thread exit paths
series.author: Christian Brauner <brauner@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/2] kthread, exit: clean up kernel thread exit paths
[PATCH v2 1/2] kthread: remove kthread_exit()
[PATCH v2 2/2] tree-wide: rename do_exit() to task_exit()

review.summary: This two-patch series removes the kthread_exit() macro alias for do_exit() and then renames do_exit() to task_exit() tree-wide to clarify it is a public API rather than a private helper.
review.review_inline:
From: Christian Brauner <brauner@kernel.org>
Subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] kthread, exit: clean up kernel thread exit paths

Verified both patches against the source tree. No regressions found.

--- [PATCH v2 1/2] kthread: remove kthread_exit() ---

> -#define kthread_exit(result) do_exit(result)

kthread_exit() was always a macro that expanded inline to do_exit(), so
no kthread_exit symbol was ever emitted in object files. The removal of
NORETURN(kthread_exit) from tools/objtool/noreturns.h is therefore
correct -- objtool never sees that symbol in any binary, making the
entry spurious from the start.

> -NORETURN(kthread_exit)

Confirmed: all direct call sites are covered. kthread_complete_and_exit(),
kthread() (two exit paths: the !done early-exit and the threadfn()
return), and __module_put_and_kthread_exit() are all updated to call
do_exit() directly. The module_put_and_kthread_exit() macro in
include/linux/module.h for non-modular builds is updated in lock-step.
No remaining callers of the kthread_exit() macro were found outside the
patch.

--- [PATCH v2 2/2] tree-wide: rename do_exit() to task_exit() ---

> -void do_exit(long error_code) __noreturn;
> +void task_exit(long error_code) __noreturn;

All in-kernel call sites verified against the source tree:

- kernel/exit.c: definition, EXPORT_SYMBOL, and the three internal
  callers in make_task_dead(), sys_exit(), and do_group_exit() are all
  updated.
- kernel/kthread.c: the four do_exit() calls introduced by patch 1 are
  renamed, along with the kthread_stop() doc comment.
- kernel/bpf/verifier.c: the BTF_ID(func, do_exit) reference is
  correctly updated to BTF_ID(func, task_exit) to match the renamed
  exported symbol.
- All remaining callers (kernel/pid_namespace.c, kernel/reboot.c,
  kernel/seccomp.c, kernel/vhost_task.c, kernel/umh.c, io_uring/,
  security/tomoyo/gc.c, mm/hugetlb.c, fs/cachefiles/namei.c) are
  covered by the diffstat.

Two files in tools/ (virtio/ringtest/main.c and
scripts/kconfig/nconf.c) contain their own unrelated local do_exit
symbol (a bool variable and a static function, respectively). These are
userspace tools that do not reference the kernel function and are
correctly left untouched.

Looks good to me.

series.title: selftest: memcg: Skp memcg_sock test if address family not supported
series.author: Waiman Long <longman@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] selftest: memcg: Skp memcg_sock test if address family not supported

review.summary: The patch modifies test_memcg_sock to skip instead of fail when socket(AF_INET6, SOCK_STREAM, 0) returns EAFNOSUPPORT, by piping errno from the server child process back to the parent via the existing control fd.

series.title: binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()
series.author: Robert Garcia <rob_garcia@163.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 5.15.y] binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()

review.summary: bm_register_write() calls open_exec() which internally calls deny_write_access() on the interpreter file; when add_entry() fails, the error path was closing the file with filp_close() without first restoring write access. This patch fixes the leak by calling allow_write_access() before filp_close() in that error path.
review.review_inline:
From: Robert Garcia <rob_garcia@163.com>

binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()

Verified the fix by cross-referencing open_exec() -> do_open_execat()
and the error path in bm_register_write().

> diff --git a/fs/binfmt_misc.c b/fs/binfmt_misc.c
> --- a/fs/binfmt_misc.c
> +++ b/fs/binfmt_misc.c

[ ... ]

> +		if (f) {
> +			allow_write_access(f);
>  			filp_close(f, NULL);
> +		}

Checked: open_exec() calls do_open_execat(), which in 5.15.y calls
deny_write_access() before returning the file. The matching
allow_write_access() must be called before releasing the file.
Without this fix, a failed add_entry() leaves write access
permanently denied on the interpreter file.

The choice of allow_write_access() over exe_file_allow_write_access()
is correct for 5.15.y: commit 0357ef03c94e introduced the latter API
after 5.15, so the older allow_write_access() is the right pairing
for this stable branch.

The error path runs after inode_unlock(), so no lock is held here.
kfree(e) is safe because e->interp_file = f was set before
add_entry() but add_entry() failure means e never reached any list.
No double-free risk. Cleanup symmetry with success path looks correct.

series.title: fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array
series.author: ranxiaokai627@163.com

patches.title: [PATCH 0/2] fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array
[PATCH 1/2] mm/slab: move obj_exts_in_slab() definition to slab.h
[PATCH 2/2] memcg: fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array

review.summary: Two-patch series that avoids over-charging kmem for objects whose slabobj_ext array is stored in slab leftover space or embedded inside the object, by moving obj_exts_in_slab() to slab.h and using it from obj_full_size() in memcontrol.c.
review.review_inline:
From: Ran Xiaokai <ran.xiaokai@zte.com.cn>

mm/slab: move obj_exts_in_slab() definition to slab.h
memcg: fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array

The series looks correct. Notes on what was verified below.

> diff --git a/mm/slab.h b/mm/slab.h
> +static inline bool obj_exts_in_slab(struct kmem_cache *s, struct slab *slab)
> +{
> +	unsigned long obj_exts;
> +	unsigned long start;
> +	unsigned long end;
> +
> +	obj_exts = slab_obj_exts(slab);
> +	if (!obj_exts)
> +		return false;
> +
> +	start = (unsigned long)slab_address(slab);
> +	end = start + slab_size(slab);
> +	return (obj_exts >= start) && (obj_exts < end);
> +}

Verified: slab_obj_exts(), slab_address(), and slab_size() are all
defined in slab.h before this point, so the inline is available to
memcontrol.c which includes "slab.h". The !CONFIG_SLAB_OBJ_EXT stub
returning false is also in the right place.

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> -static inline size_t obj_full_size(struct kmem_cache *s)
> +static inline size_t obj_full_size(struct kmem_cache *s, struct slab *slab)
>  {
> +	if (obj_exts_in_slab(s, slab)) {
> +		/*
> +		 * If obj_exts array uses slab leftover space or is embedded
> +		 * in object, no extra space is allocated, just charge the
> +		 * object size.
> +		 */
> +		return s->size;
> +	}

Verified obj_exts_in_slab() covers both in-slab cases: leftover
space (obj_exts pointer lands after all objects, within slab range)
and embedded-in-object (obj_exts pointer lands inside the first
object's body, still within slab range). Both fall in
[slab_address(slab), slab_address(slab) + slab_size(slab)).
Kmalloc'd obj_exts always land outside that range, so the condition
correctly distinguishes the two situations.

Verified charge/uncharge symmetry: a slab's obj_exts placement is
fixed at creation time by alloc_slab_obj_exts_early() for in-slab
cases, or by alloc_slab_obj_exts() for the kmalloc path. Every
call site that might invoke alloc_slab_obj_exts() guards with
!slab_obj_exts(slab), so an in-slab assignment is never overwritten
by a kmalloc'd one. The placement cannot change between
post_alloc_hook (charge) and free_hook (uncharge).

> -	size_t obj_size = obj_full_size(s);
> +	size_t obj_size = obj_full_size(s, slab);

In __memcg_slab_free_hook, slab->obj_exts is still valid at this
point: the caller memcg_slab_free_hook() captures the pointer with
slab_obj_exts(), then calls get_slab_obj_exts() before invoking
__memcg_slab_free_hook(), and only calls put_slab_obj_exts() after
it returns. So obj_exts_in_slab() cannot observe a zeroed
slab->obj_exts here.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>

series.title: binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()
series.author: Robert Garcia <rob_garcia@163.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 6.1.y] binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()

review.summary: bm_register_write() calls open_exec() which internally decrements i_writecount via deny_write_access(), but the error path after add_entry() failure was closing the file with filp_close() without first calling allow_write_access(), permanently leaking a negative i_writecount on the inode. The patch adds the missing allow_write_access() call before filp_close() in that error path.
review.review_inline:
From: Zilin Guan <zilin@seu.edu.cn>

binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()

Verified against fs/exec.c: do_open_execat() calls
exe_file_deny_write_access(), which in mm-new wraps deny_write_access()
with a FMODE_FSNOTIFY_HSM bypass. In 6.1.y, FMODE_FSNOTIFY_HSM does not
exist, so open_exec() unconditionally decrements i_writecount. The old
error path in bm_register_write() called filp_close() without the
symmetric allow_write_access(), leaving i_writecount permanently negative
on the inode.

> +	if (err) {
> +		if (f) {
> +			allow_write_access(f);
>  			filp_close(f, NULL);
> +		}

The choice of allow_write_access() rather than
exe_file_allow_write_access() is correct for 6.1.y. The exe_file_
variant and its FMODE_FSNOTIFY_HSM guard were introduced by commit
0357ef03c94e and are absent from the 6.1.y baseline. Since open_exec()
in 6.1.y always calls deny_write_access() unconditionally, pairing it
with allow_write_access() is the right call here.

Cross-referenced against mm-new: the same error path already carries
exe_file_allow_write_access(f), confirming the upstream intent and that
this backport gets the semantics right for the target kernel.

Looks good to me.

series.title: cgroup: improve cgroup_file_notify() scalability
series.author: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/3] cgroup: improve cgroup_file_notify() scalability
[PATCH v2 1/3] cgroup: reduce cgroup_file_kn_lock hold time in cgroup_file_notify()
[PATCH v2 2/3] cgroup: add lockless fast-path checks to cgroup_file_notify()
[PATCH v2 3/3] cgroup: replace global cgroup_file_kn_lock with per-cgroup_file lock

review.summary: This three-patch series reduces cgroup_file_notify() lock contention by moving kernfs_notify() outside the critical section, adding lockless fast-path NULL and rate-limit checks, and replacing the global cgroup_file_kn_lock with a per-cgroup_file spinlock. The logic is correct overall but patch 3 silently drops a WRITE_ONCE that patch 2 introduced, creating an asymmetry with the READ_ONCE in the lockless fast-path.

series.title: docs: mm: fix typo in numa_memory_policy.rst
series.author: Akinobu Mita <akinobu.mita@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] docs: mm: fix typo in numa_memory_policy.rst

review.summary: Fixes a typo in numa_memory_policy.rst where the section header incorrectly used MPOL_INTERLEAVED instead of the correct MPOL_INTERLEAVE. No code changes.
review.review_inline:
Author: Akinobu Mita <akinobu.mita@gmail.com>

docs: mm: fix typo in numa_memory_policy.rst

Verified against include/uapi/linux/mempolicy.h which defines the
enum value as MPOL_INTERLEAVE (no trailing 'D'). The rest of the
document already uses MPOL_INTERLEAVE consistently (e.g. in the
MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY description and the mbind() flags example).

> -MPOL_INTERLEAVED
> +MPOL_INTERLEAVE

Correct -- MPOL_INTERLEAVE is the name defined in the UAPI header.

Looks good to me.

Reviewed-by: Claude <claude@anthropic.com>

series.title: mm/kasan: adjust documentation
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/damon/core: verify found biggest system ram
[PATCH] fix
[PATCH] mm/damon/stat: return error if monitoring target region is invalid
[PATCH] mm/kasan: adjust documentation

review.summary: A mixed series of four independent fixes: two DAMON patches adding post-conversion overflow guards for 32-bit PAE address arithmetic, a zswap patch adding flush_dcache_folio after kmap-based decompression, and a KASAN documentation patch that moves kasan=on/off to the general boot-parameters section while incorrectly labeling it a generic parameter.
review.review_inline:
On the series "mm/kasan: adjust documentation" (4 patches):

--- [PATCH 1/4] mm/damon/core: verify found biggest system ram ---

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c

[ ... ]

> -	if (res.end < res.start)
> -		return false;
> -
>  	*start = damon_res_to_core_addr(res.start, addr_unit);
>  	*end = damon_res_to_core_addr(res.end + 1, addr_unit);
> +	if (*end <= *start)
> +		return false;
>  	return true;

Verified in damon_find_biggest_system_ram(): struct resource res is
zero-initialised (res = {}), so the old check res.end < res.start
evaluated to 0 < 0 = false and was dead code.  The new check after
damon_res_to_core_addr() correctly catches the overflow case on 32-bit
PAE, where a large physical address can truncate to zero or wrap below
*start after unsigned-long conversion.  The caller
damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default() tests the return value
before using *start/*end, so writing those before returning false is
safe.  Fix looks correct.

--- [PATCH 2/4] fix ---

> diff --git a/mm/zswap.c b/mm/zswap.c

[ ... ]

>  	if (entry->length == PAGE_SIZE) {
>  		void *dst;
>
>  		dst = kmap_local_folio(folio, 0);
>  		memcpy_from_sglist(dst, input, 0, PAGE_SIZE);
>  		dlen = PAGE_SIZE;
>  		kunmap_local(dst);
> +		flush_dcache_folio(folio);
>  	} else {
>  		sg_init_table(&output, 1);
>  		sg_set_folio(&output, folio, PAGE_SIZE, 0);
>  		acomp_request_set_params(acomp_ctx->req, input, &output,
>  					 entry->length, PAGE_SIZE);
>  		ret = crypto_acomp_decompress(acomp_ctx->req);

Adding flush_dcache_folio() after kmap_local_folio + memcpy +
kunmap_local is the correct fix for VIPT-cache architectures (arm,
mips) where writes through a kernel virtual address may not be visible
through the user virtual mapping without a cache flush.

The else branch (compressed path) also writes decompressed data into
the folio via kernel addresses; software acomp implementations
(lz4, zstd, zlib) internally kmap the output scatterlist pages.  Does
that path not also require flush_dcache_folio() on VIPT systems?  If
the acomp layer already guarantees coherency for the compressed path,
a short comment here would help future readers understand why only the
uncompressed branch needs the explicit flush.

--- [PATCH 3/4] mm/damon/stat: return error if monitoring target region
    is invalid ---

> +	if (addr_range.end <= addr_range.start)
> +		return -EINVAL;
>  	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

Analogous to patch 1.  damon_stat_set_monitoring_region() already
guards the no-RAM-found case via the arg.walked check before this
point, so the new check is purely for the post-conversion overflow
case.  Looks correct.

--- [PATCH 4/4] mm/kasan: adjust documentation ---

> +The generic ``kasan=off`` or ``=on`` command line parameter controls
> +whether KASAN is enabled (default: ``on``).

> -disabling KASAN altogether or controlling its features:
> -
> -- ``kasan=off`` or ``=on`` controls whether KASAN is enabled
> -  (default: ``on``).
> +controlling its features:

The commit message says "To reflect the change of kasan=on|off", but
in this tree early_param("kasan", early_kasan_flag) lives exclusively
in mm/kasan/hw_tags.c and is compiled only under CONFIG_KASAN_HW_TAGS.
Calling it a "generic" parameter appears incorrect: passing kasan=off
has no effect on Generic KASAN or SW Tag-Based KASAN kernels.

If a companion patch in this cycle moved the early_param registration
to a shared file (e.g. mm/kasan/common.c) and wired it to the
kasan_flag_enabled static key for all modes, please add a dependency
or forward-reference so this doc change can be evaluated together with
that code change.  As-is the documentation contradicts the source.

series.title: do_notify_parent: sanitize the valid_signal() checks
series.author: Oleg Nesterov <oleg@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH -mm v2] do_notify_parent: sanitize the valid_signal() checks

review.summary: The patch tightens do_notify_parent()'s input validation by replacing the narrow 'sig == -1' WARN with a full valid_signal() guard that returns early, and removes the now-redundant valid_signal() check before __send_signal_locked().
review.review_inline:
Oleg Nesterov <oleg@redhat.com>

do_notify_parent: sanitize the valid_signal() checks

Verified the following:

- All callers pass either tsk->exit_signal (validated by kernel_clone
  via valid_signal() at fork.c) or the literal SIGCHLD, so the new
  WARN path is correctly unreachable under normal operation.

- valid_signal() is defined as sig <= _NSIG, so sig=0 passes the new
  guard.  Inside the function the only mutations to sig are
  sig = SIGCHLD (always valid) and sig = 0 (set when parent's SIGCHLD
  handler is SIG_IGN).  The surviving 'if (sig)' guard before
  __send_signal_locked() still excludes the sig=0 case, so removing
  the redundant valid_signal() there is safe.

- Returning false early on an invalid sig is conservative: the task
  stays as EXIT_ZOMBIE rather than being auto-reaped.  This is the
  right behaviour on a bug path since __wake_up_parent() is also
  skipped, but the WARN already signals a kernel bug at that point.

> -	WARN_ON_ONCE(sig == -1);
> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(!valid_signal(sig)))
> +		return false;

Correct.  The old check only caught sig == -1; the new one catches all
out-of-range values including other negative numbers.  Early return
avoids executing the rest of the function with a bogus sig.

> -	if (valid_signal(sig) && sig)
> +	if (sig)
>  		__send_signal_locked(sig, &info, tsk->parent,
>  				     PIDTYPE_TGID, false);

Safe removal: the entry guard ensures sig is valid on arrival, and the
only internal mutations produce SIGCHLD or 0, both of which are valid.
The valid_signal() here was dead code.

Looks good to me.

series.title: Direct Map Removal Support for guest_memfd
series.author: "Kalyazin, Nikita" <kalyazin@amazon.co.uk>

patches.title: [PATCH v11 00/16] Direct Map Removal Support for guest_memfd
[PATCH v11 01/16] set_memory: set_direct_map_* to take address
[PATCH v11 02/16] set_memory: add folio_{zap,restore}_direct_map helpers
[PATCH v11 03/16] mm/secretmem: make use of folio_{zap,restore}_direct_map
[PATCH v11 04/16] mm/gup: drop secretmem optimization from gup_fast_folio_allowed
[PATCH v11 05/16] mm/gup: drop local variable in gup_fast_folio_allowed
[PATCH v11 06/16] mm: introduce AS_NO_DIRECT_MAP
[PATCH v11 07/16] KVM: guest_memfd: Add stub for kvm_arch_gmem_invalidate
[PATCH v11 08/16] KVM: x86: define kvm_arch_gmem_supports_no_direct_map()
[PATCH v11 09/16] KVM: arm64: define kvm_arch_gmem_supports_no_direct_map()
[PATCH v11 10/16] KVM: guest_memfd: Add flag to remove from direct map
[PATCH v11 11/16] KVM: selftests: load elf via bounce buffer
[PATCH v11 12/16] KVM: selftests: set KVM_MEM_GUEST_MEMFD in vm_mem_add() if guest_memfd != -1
[PATCH v11 13/16] KVM: selftests: Add guest_memfd based vm_mem_backing_src_types
[PATCH v11 14/16] KVM: selftests: cover GUEST_MEMFD_FLAG_NO_DIRECT_MAP in existing selftests
[PATCH v11 15/16] KVM: selftests: stuff vm_mem_backing_src_type into vm_shape
[PATCH v11 16/16] KVM: selftests: Test guest execution from direct map removed gmem

review.summary: This 16-patch series adds direct map removal support to guest_memfd, letting KVM VMs opt in to Spectre-class mitigation via GUEST_MEMFD_FLAG_NO_DIRECT_MAP. It refactors set_direct_map_* to take addresses instead of struct page pointers, generalizes secretmem's direct-map-removal pattern into generic folio_{zap,restore}_direct_map helpers, introduces AS_NO_DIRECT_MAP to replace per-subsystem secretmem checks in GUP/mlock/buildid, and wires the new flag into guest_memfd with arch support stubs for x86 and arm64.
review.review_inline:
On Mon, Mar 17 2026, Nikita Kalyazin wrote:

Series: Direct Map Removal Support for guest_memfd (v11)

Cross-referenced the diff against the kernel tree.  Checked error
paths, TLB flush ordering, refcount symmetry, locking, and call site
coverage for patches 01-07.  Details below; no regressions found.

[ Patch 01: set_memory: set_direct_map_* to take address ]

Verified all call sites in the tree before this patch:

  mm/secretmem.c          - 3 sites (secretmem_fault x2, secretmem_free_folio)
  mm/execmem.c            - 2 sites (set_direct_map_valid_noflush with pages[])
  mm/vmalloc.c            - 2 sites via set_area_direct_map function pointer
  kernel/power/snapshot.c - 2 sites
  arch/{arm64,x86,s390,riscv,loongarch} - implementations and headers
  include/linux/set_memory.h - stubs

All are accounted for in the 15-file diffstat.  The numpages parameter
promotion from 'unsigned' to 'unsigned long' in
set_direct_map_valid_noflush() is applied consistently across all five
arch implementations.

[ Patch 02: set_memory: add folio_{zap,restore}_direct_map helpers ]

> +int folio_zap_direct_map(struct folio *folio)
> +{
> +	const void *addr = folio_address(folio);
> +	int ret;
> +
> +	ret = set_direct_map_valid_noflush(addr, folio_nr_pages(folio), false);
> +	flush_tlb_kernel_range((unsigned long)addr,
> +			       (unsigned long)addr + folio_size(folio));
> +
> +	return ret;
> +}

The TLB flush is unconditional even when set_direct_map_valid_noflush
returns an error.  This is correct: for a multi-page folio, if the
call fails partway (e.g. ENOMEM while splitting a huge page), the
sub-pages that were already invalidated need their TLB entries flushed
before returning.  For order-0 folios (all current callers), a failure
means the page table was not modified at all, so the flush is a
vacuous but harmless no-op.

> +void folio_restore_direct_map(struct folio *folio)
> +{
> +	WARN_ON_ONCE(set_direct_map_valid_noflush(folio_address(folio),
> +						  folio_nr_pages(folio), true));
> +}

The kdoc comment correctly constrains this to be called only after a
prior successful folio_zap_direct_map().  Verified all call sites in
secretmem_fault honor this: folio_restore_direct_map() is only reached
via the filemap_add_folio() error path, which is guarded by a prior
successful folio_zap_direct_map() returning 0.  The WARN_ON_ONCE is
appropriate since failure here would indicate a kernel bug (the huge
page split already happened in the zap, so restoration only updates
protection bits and cannot need new allocations).

[ Patch 03: mm/secretmem: make use of folio_{zap,restore}_direct_map ]

> -		err = set_direct_map_invalid_noflush(folio_address(folio));
> +		err = folio_zap_direct_map(folio);
>  		if (err) {

> -		addr = (unsigned long)folio_address(folio);
> -		flush_tlb_kernel_range(addr, addr + PAGE_SIZE);

The TLB flush now occurs inside folio_zap_direct_map() rather than
after filemap_add_folio().  This is correct: the direct map PTE is
invalid as soon as set_direct_map_valid_noflush returns successfully,
so deferring the flush to after filemap_add_folio() provided no
benefit.  The flush range changes from PAGE_SIZE to folio_size(folio),
which for order-0 secretmem folios is identical.

Note that secretmem_free_folio() still calls
set_direct_map_default_noflush() directly.  This is fine: the free
path is not subject to the 'after successful zap' precondition that
folio_restore_direct_map() documents, and the direct call is correct.

[ Patches 04+05: mm/gup: refactor gup_fast_folio_allowed ]

Verified semantic equivalence of the refactored function for all
relevant combinations:

  - anon folios:          both paths return true at the mapping_flags check
  - hugetlb:             both return true early
  - secretmem:           both return false
  - shmem + longterm writable pin: both return true (shmem_mapping())
  - other file-backed + longterm writable pin: both return false
  - other file-backed, no longterm pin: both return true

Removing the '!folio_test_large(folio)' secretmem short-circuit is
necessary: future large-folio NO_DIRECT_MAP guest_memfd folios would
bypass the mapping check under the old code.

[ Patch 06: mm: introduce AS_NO_DIRECT_MAP ]

Bit 12 is free.  Current enum ends at AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY=11; bits
16-25 are reserved for FOLIO_ORDER.  No conflict.

> +static inline bool vma_has_no_direct_map(const struct vm_area_struct *vma)
> +{
> +	return vma->vm_file && mapping_no_direct_map(vma->vm_file->f_mapping);
> +}

This correctly replaces vma_is_secretmem() (which checked vm_ops) in
check_vma_flags() and mlock, since patch 06 makes secretmem_file_create()
call mapping_set_no_direct_map() on the inode's mapping, making the
two predicates semantically equivalent for all secretmem VMAs.

In lib/buildid.c, the new mapping_no_direct_map() check in
freader_get_folio() is a belt-and-suspenders addition: it avoids
taking a folio reference to memory that freader_fetch() would reject
anyway.  The check in freader_fetch() still guards the
may_fault/__kernel_read branch and is independently necessary.

[ Patch 07: KVM: guest_memfd: Add stub for kvm_arch_gmem_invalidate ]

> -#ifdef CONFIG_HAVE_KVM_ARCH_GMEM_INVALIDATE
>  static void kvm_gmem_free_folio(struct folio *folio)
> +{
> +	...
> +	kvm_arch_gmem_invalidate(pfn, pfn + (1ul << order));
> +}
> -#endif

Clean.  The inline no-op stub in kvm_host.h means kvm_gmem_free_folio
is now always registered in kvm_gmem_aops with zero cost on platforms
lacking CONFIG_HAVE_KVM_ARCH_GMEM_INVALIDATE, and the ifdef clutter is
gone.

Reviewed patches 01-07.  Error paths, TLB flush ordering, refcount
symmetry, AS flag bit allocation, and all set_direct_map_* call sites
verified against the source tree.

series.title: mm/pagewalk: fix race between concurrent split and refault
series.author: Max Boone via B4 Relay <devnull+mboone.akamai.com@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/pagewalk: fix race between concurrent split and refault

review.summary: The patch fixes a race in walk_pud_range() where split_huge_pud() clears a PUD entry and a concurrent thread (VFIO DMA setup) re-installs a huge PUD leaf before walk_pmd_range() runs, causing pmd_offset() to treat the leaf entry bits as a PMD table address and crash. The fix snapshots the PUD value via pudp_get() at the top of walk_pmd_range() and retries via ACTION_AGAIN if the PUD is non-present or a leaf, mirroring the existing PMD-level retry pattern.
review.review_inline:
From: Max Boone <mboone@akamai.com>
Subject: [PATCH] mm/pagewalk: fix race between concurrent split and refault

> diff --git a/mm/pagewalk.c b/mm/pagewalk.c
> --- a/mm/pagewalk.c
> +++ b/mm/pagewalk.c
> @@ -97,6 +97,7 @@ static int walk_pte_range(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
>  static int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
>  			  struct mm_walk *walk)
>  {
> +	pud_t pudval = pudp_get(pud);

[ ... ]

> +	if (!pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval)) {
> +		walk->action = ACTION_AGAIN;
> +		return 0;
> +	}

Verified the race path. __split_huge_pud_locked() calls
pudp_huge_clear_flush(), which atomically clears the PUD to zero.
Between that clear and the call to walk_pmd_range(), a concurrent
thread can install a fresh huge-page PUD leaf, making *pud a leaf
entry again. The old guard in walk_pud_range() only checked
pud_none(*pud) and would miss this case. The new check correctly
catches both:

  - the "still-none" case via !pud_present(pudval)
  - the "refaulted-as-leaf" case via pud_leaf(pudval)

One thing worth noting: the generic pmd_offset(pud, addr) re-reads
*pud via pud_pgtable(*pud) after the pudp_get() snapshot, so a
narrow TOCTOU between the check and the use remains. This matches
the accepted pattern used in folio_walk_start() (pudp_get() check
followed by pmd_offset(pudp, addr)), and the window is just a
couple of instructions wide. A PMD table observed as a valid
table-pointer PUD cannot be freed while the mmap_lock read is held,
so this is safe in practice.

> @@ -218,12 +231,13 @@ static int walk_pud_range(p4d_t *p4d, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
>  		else if (pud_leaf(*pud) || !pud_present(*pud))
>  			continue; /* Nothing to do. */
>
> -		if (pud_none(*pud))
> -			goto again;
> -
