>  		err = walk_pmd_range(pud, addr, next, walk);
>  		if (err)
>  			break;
> +
> +		if (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)
> +			goto again;

Removal of the pud_none() guard is correct: that case is now
subsumed by !pud_present(pudval) inside walk_pmd_range(). The
ACTION_AGAIN placement is right -- walk->action is reset to
ACTION_SUBTREE at the top of the retry iteration before pud_entry()
is called, so there is no stale action visible to walk_p4d_range()
on the normal exit path.

Looks good to me.

series.title: mm: remove '!root_reclaim' checking in should_abort_scan()
series.author: "zhaoyang.huang" <zhaoyang.huang@unisoc.com>

patches.title: [PATCHv2] mm: remove '!root_reclaim' checking in should_abort_scan()

review.summary: The patch removes the !root_reclaim(sc) early-return from should_abort_scan(), allowing MGLRU non-root memcg reclaim (e.g. memory.reclaim, reclaim_high) to abort scanning once sc->nr_reclaimed >= sc->nr_to_reclaim, preventing the overreclaim observed when iterating large lruvecs.
review.review_inline:
On [date], Zhaoyang Huang wrote:

mm: remove '!root_reclaim' checking in should_abort_scan()

Verified the two call sites of should_abort_scan() and their callers:

  - try_to_shrink_lruvec() <- lru_gen_shrink_lruvec()
    which asserts VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(root_reclaim(sc)), confirming this
    is exclusively a non-root memcg reclaim path.

  - try_to_shrink_lruvec() <- shrink_one() <- lru_gen_shrink_node()
    and shrink_many() <- lru_gen_shrink_node(), which asserts
    VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!root_reclaim(sc)) -- root reclaim only. No
    behavioral change on this caller: the old !root_reclaim(sc) guard
    was already a no-op when root_reclaim() returned true.

> -	/* don't abort memcg reclaim to ensure fairness */
> -	if (!root_reclaim(sc))
> -		return false;
> -
>  	if (sc->nr_reclaimed >= max(sc->nr_to_reclaim, compact_gap(sc->order)))
>  		return true;

For the !root_reclaim path, sc->nr_to_reclaim is properly bounded:
try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages() sets it to max(nr_pages, SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX),
so the abort condition is well-defined and not accidentally zero.

The second check in should_abort_scan() -- the kswapd watermark loop --
is guarded by current_is_kswapd(), which is false for memcg reclaim
contexts, so it falls through to return false correctly when
nr_reclaimed < nr_to_reclaim.

The fairness argument holds: after b82b530740b9 ("mm: vmscan: restore
incremental cgroup iteration"), proportional reclaim fairness across
memcgs no longer requires a full single-pass, so aborting early for
one lruvec does not starve siblings.

Patch looks correct.

Reviewed-by: <your-name> <your-email>

series.title: Encapsulate the populate and alloc as one atomic
series.author: Hubert Mazur <hmazur@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/1] Encapsulate the populate and alloc as one atomic
[PATCH v2 1/1] mm/execmem: Make the populate and alloc atomic

review.summary: The patch fixes a race in the execmem ROX cache where a freshly populated memory block could be stolen by a concurrent allocation between the populate and alloc steps. It makes the two operations atomic by holding execmem_cache.mutex across both execmem_cache_add_locked() and the new __execmem_cache_alloc_locked() inside __execmem_cache_populate_alloc().

series.title: mm/hugetlb: Remove fake head pages
series.author: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCHv7.1 12/18] mm/hugetlb: Remove fake head pages

review.summary: Introduces per-zone per-order shared tail pages (vmemmap_tails[]) to replace fake head pages in HVO, allowing truly shared read-only vmemmap tails. Changes MAX_FOLIO_ORDER to use ilog2() for a compile-time constant suitable as an array dimension.
review.review_inline:
From: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
Subject: [PATCHv7.1 12/18] mm/hugetlb: Remove fake head pages

Verified: cmpxchg race in vmemmap_get_tail(), order bounds enforced by
vmemmap_should_optimize_folio() -> hugetlb_vmemmap_optimizable(), error
paths and static_branch symmetry, and the two-phase init design (early
alloc in sparse-vmemmap.c vs. late_initcall reinit in
hugetlb_vmemmap_init()).  One functional question below.

> diff --git a/mm/hugetlb_vmemmap.c b/mm/hugetlb_vmemmap.c

[ ... ]

> +static struct page *vmemmap_get_tail(unsigned int order, struct zone *zone)
> +{
> +	const unsigned int idx = order - VMEMMAP_TAIL_MIN_ORDER;
> +	struct page *tail, *p;
> +	int node = zone_to_nid(zone);
> +
> +	tail = READ_ONCE(zone->vmemmap_tails[idx]);
> +	if (likely(tail))
> +		return tail;
> +
> +	tail = alloc_pages_node(node, GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_ZERO, 0);
> +	if (!tail)
> +		return NULL;
> +
> +	p = page_to_virt(tail);
> +	for (int i = 0; i < PAGE_SIZE / sizeof(struct page); i++)
> +		init_compound_tail(p + i, NULL, order, zone);
> +
> +	if (cmpxchg(&zone->vmemmap_tails[idx], NULL, tail)) {
> +		__free_page(tail);
> +		tail = READ_ONCE(zone->vmemmap_tails[idx]);
> +	}

The cmpxchg race looks correct: cmpxchg provides a full barrier so the
winner's init_compound_tail() stores are visible to any CPU that
subsequently reads the non-NULL pointer; nothing ever clears
vmemmap_tails[idx] back to NULL, so the READ_ONCE after a losing
cmpxchg always sees a valid page.

Minor: alloc_pages_node() here is not followed by memmap_pages_add(1).
The vmemmap_head allocation a few lines later does call
memmap_pages_add(1), but the shared tail page is left uncounted.  Is
that intentional?  It means /proc/meminfo Vmemmap underreports by one
page per zone per optimizable order.

> diff --git a/mm/sparse-vmemmap.c b/mm/sparse-vmemmap.c

[ ... ]

> +static __meminit struct page *vmemmap_get_tail(unsigned int order,
> +					    struct zone *zone)
> +{
> +	...
> +	/*
> +	 * Only allocate the page, but do not initialize it.
> +	 *
> +	 * Any initialization done here will be overwritten by memmap_init().
> +	 *
> +	 * hugetlb_vmemmap_init() will take care of initialization after
> +	 * memmap_init().
> +	 */
> +	p = vmemmap_alloc_block_zero(PAGE_SIZE, node);
> +	...
> +	zone->vmemmap_tails[idx] = tail;
> +	return tail;
> +}

The comment says hugetlb_vmemmap_init() (late_initcall) will reinitialize
the tail page after memmap_init() overwrites it.  The concern is that
hugetlb_init() is a subsys_initcall, which runs before late_initcall.

hugetlb_init() -> gather_bootmem_prealloc() ->
prep_and_add_bootmem_folios() ->
hugetlb_vmemmap_optimize_bootmem_folios() ->
__hugetlb_vmemmap_optimize_folio() -> vmemmap_get_tail() [runtime,
hugetlb_vmemmap.c].

That runtime vmemmap_get_tail() finds the slot already non-NULL and
returns it immediately, without calling init_compound_tail().  At that
point memmap_init() has already run and zeroed compound_info in the
shared tail page via __init_single_page() -> mm_zero_struct_page().

With compound_info == 0 and the mask-based _compound_head() path:

    mask = (0 & 1) - 1;   /* = -1UL, i.e. treated as non-tail */
    mask |= 0;
    return (unsigned long)page & mask;  /* returns page itself */

So compound_head() on any tail struct page of a non-pre-HVO bootmem
page that was HVO'd during hugetlb_init() returns the tail page itself
rather than the folio head, until hugetlb_vmemmap_init() eventually
reinitializes the shared tail page.

This window only opens when CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP_PREINIT is enabled
and there are both pre-HVO pages (which create the early
vmemmap_tails[] entry without init_compound_tail()) and non-pre-HVO
bootmem pages of the same order in the same zone.  Can that combination
arise in practice?  If so, is the kernel guaranteed not to call
compound_head() on a tail page of those enqueued HVO'd pages between
subsys_initcall and late_initcall?

One way to close the window: have the runtime vmemmap_get_tail() call
init_compound_tail() even when an existing slot is found, or have the
early path store the page under a separate flag so the runtime path
knows to initialize on first use.

series.title: mm: Fix a hmm_range_fault() livelock / starvation problem
series.author: =?UTF-8?q?Thomas=20Hellstr=C3=B6m?= <thomas.hellstrom@linux.intel.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 6.18.y] mm: Fix a hmm_range_fault() livelock / starvation problem

review.summary: This patch fixes a livelock in do_swap_page() where a failed folio_trylock() on a device-private folio caused hmm_range_fault() to spin indefinitely if the lock holder was blocked waiting for work items scheduled on the same CPU. The fix replaces the implicit spin-retry with a proper sleep on PG_locked via softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked().
review.review_inline:
Thomas Hellstrom <thomas.hellstrom@linux.intel.com>

mm: Fix a hmm_range_fault() livelock / starvation problem

Verified the locking protocol around softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked():
the ptl is held across the wait-queue registration (spin_lock_irq on
q->lock, folio_set_waiters, folio_trylock_flag, __add_wait_queue),
and spin_unlock(ptl) happens only after that critical section.
Since any path dropping the last device-private page reference must
hold the ptl to remove the PTE entry, the folio pointer obtained from
softleaf_to_folio(entry) remains valid until ptl is released inside
the function.

Control flow in do_swap_page() after the fix: pte_unmap(vmf->pte)
precedes the call, ptl is released inside softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked,
and we fall through to 'goto out' with si==NULL (get_swap_device was
not yet called in this branch), so no resource leak on this path.
Return value stays 0 (VM_FAULT_NONE), causing a clean retry after the
device-private folio is unlocked by the migration path.

Wake-up correctness: wait->flags=0 (no WQ_FLAG_EXCLUSIVE), so
wake_page_function does not attempt to acquire PG_locked -- it just
observes it becoming clear and sets WQ_FLAG_WOKEN. wake_page_match()
matches on wait_page.folio == key->folio and bit_nr == PG_locked,
both set correctly for the device-private folio via
softleaf_to_folio(entry).

One nit in the updated kdoc:

> diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
> --- a/mm/filemap.c
> +++ b/mm/filemap.c

[ ... ]

> + * entry referencing a dvice_private page to be unlocked. This is
                         ^^^^

Typo: "dvice_private" should be "device_private".

The !CONFIG_MIGRATION stub correctly calls WARN_ON_ONCE(1) and then
spin_unlock(ptl) to avoid a deadlock in the impossible case;
device-private memory cannot exist without CONFIG_MIGRATION so the
warning is appropriate.

series.title: zram: change scan_slots to return void
series.author: Sergey Senozhatsky <senozhatsky@chromium.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] zram: change scan_slots to return void

review.summary: Converts scan_slots_for_writeback() and scan_slots_for_recompress() from returning int (always 0) to void, since neither function ever returned a non-zero value and no caller checked the return status.
review.review_inline:
On Mon, Mar 17 2026, Sergey Senozhatsky wrote:

> scan_slots_for_writeback() and scan_slots_for_recompress()
> work in a "best effort" fashion, if they cannot allocate
> memory for a new pp-slot candidate they just return and
> post-processing selects slots that were successfully
> scanned thus far.  scan_slots functions never return
> errors and their callers never check the return status,
> so convert them to return void.

Verified the following:

- Both functions contained only `return 0` as their sole return
  statement, so no error path is silenced by this change.

- All four call sites of scan_slots_for_writeback() in
  writeback_store() and the single call site of
  scan_slots_for_recompress() in recompress_store() already
  discarded the return value, so there is no functional change.

> -static int scan_slots_for_writeback(struct zram *zram, u32 mode,
> +static void scan_slots_for_writeback(struct zram *zram, u32 mode,

> -static int scan_slots_for_recompress(struct zram *zram, u32 mode,
> +static void scan_slots_for_recompress(struct zram *zram, u32 mode,

> -	return 0;

Clean-up looks correct.

Reviewed-by: <reviewer>
